Book-1---Glass-Empires/Part-1---Rules/{-.title: { 1 } - Introduction
Book-1---Glass-Empires/Part-1---Rules/{-.content:
Power is the ability to destroy. To hold power over something, you must be able to destroy it.


An institution is tribal knowledge & great leaders.


Bureaucracies do not know how to be a bureaucracy. The bureaucrat knows how to be a bureaucracy. The bank does not know how to be a bank. The banker knows how to be a bank. All social structures are held together by the invisible web of social relationships; any structure described in aggregate is a legal fiction. Remove the human, and the network dies.


Democracy is a d√©tente; a failure to execute power. Oligarchs unable to conquer each other, buying time.


Finance is how humans compete to resolve debts. Economics is how to coordinate finance so that humans circularly resolve their debts.


The issuers of credit will always protect themselves.


All wars are bankers‚Äô wars, and are means of resolving debts.


Every man is a bank, in different forms, and to varying degrees of competency.

What This Game Is
Glass Empires is a role-playing game that simulates pre-industrial human power dynamics on an animistic discworld where there is no distinction between magic & nature.
While Glass Empires is mostly OSR-compatible & can be played as a traditional (if odd) D&D-like title, it is not a game about dungeon-crawling (not primarily, at least) & the gameplay loop bears little resemblance to the loops most typified by other RPGs. This is a game about relationships, debt, rulership, & the nature of the divine. Glass Empires is a game about factions & power. In a very real way, your faction is both synonymous with & more important than your character. It is a critical assumption that you are at the very least interested in a cult of personality (p. @) as a great hero ‚Äî & far more preferably, that you will rule an empire or die trying.
To this end, the game contains a large number of critical diversions from what we might call a ‚Äúbaseline‚Äù fantasy RPG, outlined here to make the point clearly & early.
Level is Number of Followers
Your Level is spiritual energy backed by the number of Families (p. @) who follow & depend upon you. That can be as economic dependence, heroic awe, or religious worship ‚Äî but your power is intimately associated with your ability to command & lead your fellow Man. Your ability to obtain unlimited costless personal power is highly curtailed relative to mainstream D&D or even Old School Renaissance games.
Furthermore, your Retainers (p. @) are critical to maintaining power. Because each Retainer can themselves lead a competent Subfaction as subordinate to a Faction you control, each loyal Retainer is an effective force multiplier on how much you can achieve ‚Ä¶ or a severe threat that can destabilize your realm.
Because your Level is based on your Families, you will lose your Level if you lose your Faction. Being ousted, overthrown, or annihilated in a battle or siege is a serious risk to your personal power. This is not as extreme as it may sound, because the only mechanical threat this has is that you lose your maximum Hit Points ‚Äî you keep your Class Features ‚Äî but it is something to remember. Your Level is literally your Followers.
Classes Are Not Combat-Only
Your Class (p. @) is your social class & determines your social standing as much or moreso than your combat power. Because the most important thing in Glass Empires is your ability to command humans, the most important thing that Classes do is enable new ways for you to command people & manipulate the politics of your realm. While there are still combat-focused Classes (& these indeed provide utility for a conqueror, adventurer, or warlord), just as viable to glory are roles focused on stewardship, political maneuvering, & economic subterfuge.
Furthermore, once the game starts, all Classes are functionally mix-&-match with multiclassing as the assumed default. Each Level you gain grants you one additional Class Feature for any that you qualify for, each of which is fully modular. A Jester can become a Lord, then take a level in Outlaw. Strictly speaking, you do not even have ‚ÄúClass Levels.‚Äù Your Level is derived from your Faction & stat boosts when they occur come from elsewhere; your Class Features are the entirety of your ‚ÄúClass.‚Äù
There is No Niche Protection
Classes can do the same thing as each other. The differentiation between characters (including PCs) in Glass Empires primarily comes from the factional & political level. Two warriors of identical powers can rule wildly different domains & derive their uniqueness from their differing strategic positions.
Furthermore, all characters, including NPCs & every monster, uses the same basic chassis & are subject to essentially the same rules. There are very few special functions that are unique to NPCs or PCs as a category. Animals, demons, ghosts & gods operate under the same framework as people. There are no ability scores to track (their role is filled by Facts, p. @).
Magic is Costly
All magic is a blood sacrifice to a god in exchange for a miracle. A fireball is never free. Magicians are Jester-priests & fringe freaks on the edge of society making deals to Totem-imprisoned spirits (p. @) they may have personally hunted down & killed to borrow their powers for but a moment. There is no such thing as safe, predictable, or stable magic.
This is a critical conceit of the setting & ruleset. Unconstrained abuse of blood magic results in divine Aztec state-managed sacrifices on crack ‚Äî power optimized to maximally extract value from human lives (or whatever else is valuable) as cattle. Indeed, even with this severe limitation in place, the demands of power are so great that this very pattern forms elsewhere within the world of Aia.
Everything Is Magic
There is no ‚Äúmagic.‚Äù Everything is ‚Äúmagic.‚Äù Your spells are not some bizarre set of rules bolted onto the universe-as-is. Magic is spirits acting on your behalf ‚Äî your spell to grow claws is a literal bear‚Äôs soul ripped out from its Flesh-elemental corpse-form & made into a supplicant. The earth is Stone elementals; there are only eight metals. The river is inhabited by a vast fractal kingdom of water elementals who care not to move from their dwelling. You could, in theory & with great practice, negotiate with a fire ‚Äî but it usually only wants what fire does, in the end. Belief, in some ways, is said to beget reality, & all manner of new horrors can be birthed from the fears of Men. People have a limited grasp on science, meteorology, reason, & the mind. Those on Aia live in a dark world surrounded by terrors they do not understand for which there is no clear explanation.
Eating Things to Gain Power
If you ritualistically kill, cook, & eat a creature, you can gain its power. This works on every creature, including humans. Everyone knows it exists. It is a key part of the setting. This is the source of beastmen ‚Äî men who ate beasts & grew to be like them. It is also the source of vast, systematized Flower War ritual violence whose sole purpose is to produce those beastmen for the benefit of godlike priest-kings, as well as the unending font of noble hunts for new, dangerous monsters to consume.
Ritual cannibalism is at once honored, taboo, & often mandatory for warriors. Man is often common prey for the other races of Aia ‚Äî & they know it, & fight to prevent it.
Gods Are Physical Things That Can Be Killed
To a peasant, a king is a god. In Glass Empires, the notion of divinity is an open question. The things we would call gods may be spirits, men, wild animals, powerful kings, or any number of other oddball entities. There are multiple Factions in the default setting whose primary mode of victory demands the slaughter of many gods. You can feasibly become a ‚Äúgod‚Äù by figuring out how to become ageless (p. @) & learning some magic (p. @) while hiring some spirits as guardians or angels (p. @). You could also just build a cult (p. @).
Relationships & Obligations Rather Than Discrete Economics
You will not be bean-counting. Glass Empires‚Äô power structures are principally driven by economics, but the vast majority of it is relational. Discrete quantities of coin (I have 2,000gp) is infinitely less significant to a ruler than revenues (‚ÄúI earn $2,000 per month‚Äù), which for the most part are simplified into game rules that implicitly prove your financial capabilities rather than requiring you explicitly mark up & down fluctuating balances like a petty bookkeeper.
This also plays into how dungeon-crawling is irrelevant to anyone playing for keeps ‚Äî the pile of gold you can drag out of a dungeon is insignificant next to the economic production of an entire demesne of citizens, which is not measured in gold but in labor.
And speaking of labor‚Ä¶
Amoral Bronze Age Brutality
Because Glass Empires is pre-industrial, it is pre-modern in its morality. Christ has not died for you; there is no forgiveness for an absent sacrifice. The gods demand your blood, sweat, & tears ‚Äî even if it kills you. The entirety of Glass Empires is about labor as the fundamental unit of power. Families are your resources. The game is about taking young men & marching them to die for personal rewards they may have never benefited from. Human power in this context consists of taking a civilization & treating every man & woman in it as an asset towards your conquest of Aia, the Grand Disc. Slavery is an integral component of every economic system on Glass; pre-industrial civilization struggles to exist without it, and the economic framework for alternative systems simply does not exist. Morality warps & bends, as with cannibalism & war, around the economic incentives of labor. Such is the nature of power.
Players are Not Necessarily Moral
If you want to be a hero, go ahead and try. It is not mechanically prohibited, but it is also not incentivized. Every Faction in Glass is pursuing power through some combination of violence, manipulation, & ideology. The default victory condition is conquest and/or subjugation of all other actors. There is no grand heroic quest & no moralizing. This is a Bronze Age Renaissance civil war.
A key theory behind Glass Empires is that morality is downstream from behavioral incentives between comparably powerful agents. This game will not state that you are not heroes ‚Äî but whether that is economically viable or not is an open question based on how you define heroism. In the end, the grandest of visionaries are also slaves to power. Heavy is the head that wears the crown, & the nature of the human condition may yet bring low the greatest of intentions.
For Beginners
@ Glass Empires‚Äô Game Loop
not having a preconception of how these games work ‚Ä¶ might HELP you here.
because most RPG game loops look like one of these two:

be broke ass niggas > go into dungeon because GM said so > fight monster, solve trap, get treasure > leave & buy slightly less broke ass gear > go back into dungeon
be broke ass > gm literally tell you what to do by speaking through an NPC > you go do the thing > you are still probably broke ass unless the GM awarded you fake points to spend on fake shit

sometimes you break it up & get GMs who try to build a world, but it‚Äôs still usually reactive to you as PCs
in this game, it is very different

you are a RICH ass > you have people, factional resources, and the ability to act > you are fighting ALL OF THE OTHER PLAYERS for control of Glass, and you can NEGOTIATE with them or KILL them and many have INCOMPATIBLE & MUTUALLY EXCLUSIVE GOALS > you can DO ANYTHING IN YOUR POWER to affect the outcome of the war > start killing people

there is no story. I have a faction engine. it makes events based on the NPC factions & based on what you guys do. I manage the turns as everyone submits orders
@ Dice Notation
Glass Empires only uses six-sided dice ‚Äî in mathematical terms, ‚Äúd6‚Äù dice, with d indicating die & the number indicating number of sides.

X-in-6: roll 1d6. if it‚Äôs X or less, the effect happens; otherwise, it doesn‚Äôt.

we also have an exception used exclusively for tables.

d66 = roll 2d6, but treat the first die as the tens digit & the second as the ones digit. produces 36 results from 11 to 66.

Book-1---Glass-Empires/Part-1---Rules/{-.title: { 10 } - Battles
Book-1---Glass-Empires/Part-1---Rules/{-.content:
@ Playmode: Battle
One of the two potential playmodes for resolving combat. Battles are intended for large-scale battles held between any number of individuals (but especially any battle above 12 combatants) where speed is more important than detail.
Battles take place as a series of Rounds. Each Round represents an entire segment of battle & terminates with the elimination (Defeat) of a number of combatants (Units). Rather than tracking HP & Damage on a per-character basis, each side‚Äôs combat ability is summarized using their Battle Rating & then tested via commanders‚Äô abilities combined with abstracted bonuses for terrain & other tactical considerations. There are comparatively few tactical decisions required, with the few remaining largely relegated to the choice to use single-use items (where applicable) & the decision of when to flee (if your side has taken too many Defeats for winning to remain viable, or if your tactical position is obviously insufficient to overcome the odds you face).
No grid, miniatures, or map is required.
Battle Rating
Battle Rating (BR) is a score representing everything that makes a character uniquely competent in a general combat scenario, not including exceptional cases. The formula for BR is as follows:

(Power √ó Armor Multiplier √ó Damage Multiplier √ó Helpful Features Multiplier) √∑ Harmful Features Multiplier = BR

Always round to the nearest 1 BR increment. The calculation for each variable is as follows:

Power: Equal to your Level + HD.

If you have a mount, add the mount‚Äôs Power.


Armor Multiplier: Since Armor is a multiplier of Power & we assume 1 Power = 4 HP, this is calculable based on the degree of Armor. Your Armor Multiplier is equal to 1 plus your highest Armor score divided by 4. For leather, this is 1.25√ó; chain, 1.5√ó; plate, 2√ó.

If mounted, use an averaged Armor score.


Damage Multiplier: Divide your best possible Damage output per Turn (accounting for all Strikes & other esoteric factors i.e. Reload weapons) by 4.

Ask your GM if you have other, weirder modifiers for a given weapon.
Mount attacks are only added if the mount is a sapient combatant.


Helpful Features Multiplier: Sum the number of Features you have that help your combat performance (& that aren‚Äôt already implicitly included in other multipliers) & add 1.

Add mount Features if applicable.


Harmful Features Multiplier: Sum the number of Features that harm your combat performance (& that aren‚Äôt already implicitly included in other multipliers) & add 1.

Add mount Features if applicable.





                  
                  Example
                  
                

A Lv2 human (HD 2) soldier with full plate, a shield & sword (medium melee, 5 Physical) & no outstandingly combat-useful Features (i.e. from his Class Features) has a Battle Rating of (4 √ó 2 √ó 1.25 √ó 1 √∑ 1) = 10.
An Army of 120 such soldiers would have a BR of 120 √ó 10 = 1,200.




                  
                  Example
                  
                

A Lv4 human (HD 2) with leather armor, a 2-handed longsword (large melee, 8 Physical), an extra Strike (for 2 total per Attack) & 2 other combat-applicable Features has a BR of (6 √ó 1.25 √ó 4 √ó 3) √∑ 1 = 90. He‚Äôs worth about 8 of the swordsmen in the previous example by himself.
If he instead had plate armor, he‚Äôd be worth (6 √ó 2 √ó 4 √ó 3) √∑ 1 = BR 144. Not bad!


Units
A Unit is a single character, or a Group (p. @) of statistically equivalent characters, representing a discrete unit of command on the battlefield.
Generally speaking, no side should have more than 20 Units. If a side has more than this, some of them should be consolidated into larger Units.
Round Duration
Rounds last a variable amount of in-game time depending on the number of participants. These are approximate numbers, & if the GM has a case for why a different period would elapse per Round for a given Battle, he may enforce it.

Less than 100: 1 minute per Round.
‚â•100: 10 minutes per Round.
‚â•1,000: 1 hour per Round.
‚â•10,000: 1 day per Round.
‚â•100,000: 1 month per Round.
‚â•1,000,000: 1 year per Round.



                  
                  Why does each Round take so long at small scales? 
                  
                

We have to account for the time required to clean up & double-check the battle space after a combat.




                  
                  Whole wars as single Battles 
                  
                

You can resolve an entire war as a single lengthy Battle, and might wish to do so if you just need to determine an outcome fast with low fidelity. The Bonuses may prove to be hard to adjudicate, but treat it as a Fermi Estimate & round appropriately. Make sure to account for alliances, trade, supply chains & logistics, resource availability, & the state of the realms‚Äô finances.


Procedure: Battle Round
Each Round of Battle is resolved by the following procedure done in order of the listed steps.
1. Declare Intent to Flee
If one side is not yet fully defeated but wishes to flee, it must be declared as the Round is starting. If one side is fleeing, the Round is a Pursuit, which is managed as another full Round of Battle.
2. Declare manual & one-time use effects
Characters may declare that they are temporarily boosting their own Battle Rating using any manual effects that isn‚Äôt accounted for by the default BR calculation method. Examples include one-time use items & Features that can only be activated once within a certain period of time.
Characters get:

+0.25 BR per point of damage or HP healed;
To multiply their BR by the number of nonlinear beneficial special Features triggered by the effect for this round only.

For multi-target & area effects, multiply damage by total number of reachable targets (at the GM‚Äôs discretion). If you don‚Äôt know the enemy formation ahead of time, area effects hit up to their widest diameter in feet divided by 5‚Äô in characters when targeting disorganized (loose, chaotic) formations, including chaotic Groups as well as masses of individual characters. Square this value if targeting a unit that‚Äôs a Group in organized formation or if the targets are known to be tightly clustered (i.e. from a chokepoint).
3. BR ratio
Sum the BR of all allied units together for every side. Determine which side has more BR and by what ratio. It gains:

+1 If it‚Äôs at least 10% stronger than the enemy, but by less than a 2:1 ratio.
+X if it‚Äôs X times stronger, ignoring the remainder.

4. Tactical bonuses
Add the following bonuses if their matching condition is true. If the Party‚Äôs enemy has the listed advantage, instead of adding it as a bonus, subtract it as a penalty.

¬±2 if you are ambushing or surprising the target (independent of positioning; only applicable on the 1st Round).
+1 if the enemy is fleeing & this Round is a Pursuit.
¬±1 for each disease, poison, or other broad debilitation the enemy suffers.

In addition, give ¬±1 to a side for each of the following questions that unquestionably resolves in their favor:

Terrain: Do you hold better, more defensible ground?
Perception: Is your commanders‚Äô vision of the enemy‚Äôs positioning & movements superior to their commanders‚Äô perception of yours?
Flanking: Are your units flanking the foe?
Maneuverability: Do your Units have superior speed, flight, swimming, tunneling, climbing, or other abilities that are not matched by a meaningful proportion of the enemy‚Äôs Units, or have you pinned the enemy in place (i.e. via encirclement)?
Area vs Formation: Does your side field area-of-effect weaponry against a tightly-formed & regimented enemy?
Morale: Are your Units defending their home and/or children?

Players, while arguing for tactical bonuses, may at this time may note any tactics or stratagems that depend upon the current state of the battlefield.
5. Making the Battle Save
The Battle Save is made by the PCs‚Äô side‚Äôs commander. (If PCs are on all sides, flip a coin for who rolls.) The TN is 7 plus the enemy‚Äôs Mod. Compare the Result:









































ResultBattle Save EffectsDefeat %(You)Defeat %(Enemy)Crit‚â•12The enemy suffers a Morale Break.1-in-63-in-6Pass8‚Äì11Your side gains a Bonus Shift.1-in-62-in-6Seven7The violence continues.1-in-61-in-6Failure3‚Äì6The enemy gains a Bonus Shift.2-in-61-in-6Fumble‚â§2Your side suffers a Morale Break.3-in-61-in-6
Each side‚Äôs commander rolls the listed X-in-6 chance of Defeat for each NPC Unit on their side. Players may roll their PC‚Äôs own dice to determine if they were Defeated in the skirmish.
Units that are totally Immune to all forms of attack that the enemy is bringing to bear in a fight cannot be Defeated.
Bonus Shifts
When a side receives a Bonus Shift, the GM describes how this Round‚Äôs victor‚Äôs position improves, or how the losers‚Äô position worsens, represented by a gain of a +1 bonus for the victor or the loss of a +1 bonus for the loser. If the commanders stated a tactical goal at the opening of this Round, the GM may grant that achievement as the bonus. Otherwise, the GM rolls 1d6 to determine characteristic gained/lost:

Terrain/footing
Range/freedom of movement
Energy/exhaustion
Confusion/perception
Command/control
Confidence/morale

Morale Break
If the losing side‚Äôs NPCs are capable of fleeing (physically & mentally), they will flee during the next Round, making the final round a Pursuit. If they cannot flee & can surrender, they will attempt to surrender. If they cannot surrender, they will remain on the battlefield & become immune to Morale Breaks until killed or victorious.
If only some NPCs can flee, the ones who do have a 3-in-6 chance of Defeat, & are otherwise removed from the battlefield as escaped & undefeated.
6. End of Round
At the end of a Round, if no side is yet fully defeated or currently fleeing, start a new Round.
End of Battle
When all members of one or both sides are defeated, or if a Round has ended where one side was fleeing, the battle is over. Fleeing sides count as defeated.
Defeated Side

Defeated named characters: Make a Mortality Save for each. If alive, captured by the enemy.
Defeated unnamed characters: 50% die or desert forever; the other 50% are wounded & captured. When rounding, round towards the lighter consequence.
Undefeated units: Escape without consequences.

Victorious Side

Defeated named characters: Make a Mortality Save for each, with a +2 bonus.
Defeated unnamed characters: 25% die or desert forever; 25% are wounded; 50% are fine. When rounding, round towards the lighter consequence.
Undefeated units: Escape without consequences.

Battle Mortality Save Table





























ResultBattle Mortality Save EffectsCrit‚â•12You are alive & intact, with only minor wounds. You fully escape capture regardless of other rules.Pass8‚Äì11Reduced to 0 HP. If character, roll for a Wound; if group, all assumed wounded.Seven7Reduced to 0 HP. If character, roll for a Wound; if group, all assumed wounded. You are captured by the enemy, regardless of other rules.Failure3‚Äì6You are dead.Fumble‚â§2You died in a particularly gruesome manner.

Book-1---Glass-Empires/Part-1---Rules/{-.filePath: Book 1 - Glass Empires/Part 1 - Rules/{ 2 } - Core Terms.md
Book-1---Glass-Empires/Part-1---Rules/{-.title: { 2 } - Core Terms
Book-1---Glass-Empires/Part-1---Rules/{-.content:
@ Moves
A Move is any action taken in-game by an actor. Moves can be long or short-term actions; they may involve a chance of failure (& if they do, probably invoke a Save). They may involve movement, thinking, or long-term labor.
Moves may be instant or extended.

An instant Move takes just a few seconds (& is called an Action).
An extended Move has a Deadline set some period into the future, often days or months out; until that Deadline is reached, the character is dedicating all their time to continuing the Move & isn‚Äôt able to perform other Moves.

Characters who lead Factions may oversee their Faction performing a Move (p. @) instead of taking Moves personally.


                  
                  @ What if we don't have Moves for what we want to do? 
                  
                

You can think up & execute on actions for which there are no Moves. This is okay; it‚Äôs the GM‚Äôs job to determine reasonable outcomes in the absence of Moves.
If it turns out to be something that keeps coming up, the GM can also construct new Moves as necessary to meet the needs of the direction that play goes in.


Actions
Actions (p. @) are a special kind of Move lasting only a couple of seconds. They‚Äôre mostly used within the context of Skirmish combats, but they can also be used outside of this to resolve similar actions in other contexts.
If a character ever has a ‚Äúsplit second‚Äù to react to something, they have enough time to perform a single Minor Action.
Actions are further split into the Full-Turn, Major Action, Minor Action, and Free Action categories.
Facts
A Fact is a true statement about a character that grants permission or denies the possibility in the fiction to attempt related actions. Related Facts may also be invoked by any Player or the GM on Saves they‚Äôre relevant to add a +1 bonus or ‚àí1 penalty to an actor‚Äôs efforts.
Saves
A Save is a standardized 2d6 dice roll made to determine the effect of actions whose outcome is both uncertain & meaningful.
When figuring if a Save is required, consider if any applicable Facts make the task trivial or impossible. If so, the Save may be either unnecessary or pointless.
Procedure: Performing a Save

The Party and/or the GM adds every distinct numeric bonus & penalty that applies to the Save together. The result is called the Modifier (or Mod for short).

By default & unless explicitly stated otherwise, this includes a ¬±1 bonus/penalty for each distinct Fact that would serve as a bonus (+1) or a penalty (‚àí1) to the action the Save represents.


The specified participant making the Save (usually a Player) rolls 2d6 to get the Roll. A Roll of 12 (boxcars) on the dice is always a Crit; a Roll of 2 (snake-eyes) is always a Fumble. If neither is scored, add the Mod to the Roll & then compare to the adjacent table to get the Result.

The general results for a Save are displayed on the adjacent table. If a GM finds the need for an ad-hoc Save, this table can be used to determine how the results should be shaped.





























ResultAd-Hoc Save EffectCrit‚â•12Perfect/dramatic triumphPass8‚Äì11SuccessSeven7Mixed outcome with side-effectsFailure3‚Äì6Failure and/or consequencesFumble‚â§2Severe or catastrophic failure
Most of the time, when the game rules call for a Save, a table of effects unique to that mechanic will also be provided.
Binary Saves
Sometimes, a mechanic will call for a Save as a simple yes/no check. If this occurs, a Crit, Pass, or Seven is treated as a success, while a Failure or Fumble is a no. The GM may treat a Crit as twice as good a result or a Fumble as twice as bad if the situation allows for it.


                  
                  Example
                  
                

You may be asked to Save vs. falling off your horse, or Save vs. Panic.


@ Value, Credit & Debt
Silver Pieces & Value
A silver piece ($1) represents 1 day of unskilled labor & is the smallest denomination of coin in circulation. Most things are paid for with debt (p. @), typically via the gift economy. An asset‚Äôs price in $ is said to be its Value.


                  
                  The Gift Economy 
                  
                

You are gifted what you need today, expecting that tomorrow you will return the favor. Not doing so is dishonorable. Coincidentally, travelers & transients are dishonorable because you cannot ever hold them to a promise the way you & your village can hold your neighbor to account. If you need to track a debt explicitly, use the Debt system (p. @).




                  
                  Converting from other systems 
                  
                
The best way to convert prices from other game systems‚Äô price lists is to find the daily labor-value & convert based on that ratio. Be warned that many game designers have little grasp on finance or economics & that their assumptions may not hold steady across systems or settings.

@ rewrite this to be more clear for this section
@ Characters & Actors
Taxonomy

Actor: An entity capable of taking action.

Character: A featherless biped (or otherwise independent entity).

PC: A character controlled by a Player.
NPC: A character controlled by the GM.
Leader: A character who controls & represents a Faction.
Retainer: A character who is suborned to another for some reason (hired, sworn servants, friends, slaves).
Liege: A Retainer‚Äôs master.


Faction: A statistical grouping of many characters taking collective action.

Subfaction: A Faction whose Leader is the Retainer of another Leader.





@ Dice Notation
Glass Empires exclusively uses six-sided (d6) square dice.
@ Until we publish, there won‚Äôt be a section on this, but we need a cursory overview for the newbies to reference.
Campaign
A campaign consists of a calendar (p. @) & a map depicting the world or a region of the world in which the game takes place.
@ Calendar
A calendar is required in order to keep track of Deadlines for Moves (p. @) & for synchronizing Players with the current state of the world.
Ideally, a private/public calendar split should be set up so that the current date is accessible not just by you but also by the Players & so that they can take their own notes.
Aia‚Äôs Calendar
This is the calendar in use on Glass.

Every year has 360 days, divided evenly into 12 months.
Every month has 30 days, separated into 4 weeks.
Each week has 7 days, but the 2nd & 4th week of each month ends in the Full Moon Day (15th) and New Moon Day (30th) respectively, neither of which are formally part of the week.
The current year is 1002.
























































MonTueWedThuFriSunSatSpecial1234567‚Äî89101112131415 (Full Moon)16171819202122‚Äî2324252627282930 (New Moon)



































Season1st Month2nd Month3rd MonthSpringJanuaryFebruaryMarchSummerAprilMayJuneAutumnJulyAugustSeptemberWinterOctoberNovemberDecember

Map
The map is the area in which play happens.
Any scale of map is acceptable so long as you have a scale measured in miles. Glass Empires requires no strict hex grid or other elements; measurements, where necessary, are made with a marked straightedge such as a ruler. It is recommended that your map covers a land area at least rivaling that of Hokkaid≈ç, Japan (~50,000 square miles / ~225√ó225 miles). You may find a 5-mile square or hex grid useful for quickly estimating distances.
Glass Empires mechanically assumes you can mark or identify the following kinds of features:

Locations including cities, fortresses, critical strategic production facilities, dungeons, or other notable spots;
Roads as lines, ideally including a distinction between paved & unpaved roads.
Terrain types, sufficiently distinct enough for the GM(s) to make rulings regarding what areas of the map qualify for which travel costs (p. @).

We also assume you can persistently track the location of various mobile parties, such as armies.
Location
A Location is a point on the map representing some kind of feature.
Exceptionally large Locations or exceptionally small campaign maps may require marking of areas or biomes instead of points.
Settlement
A Settlement is Location permanently inhabited by one or more creatures operating a society. At least 1 Family must live at a Location for it to be considered a Settlement.
Resource
A Resource is a Location that, when settled, produces some form of trade goods that can be transported & benefited from elsewhere. They represent production of strategic signifiance (such that actors may want to attack their production bases, trade access, or secure them with additional forces).
Structural HP
Structural Hit Points (SHP) is a measure of fortification troop capacity (how many soldiers you can fit inside a Location) combined with durability (how resilient it is to assault). Every Location can have an SHP score.
Structures cost $100 per SHP. Most are a mixture of wood & stone.
Structural Hardness
Structures divide all Damage delivered via weapons of inferior hardness by 10. Approximately, hardness in ascending order:

Wood & bone & copper
Stone & keratin & bronze
Iron & steel
Gemstones

Siege machines count as the toughest material used in their construction.
Structural Unit Capacity
Structures are assumed to be able to operationally hold 1 soldier per 1 SHP. This is not accurate for small volume-focused civilian buildings (i.e. warehouses, which could manage up to 10 soldiers per SHP) or for heavily-fortified defensive structures (i.e. walls, sanctums) but is approximately correct for castles & Settlement fortifications taken as a whole.

Book-1---Glass-Empires/Part-1---Rules/{-.title: { 3 } - Character Creation
Book-1---Glass-Empires/Part-1---Rules/{-.content:
This procedure creates a Player Character embedded in Glass‚Äôs factional landscape. New characters begin as leaders of small Factions with a Follower or two, some resources, and the scars of their past.

@ We have to explain how Levels work first. check Faction section for the table on that

Procedure Overview

Choose Class - Select your social position (as a Class). Simultaneously, you may:

Choose a sex;
Determine your starting Class Feature;
Generate & create two Facts derived from your Class‚Äôs table of backgrounds.


Choose Race - Choose a Race from those within your Class & copy its statblock & Racial Features onto your sheet.
Determine Distinction - Add a bit of personal color to your character.
Roll Follower - Determine your starting Faction

Optional: Re-roll Feature - You may drop your second Class Feature & re-roll it on your Follower‚Äôs Class Features table.


Group Entanglement - Determine why the Party works together & optionally set your PC as a Follower of another PC

1) Choose Class
Your Class is your literal social position in Glass. It determines what you can become and which pools of power you can draw from.
Your Rank in a class is your level of achievement in it. You start with 1 Rank in any Class & may gain Ranks by increasing your commanded Factions‚Äô Level (p. @) via new followers & new conquests.

Choose one Class from the list below to gain a Rank in, or roll randomly. Your choice determines which Races you can play, which Feature pool you draw from when gaining power, & your starting social position.
Determine your Class Feature according to that Class‚Äôs means of doing so. Write it down.
Roll for an Origin on your Class‚Äôs table of Origins. Write the whole thing down.

Tables of Classes

































1d6Class1Lord2Magistrate3Magician4Burgher5Mercenary6Gangster
Lord
Races: Human Glassians only (with rare Salamander exceptions)
Sex: Male only (occasional exceptions made for reasons of inheritance)
You have a porcelain mask. It covers half your face. It is traced with ruby & jade & sweeping gilded lines. You were given one at birth & have worn one since that day.
You have a duty. You are the miniature Kain of your domain. You are a poet, a judge, & a general. You are a murderer. You were born to rule.
And just might have, if the world hadn‚Äôt fallen to pieces.
If you start as part of this class, you are part of a Governing House, descended from the Second Phoenix‚Äôs children through at least one tenuous familial link. The three main houses are Eagle, Beacon, and Arcades, but you may always invent a smaller Cadet House. There are many children of Kain.
Class Features
Roll once on the table per Rank obtained. If you roll a Feature you already have, you may choose a Feature from above or below it on the list that you don‚Äôt have yet.













































2d6Class Feature2‚Äì3Killer Instinct: If you can observe someone move or speak for at least 10 seconds, you can tell if they‚Äôve killed nobody, somebody, or many people, & may have the GM tell you their most immediate, deepest intentions as expressed by a single word.4Command: You may issue single-sentence commands to any non-Lord Glassian of lower Level & they will obey (possibly after hesitation) as if you were their unquestioned superior. If their Level is equal or greater than yours or your order contradicts a superior‚Äôs orders, you must make a Persuasion Save (p. @) to influence them, adding your Level to the Save & subtracting their Level (or their superior‚Äôs Level, if it‚Äôs greater) as a modifier.5Defeat Means Respect: If you defeat a warrior in battle & afterwards treat their wounds or injuries, you may compel them (with the same rules as the Command Class Feature) to join you as Retainers or as Followers of a Faction. Units are affected as a group, or by influencing their Leader if one is present.6Cleaver: Your Strikes in an Attack can target any number of valid characters & if a target dies, you may retarget unspent Strikes to new valid targets within reach ‚Äî and you may make a new Angry Step before each Strike if doing so would pull an otherwise out-of-reach character into your reach.7Murderer: Gain an extra Strike per Attack.8Courtly Influence: You can automatically obtain invites to balls & other public, semi-public, or closed events through fame, guile, or familiarity. Other Lords are obligated to grant you a court appearance if they aren‚Äôt Hostile to you.9Blade Wind: You may take [Power] Damage to instantaneously move to any spot that can contain you within the greatest of your Speed scores, faster than anything can track or react to, so long as a taut rope could tie your current location to it. This Feature can interrupt any Move & might negate that Move thereby.10Master of the Hunt: Your hand-held ranged weapons have no maximum range, provided you can see your target. You never take range penalties.11‚Äì12Strength Training: Choose one Type of Damage. You are Resistant to it.
Class Backgrounds
If this is your first Class at character creation, roll twice. For each, write a Fact that describe a true lesson of your choice you learned from the experience: ‚ÄúBecause I [was (a)‚Ä¶], I learned [lesson].‚Äù You can play with the phrasing so long as the background & lesson are both included.

















































































d66I was (a)‚Ä¶11‚àí12Favored heir, pampered at every turn & envied by my relatives13‚àí14Ignored extra, left to scrounge wisdom from idle courtiers15‚àí16Student of (1d6: 1‚Äì2 history / 3‚Äì4 language 5‚Äì6: medicine)21‚àí22Unwanted problem who survived everything meant to have killed me23‚àí24Secretly responsible for the disappearance of my parents25‚àí26Incompetent tyrant31‚àí32Hobbyist (1d6: 1‚Äì2 gardener / 3‚Äì4 astronomer / 5‚Äì6 drug alchemist)33‚àí34Wanton (1d6: 1‚Äì2 drunkard / 3‚Äì4 casanova / 5‚Äì6 addict; say how)35‚àí36Scribe & expert calligraphist41‚àí42Catastrophically failed (1d6: 1‚Äì2 poet / 3‚Äì4 banker / 5‚Äì6 businessman)43‚àí44Never fond of the harpies my father kept as servants45‚àí46Ruthless duelist51‚àí52Scheming courtier in my Liege‚Äôs court53‚àí54Driven from home by a Commons‚Äô Uprising55‚àí56Always hosting guests who were taking advantage of hospitality customs61‚àí62Known for firing windgun warning shots at annoying petitioners in court63‚àí64Spendthrifty administrator65‚àí66Struggling (1d6: 1‚Äì2 mercenary / 3‚Äì4 revolutionary / 5‚Äì6 commander)
Magistrate

Races: Mostly human, plus a handful of salamanders who graduated from servitude
Sex: Male only (occasional exceptions made for reasons of inheritance)

You have a seal. It is carved from jade, set in bronze, & it bears your name and office in the old Imperial script. Without it, you are nothing. With it, you are the law.
You are the machinery of Glass. You count the people, collect the taxes, adjudicate the disputes, and maintain the records that make civilization possible. The Lords rule ‚Äî you merely make ruling work. Promoted from the Burghers, theoretically at the pleasure of whatever Lord holds your district, in practice you have outlasted every Lord you‚Äôve served.
If you start as part of this class, you hold (or recently held) an Imperial appointment ‚Äî however minor ‚Äî & are recognized as a member of Glass‚Äôs governing class. You own your seal.
Class Features
Roll once on the table per Rank obtained. If you roll a Feature you already have, you may choose a Feature from above or below it on the list that you don‚Äôt have yet.













































2d6Class Feature2‚Äì3Orator: Anyone willing to listen to you for a minute (a Persuasion Save if in doubt, p. @) can be made to listen to you for up to eight hours.4Audit: Given a day‚Äôs work and access to a Settlement or Faction‚Äôs affairs (through employment, legal authority, or physical investigation), you may ask the GM to compare that Faction‚Äôs public claims (alliances, wealth, territory, number of Followers) against their actual state. The GM tells you each discrepancy as a single factual ‚Äúworse than/basically accurate/better than‚Äù statement.5Administrative Oversight: When a Retainer or Subfaction member takes action that contradicts their stated role, standing orders, or assigned duties, you know it happened. You know who deviated & when. You don‚Äôt learn why, or on whose behalf. This does not detect people who follow their orders to the letter while working against your interests.6Bureaucrat: You cannot be made to admit to having made any claim or statement, especially if it would violate dogma or authority. You may automatically know the immediate intent, hidden or otherwise, of any bureaucrat or fellow Magistrate you talk to.7Legal Precedent: When you need to establish a legal claim, dispute someone‚Äôs authority, or uncover recorded history, if written records could reasonably exist, you find them automatically. The GM must tell you what they reveal, even if they hadn‚Äôt prepared such documentation beforehand.8Coded Language: You can write poems with encoded messages hidden in the subtext that only either a specific target you know well (as a friend or acquaintance) or another person with this power can interpret the true meaning of.9Document Forgery: You can instantaneously recognize forgeries of official Glassian documents, seals, contracts, or titles & with a day‚Äôs work & calligraphic tools create your own that nobody other than another Magistrate will recognize as a forgery.10Courtly Influence: You can automatically obtain invites to balls & other public, semi-public, or closed events through fame, guile, or familiarity. Lords are obligated to grant you a court appearance if they aren‚Äôt Hostile to you.11‚Äì12Delegitimize: With a day‚Äôs work & enough data to make a coherent (not necessarily true) argument, you can construct a formal case against a specific claim made by an individual or Faction; a bloodline, a title, a treaty, a property right, or a debt. This produces a document that exists as a game object. When you produce it, the GM tells you which of the Factions known to you would be most willing to act on it. Anyone who possesses the case may use it as a valid attack vector for a Sabotage Move against the target with a +2 bonus. You may freely distribute copies. A case built on genuine evidence (from Audit, Legal Precedent, or firsthand knowledge) is airtight; a case built on fabricated evidence (i.e. via Document Forgery) holds until a Magistrate examines it.
Class Backgrounds
If this is your first Class at character creation, roll twice. For each, write a Fact that describe a true lesson of your choice you learned from the experience: ‚ÄúBecause I [was (a)‚Ä¶], I learned [lesson].‚Äù You can play with the phrasing so long as the background & lesson are both included.

















































































d66I was (a)‚Ä¶11‚àí12Tutor to a Lord‚Äôs children who noticed too much13‚àí14Tax collector in a district that hated me15‚àí16Student of (1d6: 1‚Äì2 law / 3‚Äì4 natural philosophy / 5‚Äì6 calligraphy)21‚àí22Appointed to a post nobody else wanted in a dying village23‚àí24Son of a Magistrate who inherited the position & all its enemies25‚àí26Reject who passed the Imperial examinations against all expectations31‚àí32Census-taker who mapped a district so thoroughly the Lord grew afraid of me33‚àí34Clerk who discovered my superior was (1d6: 1‚Äì3 embezzling / 4‚Äì6 a spy)35‚àí36Keeper of a provincial archive nobody else could read41‚àí42Prosecuted a case I knew was unjust43‚àí44Hobbyist (1d6: 1‚Äì2 poet / 3‚Äì4 astronomer / 5‚Äì6 natural historian)45‚àí46Idealist who tried to reform the tax code & was reassigned to a swamp51‚àí52Disgraced by a scandal (1d6: 1‚Äì2 real / 3‚Äì4 fabricated / 5‚Äì6 someone else‚Äôs)53‚àí54Manager of an alchemist‚Äôs workshop55‚àí56Ghostwriter for a Lord who couldn‚Äôt compose his own correspondence61‚àí62Adjudicator of water rights in a region where everyone was armed63‚àí64Spy for a Lord who needed someone literate to read intercepted letters65‚àí66Exiled from my post by a Lord who wanted someone more compliant
Performance: Given a stage to perform on, you may attempt a mass Persuasion Save (p. @) against an entire audience. A performance can only be used to convey a general lesson or teaching, not specifics to a present case, but you may attempt a Save for performance quality to qualify you for Persuasion‚Äôs incentive requirement (‚ÄúDoes the target like you?‚Äù).
Magical Wisdom: You have mastered the art of being implicitly treated as an authority on almost any topic relating to the supernatural, spiritual, & divine. Your warnings & instructions are taken with paranoid, superstitious credence, provided consistency (p. @) is met. Only other magicians are unaffected by this.
Teach By Experience: Anyone you‚Äôve been traveling with for more than a week can, while traveling with you, borrow any Facts of yours that describe lessons or experiences (not skills) that are unambiguously describable as wisdom for use on their own Saves.
Magician

Races: Varies by Tradition.
Sex: Varies by Tradition.

Magicians are unstable. Magic is horrifically dangerous. Few magicians live without suffering mutations, personal injury, and an eventual horrifying death. Sorcerer-kings are feared. Tame magicians are kept on tight leashes. Their prowess is unquestionable, but their training makes it difficult for them to rule men. And yet, the most powerful wizards of all have done just that ‚Äî commanding through force of sorcerous magic or institutional benefice as Fisher Kings who shape their realms as surely as it shapes them.
A spell is a miniature spirit, sprite, demon, devil, angel, elemental, or outsider presence whose body can serve as a lens for power. Magicians are spirit-speakers & god-keepers who tend the miniature shrines (the Totems, p. @) in exchange for power provided through magic (p. @).
Magicians are not a straightforward Class to play. Unlike lords, magistrates, or gangsters, all of whom have direct & intrinsic paths to the institutional power Glass Empires revolves around, magicians rely on specialized magical abilities that carry enormous risks. They have less in the way of institutional ability & therefore most ‚Äúsorcerer-kings‚Äù rely on unique combinations of talent & discovered magic to power their conquests. Because of this, they may lack a straightforward route to power. Most magicians are the right-hand man of conquerors, not the conqueror themselves. That doesn‚Äôt mean it‚Äôs impossible; rather, it means finesse & intelligence is required to succeed.
Magical Traditions
Every magician has a Magical Tradition from which their specialization descends. Your Tradition is your philosophy & history that shapes the way you see the world & the sort of spirits you can interact with; it also shapes the spirits you are most likely to encounter.
Each Magical Tradition defines the following:

A single Tradition Feature, a special Class Feature that‚Äôs always obtained the first time you gain a rank in the Magician Class, that comes with both benefits & drawbacks reflecting the way that tradition of Magicians see & interact with the world.
A list of 12 backgrounds unique to that Tradition.
A list of 12 spells, the most frequent spirits encountered & captured by that Tradition‚Äôs practitioners.

Magicians are not limited to capturing (& casting) spells from their Tradition. Per the rules on spellcasting (p. @), any magician can cast spells from any Tradition or even those that have no native Tradition, provided that they have a Fact or Feature that relates to that spell. That said, the spells in a Tradition‚Äôs list are the ones they‚Äôre most likely to attract as they grow in power.
Class Features
The first time you take a Rank in Magician, pick a Magical Tradition. It‚Äôs yours now. You can‚Äôt change it later. You gain its Tradition Feature, a special Class Feature.
Every time you take a Magician Rank, gain +1 Spell Die. You also attract an additional random spell from your Tradition‚Äôs spell list as soon as you make or otherwise obtain a Totem capable of holding it.
Every even-numbered Magician Rank, roll once on the adjacent table of Class Features & gain that Feature.

































1d6Magician Class Feature1Overcharging: Every time you cast a spell, you can declare you‚Äôre overcharging the spell. When you do so, take 1d6 Damage, boost your [Sum] by the amount of Damage taken & increase your [Dice] by 1.2Addiction: Whenever you suffer a Mishap, restore one of your exhausted SD.3Magician‚Äôs Eyes: In bright light, you can see invisible things (including spirits) as a faint lensing outline. Through direct eye contact, you can tell if someone is possessed, charmed, or soulless. Enchanted items (p. @) glow with a faint rainbow hue.4Identify Spirit: Given 1 hour of study, you can identify if something is enchanted or possessed by a spirit/spell & the approximate nature & intent of that spirit/spell. This accounts for all permanent & temporary effects inflicted by casting (p. @), including curses & more mundane effects like long-lasting poisons.5Cautious Casting: Once per day, when you cast a spell but before calculating spell effects, you may willingly reduce any single Spell Die‚Äôs rolled result to 1.6Spell Possession: You can treat your head as being a Totem capable of housing 2 spirits. Internally-held spirits are always available for casting & can only be unwillingly extracted from your body if you are killed. You count as possessed if you have any spirits inside you.
Class Backgrounds
If this is your first Class at character creation, your backgrounds are determined by the list in your Magical Tradition (p. @). Roll twice. For each, write a Fact that describe a true lesson of your choice you learned from the experience: ‚ÄúBecause I [was (a)‚Ä¶], I learned [lesson].‚Äù You can play with the phrasing so long as the background & lesson are both included.
Burgher

Races: Any.
Sex: Both.

What is a sword? Paid for by debt, a creator of debt ‚Äî for every man slain by it is a debt in blood, owed to his wife, his children & his clan. And a debt is not easily forgotten; men will gain vast riches from prosecuting the interests of others, for those now creditors in blood will find themselves paying handsome sums for revenge.
The Burgher is the shrewdest of their eponymous social class. Towns live & die by commerce & it is the industry & financial avenues built from chains of people that the Burgher navigates. No Lord can live without his blacksmith & to the right man, that is enormous leverage. There are Magistrates whose whims are slaves to the guildsmens‚Äô contracts they sign. A king cannot retaliate against a merchant whose success he too depends upon. Therein is a kind of liberty. He who commands the financial horizons of his foes will come to rule them, for it is within the boundaries of commerce that men make their decisions.
Class Features
Roll once on the table per Rank obtained. If you roll a Feature you already have, you may choose a Feature from above or below it on the list that you don‚Äôt have yet.













































2d6Class Feature2‚Äì3Hostile Acquisition: You can pay *full value* for other peoples' financial & commercial Debts (p. @) & seize them regardless of whether the original creditor of the Debt wants to give it up. An institution *must* be involved at some point; this doesn't work on personal Debts (or those not rated in). You can immediately & legally treat the Debt as owed to you.4Mobbing: You can perform mass Persuasion efforts (p. @) on crowds whose size does not exceed ten times your largest Faction‚Äôs Capital Population. This can only be used to instill generalized emotions or ideas (describable in 3 or fewer words), but you are the Leader of any subsequently created ‚ÄúFaction‚Äù. The mob disintegrates within 10 minutes of your being preoccupied with anything that is not leading it.5Commercial Gravity: If you or your Faction is destroyed or removed from a Settlement where you‚Äôve operated for at least a month, everyone who was thoroughly dependent on your services (i.e. if there is no named competitor to what you/your Faction did) automatically suffers the effects of Sabotage (p. @) as rolled by you against them (even if posthumously). If the target was not unquestionably dependent, you have a ‚àí2 penalty on the Sabotage Save. You cannot choose to not inflict this damage. Everyone knows it will happen.6Trade Network Whispers: If you want to know anything substantial (defeats, leadership changes, debt crises, shifts in priority) that occur to any Faction whose Capital is part of your physical trade routes (i.e. city governments) or supply chains (i.e. material suppliers), the GM must supply you a truthful answer.7Good Credit: Your word is money. You may make purchases, hire services, offer gifts, & issue loans totaling up to your Faction‚Äôs full monthly income ($150/Family) without paying upfront; recipients accept your promise as payment & you owe them the balance as a Debt. If you cannot cover any single outstanding note when its creditor calls it in, all of your creditors learn of the default simultaneously & you permanently lose both this Feature & 1 Rank in Burgher.8Carouser: If you can convince someone to spend a night committing a crime (however minor) or indulging in their favorite vice with you, they automatically become Friendly towards you. (Chaining a variety of crime & vice proposals is still a severe social offense.)9Honor Gifts: When you offer someone a gift, regardless of whether they accept it or not, there is an implicit understanding that they owe you for it & they gain a Debt described as an ‚ÄúObligation‚Äù with a Value equal to that of the gift‚Äôs Value or other identity. If the gift is refused, you must immediately choose between calling the Debt in (as an attack on their honor & character) or forgiving it.10Art of Subtlety: You cannot incriminate yourself for bribery while making bribes or while commanding a Faction to a Move with that intent; your words or actions are always too slippery. You may also treat this as a +1 bonus to attempts to do so.11‚Äì12Guild Monopoly: If your Faction is the only active Faction providing a specific service (wheat, pottery, smithing) within a Settlement, you may declare yourself a monopoly in that service. No other Faction can develop capabilities in it & no sufficient legal side-channels can be found to fulfill those interests. This overlaps with Commercial Gravity; anyone who requires what you offer is automatically thoroughly dependent.
Class Backgrounds

















































































d66I was (a)‚Ä¶11‚Äì12Guild apprentice who (1d6: 1‚Äì2 outgrew my master / 3‚Äì4 stole his clients / 5‚Äì6 married his daughter)13‚Äì14Raised behind dad‚Äôs teahouse counter, eavesdropping on everyone‚Äôs business15‚Äì16Caravan guard21‚Äì22Salamander middleman, one of very few humans the miners would deal with23‚Äì24Records- & debt-keeper for a village too poor for a Magistrate25‚Äì26Silk trader who lost everything to (1d6: 1‚Äì3 bandits / 4‚Äì6 an angry Lord)31‚Äì32Second child of a prosperous family with nothing to inherit & everything to prove33‚Äì34Market-day festival (& protection racket) organizer35‚Äì36Brewery heir, popular in every tavern for miles41‚Äì42Snooping, eavesdropping harpy-mail clerk43‚Äì44Child of a former (& executed) monopolist45‚Äì46Failed (1d6: 1‚Äì2 Magistrate / 3‚Äì4 Jester / 5‚Äì6 steward) who fell back on trade51‚Äì52Moneylender‚Äôs muscle enforcer & debt-collector53‚Äì54Survivor of a knives-out guild rivalry war55‚Äì56Innumerate lord‚Äôs bookkeeper61‚Äì62Raised in a tavern, became its owner & then lost it all63‚Äì64Impoverished (1d6: 1‚Äì2 ceramics / 3‚Äì4 jade / 5‚Äì6 metals) trader65‚Äì66Raised by a foreign merchant‚Äôs household as a translator & local liason
Warlord

Races: Any; mostly human & coraler.
Sex: Mostly male, with rare exceptions.

‚ÄúMercenary‚Äù is such a dishonorable word. A good mercenary keeps his word. A contract is your life-line; clients won‚Äôt contract you if you don‚Äôt pay heed to past promises.
Adventurer, fighter, conqueror. You are the disruption. You are the king-slayer & the contractor. Your men follow out of greed, fear, ideological absolutism. Wherever there is strife in the world, you emerge, blades drawn for profit & glory. And goodness, does Glass ever have strife!
Now is the time of the warlords. Those Lords who cling to their old stories & tales of law wield them like weapons, but their foundation is debased. Kain is gone. Their gods turn on them. In times of madness, prophets spread like wildfire ‚Äî & what is a warlord, if not a prophet with a windgun? So sell your cloak, & buy a sword; there is no more room for weak men on Glass.
Class Features
Roll once on the table per Rank obtained. If you roll a Feature you already have, you may choose a Feature from above or below it on the list that you don‚Äôt have yet.













































2d6Class Feature2‚Äì3Blitz & Siege: Your army‚Äôs BR is doubled when calculating efficiency & speed of a siege. Any army you lead moves 50% faster on roads.4Join For Riches: After defeating an enemy, you may have any defeated, displaced, or unemployed soldiers of Level less than yours join you for half the normal wage rate ($5/BR/month). They‚Äôll serve for as long as you can pay them.5Reckless Assault: You may make an extra Strike per Attack if you allow your enemy to before the Attack make a single Strike against you as a Reaction. If the enemy has already used its Reaction, there is no cost to this Feature.6Murderer: Gain an extra Strike per Attack.7Read the Field: Before committing to a Battle that you can see occurring within the next hour, you may demand the GM reveal the enemy‚Äôs total BR & if there is or isn‚Äôt any hidden element.8Contractual Oath: When you (or any forces you command) enter a fight where victory fulfills contractual obligations to an external party or to a greater shared cause, you always have the morale advantage (in Battles, p. @) & a +2 bonus on all Saves to avoid panic or flight (in Skirmishes, p. @). If your employer breaches the terms of a contract, you also gain this bonus while working to coerce, loot, or destroy them.9Cleaver: Your Strikes in an Attack can target any number of valid characters & if a target dies, you may retarget unspent Strikes to new valid targets within reach ‚Äî and you may make a new Angry Step before each Strike if doing so would pull an otherwise out-of-reach character into your reach.10Kingslayer: You keep a Kingslayer score, starting at 0. When you personally kill a character, if their Level is greater than your Kingslayer score, increase your score to match it. Add your score to all Damage-dealing weapons you wield as bonus Damage, using a Damage Type of your choice that the weapon already possesses.11‚Äì12Company Loyalty: The Factions you lead are immune to external persuasive sabotage (i.e. morale, negotiations). So long as you reign, their trust in their own institution is unshakable ‚Äî dissent can only come from within.
Class Backgrounds

















































































d66I was (a)‚Ä¶11‚Äì12Professional who (1d6: 1‚Äì2 committed regicide / 3‚Äì4 was betrayed / 5‚Äì6 deserted)13‚Äì14An impoverished Jester trainee who fled in my youth15‚Äì16Amateur bandit from childhood onward21‚Äì22Militia drill leader during the coraler invasion23‚Äì24Youngest child of a Lord, threatened with disinheritance25‚Äì26Arena fighter & entertainer who (1d6: 1‚Äì2 killed the wrong man / 3‚Äì4 became a revolutionary / 5‚Äì6 was freed by a patron with a purpose)31‚Äì32Jester‚Äôs temple guard, who failed to protect it33‚Äì34Freed from debt-slavery only to find my home destroyed35‚Äì36Caravan guard captain who grew sick of dangerous roads41‚Äì42Veteran of a (1d6: 1‚Äì3 foreign war / 4‚Äì6 failed revolt) with nowhere left to go43‚Äì44Hunter who kept going after bigger prey, until the prey started being people45‚Äì46Magistrate‚Äôs leg-breaking enforcer51‚Äì52True believer in (1d6: 1‚Äì2 Kain‚Äôs return / 3‚Äì4 the old gods / 5‚Äì6 a personal cause)53‚Äì54Sailor shipwrecked on Glass during the Coraler War a few years ago55‚Äì56Trained from childhood by a (1d6: 1‚Äì3 disgraced Lord / 4‚Äì6 wandering master)61‚Äì62A (1d6: 1‚Äì2 pit fighter / 3‚Äì4 gladiator / 5‚Äì6 slave) who won my way to freedom63‚Äì64Village hero who killed something terrible & couldn‚Äôt escape the fame65‚Äì66Child of a foreign mercenary commander
Gangster

Races: Any.
Sex: Both.

You are a smuggler, racketeer, & underworld financier. You solve problems beyond the scope of what the Lords‚Äô courts can help with. You enforce contracts & loans a Magistrate would arrest both parties for. You deal in foreign windguns & gold. You deal in the shadows. If you had your way, nobody would ever know you existed ‚Äî and most never do.

Origami Assassin

Race: Human, if questionably so.
Sex: Both.

A tool does not carve its own path. It is wielded by the hand that grips it. A blank sheet is an infinite canvas. Folded properly, it makes a million masks.
A Glassian court is porcelain masks hiding snakes. When your skin is white as snow, the two blend together. The Origami Assassin can no longer discern between their mask & their nature.
For two thousand years, Kain‚Äôs court instruments kept a grip on rebellious lords. The Emperor‚Äôs Thousand Hands served a dual purpose as a court‚Äôs weapons & chains. Priests for the priesthood, driven by a blade, just proved to be fuel for the revolutions & uprisings that followed in the end when the Second Phoenix left his post & tossed the realm to wolves.
The Origami Assassin, supposedly, does not want power. They want a task. Give them a name, a problem & a patron. Some say that it is the Origami Assassins who hide behind the thrones of the Lords, ruling not by iconography but by the knife they place on the throat of their employers. Some say that with Kain gone, there‚Äôs nothing beneath the mask.
Tradition Feature

Origami Assassin: You are an instrument of the defunct Empire of Glass. A blade without a master is a dangerous thing. You live for bloodlust, violence, stealth, restraint, & the fine arts. Your antithesis is pacifism, protection, emotions, & nurture.

When you incapacitate or kill a character, restore 1 exhausted Spell Die.
You can only cast spells while touching a bladed weapon; a sheathed one is fine.
You can illustrate, sculpt, write, & compose as a competent artist in any mundane discipline. You are a master of origami.

Forester

Race: Any.
Sex: Both.

The natural world is chaotic & disordered. Nature is the enemy of civilization. Men are born into a world that wishes them dead. Were it the choice of nature, the mortal tribes would struggle under flesh, sinew, & bone.
It is not mere metal alone that broke the back of Gaia; it was the will of men that forged iron into the axe. And It was the foresters who took that axe & swung it against the oak & ash.
A Forester is a controlling magician. He is stewed in zen silence. He orders gardens of rock & tree into tightly-bound aesthetic spiral patterns. He sees the world & wishes to make it like those places. Foresters are beloved by the Commons & hated by the wild. There was never an animal that didn‚Äôt recognize that bold & arrogant flesh.
Tradition Feature

Forester: You defy & conquer nature. Your world is that of flesh & flora groomed to mortal will. You love order, quiet, aesthetics, calming & management; you despise chaos, competition, unordered change, extremes (especially in temperature) & decay.

While in a managed garden or direct sunlight, your SD cannot be exhausted. You know what any kind of plant needs to live by touching it.
Non-domesticated animals do not trust you & when they‚Äôre hostile, they always preferentially attack you over other options. Tame animals (i.e. circus tigers) are not domesticated.

Metallurge

Race: Any.
Sex: Both.

Metal wants to usurp. It is metal that let men dishonor the bear, sever sinew, cut stone.
Metal wants to conquer. Perfect metal, whose lustrous gold & silver draw men to its side & whose copper & tin gave them weapons to fight ‚Äî whose steel made them gods.
The earth, fire, sea & sky revile the wound that metal is. A hated alien hive imprinted in its mountains, an interdimensional scar from a prior history ‚Äî lead in the water. But it whispers, it calls, & it grants men power.
Metal is a goddess. The metallurge is simply its priest. They are competent but mad blacksmiths. They are swordsmen granted too much time in plate-metal shells. Cobblers & craftsmen whose veins run with mercury. A metallurge has been touched by a legionary mind, has succumbed to the interests of their lustrous god. Their will blends with their tools‚Äô. They will find a way to become complete.
Tradition Feature

Metallurge: Metal has a soul. You know it when you feel it. Perfect immortality can be found in lustrous gold, flowing mercury, unbreakable adamantine. You love heat, craftsmanship, armor, the blade, metals of all sorts; you despise the natural world, its flora & fauna & waters & biological life.

You are Resistant to Fire Damage. You can ignore small flames & brief exposures (i.e. reaching into fireplaces & brushing aside torch-flames).
Given at least a minute, you can warp, twist, extrude, or reshape small metal objects with gentle, steady pressure.
You cannot swim. You sink like a lead brick.

Jester

Races: Mostly human Glassians, but a handful of salamanders & harpies are also Jesters.
Sex: Stereotyped as female, but approximately 50:50 split.

A clown is a warning. An excess of fear, of curiosity, of sloth, of disregard. Clowns are excessive. They warn through example.
Glass is a forbidden fantasy, a dance of light & color among the falling blade of civil war. Glass as a hidden kaleidoscope of spirits in the woods is the vision that the Jesters reveal. In Glass, the monster is in your house; in your drink; in your mind. In Glass, the monster is smiling, dancing, dressed in rainbow or in general‚Äôs ribbons, leading your children away ‚Äî away to a fairies‚Äô feast, or to the war ‚Äî as the prize dish in either case!
O, to the Jesters you will walk when your body gives out! They know more than dance & song. Their shrines house graveyards for Man & beast alike. More than warn, they negotiate. A Jester teases the beast who‚Äôd eat you away from the thought. Color & light to speak to the spirits who‚Äôd never utter a word. Tricks & surprises are what keep you alive when you‚Äôre up against the woodlands. They are, at their best, every mythic trickster in a frail mortal shell. plastered with colored paints & covered under a smiling mask.
Tradition Feature

Jester: You are a spirit-keeping clown, warning & advising the Commons of Glass through trickery & story. You love indirection, insight, trickery, aid, wisdom, & horror. You eschew direct violence, the elements, & the alteration of nature.

You are assumed neutral. You are welcome at all non-private social functions where invites are distributed provided that you do not act to harm anyone, up to & including distribution or malicious use of private information. This protection extends to all sacred shrine grounds & general hospitality.
If you violate your neutrality, your exhausted SD cannot be restored until you persuade the victim to accept an offering in apology.
Your SD cannot exhaust while at a party, ball, play, funeral, or other social event or rite.

@ Snow Jester
@@@
Out in the taiga, beneath boughs of cold conifers, silence deafens the senses.
A blanket of snow is a quieting thing. It cushions sound, smothers it as it does life.
Snow Jesters are not excessive. They are stilling. They control excess.

Socioeconomic function: Same as Jesters. They‚Äôre just a branch that deals heavily with the dead. Their work is in reanimation & ancestral work. The wilderness is colder & more dangerous out rimward than coreward.

2) Race
Your Race is your lineage. You may pick any Race that your Class is eligible for. Your character inherits the Race‚Äôs statblock (write down each stat on your character sheet) as well as its Racial Features (write all of these down too).

You only really solidify under pressure. That‚Äôs what the Coralers know. The instant they lose a clear target & source of resistance, their clans dissolve into a fiery mess of tangled feuds.
Coralers are frighteningly quick to violence & almost as quick to forgive; playful & vicious, voracious artisans & (often) schizophrenics; their ink makes them natural painters. They are as ephemeral as the tides & as ever-resurgent. Their dominion over the Inner Sea‚Äôs trade will remain unquestioned insofar as their coral reefs are a redoubt no enemy can assault. They will never rule Aia, but will always command its heart.
Almost every Coraler in Glass is an invader from the decades of their conquest of the coast, or a refugee fleeing the new imperial powers of the Inner Sea who‚Äôve swept in to take revenge after their latest explosion of violence & a devastating plague ripping through their under-sea reefs. A rare few are Deep Nomads on pilgrimage or estranged from their clan, a tribal distinction that nobody else ever cares to make.

With a brush & glue medium, it becomes neon glow-in-the-dark underwater paint, good for a few days. Changing your ink color takes an hour of concentration (& changes your tentacle color to match).

Harpies have a bad rap. It‚Äôs not that flying isn‚Äôt useful ‚Äî they‚Äôre favored as messengers & scouts. But when you can‚Äôt manage an ounce of foresight & your principal unit of society is an invading flock, you might expect you‚Äôd become known as ‚Äúbird-brains‚Äù, ‚Äúfeatherheads‚Äù, or ‚Äúrats with wings‚Äù. But that doesn‚Äôt matter. As far as you know, your kin, the flock, is the only way to survive.
Glassian harpies are split between urban ‚Äútownbirds‚Äù & wild hunter-gatherers. Harpies are the backbone of every village defense & military reconnaissance plan in addition to serving as a vast distributed post office capable of delivering almost anywhere on Glass in under a week. Even though harpies are disastrously unproductive & energy-intensive in an agricultural society (averaging 1 family per 50 supporting human families),
Wilderness harpies are, in contrast, intermittently tolerated & despised. Glass ‚Äî the rivers run with ruby-crusted sands! Glass ‚Äî the water-flows off arching rooftop gutter channels! Glass ‚Äî gold-white birches paint the land!
People keep drinking to pretend things are okay.
Imagine what that‚Äôs like. The festivals are dying out & the forest spirits are rioting. The greatest war in all known history is eating the world. Everyone‚Äôs a slave, broken, fleeing, joining General Ram, or turning mercenary.
And they‚Äôre still here, partying. Because nobody has ever invaded Glass & won. Nobody has ever made finer pottery or jewels. None were ruled by a wiser lord. Nobody was wealthier, or more beautiful ‚Äî yes, you, slender & porcelain-white & tall, you Men of Glass ‚Äî the gods themselves crawl in every cup & tree & make themselves your servants‚Äî
‚Äîand yet, it all burns. Immortal or not, reincarnating or not ‚Äî the free Men of this island will die with it if they don‚Äôt soon learn to fight.
Racial Features

Fists (Natural Weapon): 2 Physical.



                  
                  What makes humans any good? 
                  
                

What humans lack in special qualities (the flight, caving, or swimming of other races) they gain in logistical power. Humans are made for war. They have the best carrying capacity & their base 2 HD means that they can field armies of twice the brute strength of the other races, who only make up for it by their environmental advantages. You can‚Äôt challenge a harpy in the air, a salamander underground, or a seasucker in the depths ‚Äî but you cannot fight an armed human on dry land, & every other race is in some way dependent upon that land.


Salamander









































KeyValueHD1BR1Power[HD] + [Level]Starting HP6 √ó [Power]SpeedsRun 25‚ÄôWeight15#Max. Load10# (2 per 3# Weight)Size5‚Äô
There are no records of dwarves. No faces, no records in the Templevaults they were said to have built. After their supposed vanishing off the face of Aia, the salamanders are their only testament ‚Äî a strange race of predatory lizard-like ‚Äúhumans‚Äù with scaled tails, claws, and hydra‚Äôs ears, brought forth from the depths of the earth.
Glass‚Äôs salamanders are all imported & mostly not by choice. Intermittent wars with the Bread Bay kingdoms saw captives made servants on both sides, & in Glass, ‚Äòmander slaves found a potent role in underground labor. Mines are brutal, unrelenting places for a human, but for a salamander, it‚Äôs home ‚Äî so much a home that as centuries passed, mining clans wholly native to Glass formed around fractured blood-memories of their prairie homeland.
Salamanders dominate the mineral trade. Glassians haven‚Äôt mined ore in a full century. You are the mining guilds. You are the source of iron, bronze, & steel. After centuries of increasingly nominal servitude, you control the beating heart of every war machine fighting for control over Glass. In the midst of this infernal slaughter, your clans may find a permanent freedom.
Racial Features

Claws (Natural Weapon): 4 Physical. Excavate dirt & stone at twice a normal human rate.
Semi-Subterranean: You can detect all harmful gases & can identify their effects (if not their names) with the slightest whiff at least a split second before you start to suffer from it (& can choose to hold your breath). Your scaled ears always know the direction to go in to find the nearest open-air exit in any structure or underground cavern from subtle air currents (unless the air is being heavily disturbed).
Cave Joints: Being Prone has no impact on your Run score. When wearing no armor, you can double over yourself & turn around in crawlspaces. You cannot throw things.
Prehensile Tail: You have a lizard-tail that can serve as a hand for holding & lightly manipulating things (i.e. lanterns, Totems for casting). It lacks the strength & dexterity required for complex or forceful motions.
Dwarven Blood Progenitor: If you were born into a Salamander clan, you have a legendary dwarf ancestor. You genuinely believe he existed. Make up (or roll for, see below) an absurd dwarven heraldic feat & write down the one-sentence story as a Fact. Each Salamander PC, if they declare themselves part of your clan at character creation, can add their own absurd heraldic feat (& sentence) to the Fact & share in its combined legend. The legend can be invoked any time you attempt something related to your clan‚Äôs legend. No matter how many feats are added, it‚Äôs still only one Fact, so it can only be invoked once for any given Save or other effect.

@ 4) Level & Retainer
Your Level in Glass Empires is derived from the people who depend upon & support you. Your ‚ÄúLevel‚Äù is borrowed from your largest ruled Faction (p. @) ‚Äî specifically, it‚Äôs determined by the number of Families in the Capital of your largest ruled Faction.
While a Family for population‚Äôs sake is estimated to be ~5 people (a husband, wife, & 3 offspring of whom ~1.5 on average can work), for the sake of getting Level 1, any number of dependents qualifies you. If you have a Retainer you‚Äôre responsible for, whether that be your squire, little sibling, wise old parent, or apprentice, you have a Family.
For the sake of PCs alone, the Party (as in a ‚Äútraditional‚Äù tightly-knit group of PCs) qualifies as a Family, because you all depend upon each other & support each other (as a gaming group). Therefore, all PCs have at least 1 Family even if they have no Retainer, and are Level 1.
Since a traditional Party of PCs is at the outset likely a mobile band, you qualify as Nomads (p. @) & therefore your Capital is your mobile encampment. As a consequence, your Retainers are qualified as Family by virtue of being ‚Äúin your Capital‚Äù as in ‚Äúbeing your traveling partners‚Äù.
You may check the adjacent table to find the association between ruled Families & Faction Level.
