@ 6) Group Entanglement


                  
                  @ Braunsteins 
                  
                

I should investigate how to run Braunsteins. May be a better setup than this gang thing. When I do, this section will need rewriting.


Stop. This step is done by all Players at once & you should wait until everyone is done with their PC before proceeding. The most important element to the creation of a new Party is making sure that all PCs in the Party are capable of working together & have a good reason to do so. Therefore, all Players must collectively identify a reason for them to trust each other & be working towards the same approximate goal ‚Äî power & security.
To do this, the Players may collectively come up with a novel reason or roll on the table below to generate a conceptual seed to expand on. Alternatively, the GM may establish a campaign-specific reason for entanglement.

































1d6Entanglement1You‚Äôre all in debt to the same local Faction who called in favors simultaneously.2You‚Äôre old friends, planning joint revenge against a shared enemy who wronged you all.3You‚Äôre old friends planning a joint venture to survive the civil war‚Äôs chaos.4You‚Äôre collectively seeking a treasure, person, or secret that requires all your skills.5You‚Äôve been hired by a local Faction for a specific dangerous job none could do alone.6You‚Äôre all being hunted by the same Faction; there‚Äôs safety in numbers.

Book-1---Glass-Empires/Part-1---Rules/{-.title: { 4 } - Factions
Book-1---Glass-Empires/Part-1---Rules/{-.content:
A Faction is one or more characters that compose a set of dependent & supportive Families surrounding a place of operations called a Capital, working under a notable or charismatic Leader (or Ruler) for the sake of achieving a shared agenda, represented by a series of Moves set & achieved on or after their Deadlines come to pass.
Factions may occasionally also have Features just like characters. Features represent exceptional abilities or rules that apply to that Faction and usually relate to the institutional structure of a Faction (i.e. feudal kings vs. mercantile enterprises).


                  
                  Example
                  
                

The following is a complete Faction:

Myrtle Seedshall (Seedshall Cottage, 0) - Work up the nerve to ask Mausashi to take her to the Academy in the Woods (April 4)

Debt to Mausashi for saving her life



Name, Capital & Families (0 means it‚Äôs just the Leader), Move, Deadline, & a Debt to Mausashi.


Sovereignty & Subfactions
Many of a Faction‚Äôs Actions are delegated to Subfactions, which are Subordinate Factions. Each Subfaction is led by its own Leader, who is a Retainer of their Liege (the Leader of the master Subfaction). Factions with no master are Sovereign.
A Faction can be Subordinate to at most one Faction, and is also considered Subordinate to any Faction above their Liege if their Liege is not Sovereign (a Faction that is not Subordinate to any other Faction is Sovereign).


                  
                  Example
                  
                

An Earl whose direct Liege is a Count is also a Subordinate Subfaction to the Count‚Äôs Liege, the King of England. The Earl cannot have a second Liege ‚Äî for who can serve two masters without loving one & despising the other?


Limits on Factions
Leader Limitations
A character can be the Leader of many Factions at one time, but their Factions can only work on a Move if the Leader personally dedicates himself full-time to overseeing the Move.
Therefore, a Leader overseeing multiple Factions without delegation to Subfactions is substantially weaker than one who delegates, & he is likely to invite catastrophe.
A Faction delegated to a Retainer will do nothing without its Leader unless it is wholly granted to that Retainer & made into a permanent Subfaction (p. @).
Factions Can‚Äôt Have Facts
Factions cannot have Facts. Only their leaders can. They do have types (p. @), which are similar in that they allow specialization of purpose.
Factions Don‚Äôt Have Moods
Factions share the Mood (p. @) of their Leaders.


                  
                  What If a Faction's Population Hates Its Ruler? 
                  
                

Then it should have a Subfaction representing its angered Families (maybe a Cult of Personality?), with a Leader who hates his Liege. Remember that characters can be part of any number of Factions, so there‚Äôs no reason they can‚Äôt share most of the same Families! Doing it this way also provides you the mechanical framework for hostilities between the populace & the Leader.


Families, Capitals & Settlements
Families is a score indicating the number of households located in a Faction‚Äôs Capital that provide support for & has a dependency on that Faction. All human power is downstream from the Families who follow a Leader.
One household is estimated to be 5 people ‚Äî on average, a husband, his wife, and three children, of whom one is capable of labor & war & might be an independent unmarried agent.
The table adjacent correlates Families to Faction Level. The scale is approximately logarithmic; each step is around 3.16 √ó the Follower count of the previous step.





















































































































Familiesin¬ÝCapitalFactionLevelCapitalSizeArmySizeRealmSizeEuro NobilityEquivalent0‚Äî‚ÄîA person‚Äî‚Äî1+1Cottage3-man lanceFarmGentleman3+2ManorSquadManorLord10+3HamletPlatoon‚Äî‚Äî30+4Small villageCompany‚Äî‚Äî100+5VillageCohort‚Äî‚Äî300+6Small townBattalion‚Äî‚Äî1,000+7Large townBrigadeBaronyBaron3,000+8CityDivisionCountyCount10k+9Large cityCorpsDuchyDuke30k+10MetropolisField armyPetty kingdomKing100k+11MegalopolisImperial armyKingdom‚Äî300k+12RomeFrontEmpireEmperor
To determine Faction Level from a Families score:

Find the number of digits in the Families stat (i.e. a village of 120 = 3).
Double it (i.e. 3 √ó 2 = 6).
If the first digit in the Families stat is 2 or 1, subtract 1 (i.e. ‚Äú1‚Äù in 120, so 6 ‚àí 1 = 5).

To determine a viable Families number from a given character Level:

Divide the character‚Äôs Level by 2 (i.e. Level 7 √∑ 2 = 3.5).
Write any number with that many digits. If you got a 0.5, round up, but the first digit must be 1 or 2 (i.e. 3.5 = 4 digits, but no more than 2,999).

The Followers of the Leader of an entire Settlement (a Government, p. @) is equal to the number of Families living in that Settlement, because even if they don‚Äôt like their Leader, their presence is still contributing to that Leader‚Äôs power.
If a Leader doesn‚Äôt control the entirety of a Settlement, their Capital is a smaller structure within that Settlement (such as its market, gate society/guards, temple, bank, or a business) & his Families represent the attendees, customers, employees, or other people who support his Faction, depending on his Faction Type (p. @).
A Faction‚Äôs Level is tied to its current Families score & is frequently referenced by a character, so it‚Äôs worth writing down the Level of a Leader‚Äôs controlled Factions on his sheet.


                  
                  Tip
                  
                


For example, a city of 40,000 has 5 digits (√ó2 = 10) & the highest digit (4) is greater than 3; therefore, that city‚Äôs Leader has a Level of 11. And if you look at the table, you‚Äôll find that that‚Äôs exactly correct.

Similarly, you can convert from Leader Level to Follower score by dividing the Leader‚Äôs Level by 2 & writing a number with that many digits, starting with 3 if there‚Äôs a 0.5 remainder & 1 if there isn‚Äôt.

For example, a Leader of Lv13: 13 √∑ 2 = 6.5. There‚Äôs a 0.5 remainder & 6 digits, so the Leader must rule a Capital of at least 300,000 people (but not ‚â•1,000,000, or else he‚Äôd be Level 14).





                  
                  How many Factions can someone be a Follower of? 
                  
                

People can be Followers to many Factions at once.
The Followers of the smallest village in a kingdom are considered Followers of the village mayor, the local province‚Äôs lord, the magistrate above him, the bureaucratic body overseeing the region, & the emperor himself, in addition to being Followers of whichever businesses & gods & family members they patronize. Their commitments directly serve to reinforce & enhance the Factions they commit to. The degree of their devotion doesn‚Äôt matter; the math already accounts for variation in support.




                  
                  Why do we track or care about Capitals? 
                  
                

Many reasons!

You do not rule territory; you rule people. Rural regions are of such thin density that to declare control over them is at best a technicality.
You need administrators to rule any other settlement. They‚Äôre simply too distant to monitor.
It is trivial (both in-world & as a GM) to count the population of one city, which is related to the fact that it‚Äôs a small area that‚Äôs easily managed by a person living in a single fortified location nearby.

As soon as you start counting all controlled populations, you have a billion issues determining which areas are controlled & which aren‚Äôt; this leads to drawing borders & arguing over population density for people you‚Äôd have no right to know even existed.
Additionally, outlying populations‚Äô proof of existence is in the existence of the city itself. If a city didn‚Äôt have rural populations, the city would not exist. It is dependent upon them for survival. Therefore, we can simplify them out of the equation & only account for the city.


Gods, too, are limited in power by space & distance. Every god is local.
Rulers, for much the same as the above reasons, cared far more about their local demesne & capital than outlying regions, and tended to invest heavily in their own capital precisely because it was most important to them.
Nearly all pre-industrial capitals were the single largest city in their respective realms, because power attracts people ‚Äî specifically, it attracts courtesans, foreign diplomats, assassins & thieves, mercenaries, fellow rulers, scribes, spies, and every other form of scum who seek proximity to power.
It is absolutely critical to maintain a basis in economics. All human relationships are economic in nature, especially grudges. A defined capital allows tethering of Faction activity to in-game economic factors (such as trade routes, iron mines, and population densities) which lets us turn proximity into relationships that matter.



@ Factions Performing Moves
A Leader can orchestrate one of their Factions into performing a Move with them, such as Resting (p. @) an Army or stockpiling assets (p. @) using the Faction‚Äôs collective labor force. Many Moves are in fact designed exclusively for use by Leaders who are orchestrating a Faction & require that a Faction be involved.
When a Move is set for a Faction by its Leader, the GM writes down a Description of what the Move is set to accomplish adjacent to the Faction name on his list along with the Deadline on the calendar (p. @), the date on which it will be completed.
Faction Move Calendar Process
Whenever the Deadline for a Move made by a Faction comes up on the calendar (p. @), run through the procedure below.
1) Save Modifiers
Factions don‚Äôt have Facts other than for Debts (p. @), although a Leader‚Äôs Facts can still impact his Faction. Instead, every time a Leader performs a Save for his Faction, he must run through this list of questions. For each question, give +1 if it resolves unquestionably in the Leader‚Äôs favor, and penalize ‚àí1 if it‚Äôs unquestionably against it.

Institutional Knowledge: Is this within the Faction‚Äôs role as an organization to do, as determined by the social context of what the Faction is?
Power: Is it obviously within the Faction‚Äôs power (financial, military, social) to accomplish?
Precedent: Has this ever been done before (to local knowledge)?
Foreign: Is it reliant on foreign technology/methods that aren‚Äôt adopted locally?
Opposition: Is at least one Faction strongly opposed & in a position to interfere/deploy at least some countermeasures?

2) Faction Opinions & Counter-Moves
Whenever a Move is completed, scan the Faction List for Factions that were harmed or aided by the outcome:

If more than one Faction is Angered by a Move‚Äôs outcome, they will join forces if appropriate & scheme together to bring about their revenge or adaptations to the outcome of the Move.
3) Inform the Players
Whatever the effects, find a way to inform the Party that it happened. Gossip, town criers, briefings by castellans or stewards or courtiers; if a Faction did it, it comes up soon after with the cause-effect relationship reasonably clear. Even if it was an act of espionage or subtlety, they hear about the consequences or something related to it in a subtle or coded way. Make a note on the calendar if you have to delay it.
4) New Move & Outcomes
Next, the GM comes up with a new Move for the original Faction to pursue.
If it isn‚Äôt obvious what the Faction would do next based on their current Debts, agenda, enemies, or situation, roll on the table below to generate a new Move.

























1d6Faction Move1(Conquer/sabotage/subjugate/destroy/supplant) the most vulnerable neutral/hostile Faction, whichever is most appropriate.2Build/expand on either the easiest or most substantial advantage available to either create a new Subfaction or to gain powers/resources/manpower directly3Form a friendship, alliance, exploitative partnership, exchange/trade agreement, or vassal protection scheme with a Faction who has common interests for the sake of extracting a specific advantage or achievement4‚àí6Do something related to satisfying the Leader‚Äôs personal interests or agenda.
Then, try to fit it to the Faction. The final Move should be both:

Applicable to the agenda & capabilities of the Faction;

Remember that Subfactions are not obligated to act in their Liege‚Äôs interest and will, Leader morality notwithstanding, generally pursue their own even if it conflicts with their Liege‚Äôs interests if they believe they can get away with it.


Capable of impacting the PCs, their environment, or any party they care about.

If you try as hard as you can & fail to find a Move that meets both of these criteria, ask yourself if the Faction is still relevant to the PCs, their subordinates, Factions they care about, or their environment:

Yes: The Faction becomes Idle, working on nothing in particular.
No: The Faction becomes Veiled & you must roll a Random Faction Event (p. @).

5) PC Recruitment Event
At the outset of any new Move, if members of the Faction believe any PC in the area can potentially help with it & that seeking their assistance is unlikely to compromise their goals, one of them will reach out to one of the viable PCs (chosen at random) to recruit them.

Procedure: Unveiling Factions
Veiled Factions are those that have become, for the time being, irrelevant to the game. Maybe the scope of play moved far beyond or beneath their interest; maybe the PCs left the city to chase various quests.
Whenever the Party re-enters the area of influence of a Veiled Faction (by literally re-entering a controlled region such as the capitol or by attempting to probe/interact with the Faction), the GM performs a Save to assess changes to that Faction. Add the Faction Leader‚Äôs Level; subtract a penalty based on time passed using the adjacent Time Passed/Penalty table.

@ The Party is a Faction
But only if the PCs make it one.
In other words, the Party is a Faction so long as all PCs merge their own activities & Subfactions underneath its banner, and clearly delinate the Party as an entity that actually exists in the world. It might be framed as a warband, a traveling carvan, or a nascent government ‚Äî just so long as it can encompass the Factions the PCs also run.
Any PC may represent the Party as its Leader for any situation requiring a Leader‚Äôs action.

Move: Create a new Subfaction
To create a new Subfaction, you must:

Promote a Leader: You have to promote one of your Retainers or someone else you know to the position of Leader.
Define its role: Determine the nature of the Subfaction. This has a direct impact on its actual ability to serve you; you are shaping the structure of the institution, and that‚Äôs limited by what your realm is actually capable of supporting.
Gift a Capital: A physical Settlement or other Capital (such as a castle, or a building or district inside a larger Settlement) suited to its nature must be gifted to the Leader. The new Capital must:

Be controlled by you.
Possess Families at least equal to the minimum Families required to support a Subfaction of the Level you wish to create ‚Äî you can‚Äôt make a high-Level Faction without gifting them a real Capital.
Be unclaimed ‚Äî not currently be the Capital of any other active Faction, excepting claimed subdivisions (i.e. buildings in a city, offices in a building).

No active Faction doesn‚Äôt mean ‚Äúno current government‚Äù ‚Äî it means the current institution isn‚Äôt organized or independent enough to resist your administrative meddling.


Come with any territory or land that would de jure be part of its domain (you can‚Äôt gift a castle without gifting the lands around it, after all).


Transfer assets: You may need to transfer assets such as Settlements located within the territory granted to a Subfaction.

The Deadline for creating a new Subfaction is set based on the adjacent table & the Followers in the new Capital.

                  How do I make an asset when I don't rule a Faction? 
                  
                

Characters may treat their job as a Fact. Similarly, applicable Facts (i.e. greed, thriftiness) may indicate higher production capacity than your Faction‚Äôs Followers otherwise suggests. In addition, if you have a liquid sum (such as silver dollars you plundered), you can simply dump that into an Asset.
Then again, even a master blacksmith is Level 3 ‚Äî he has journeymen & apprentices under him ‚Äî and a fundamental tenant of Glass Empires is that no man is an island. If you need better production capacity, the best place to get it is to learn to command.


Move: Plunder a Settlement
To plunder a controlled Settlement, you must order an Army whose BR is at least equal to the Settlement‚Äôs Families to do so.
You may simultaneously plunder any number of smaller nearby controlled Settlements so long as they aren‚Äôt closer to a Settlement larger than the main target. Add 1 day to the Deadline for each additional Settlement plundered in this manner.
The Deadline for plundering in days is equal to the Army‚Äôs number of troops divided by the total plundered Families (across all afflicted Settlements), minimum 1 day.
At the end of the Deadline, you & your Army gain $5 √ó 3d6 √ó [Families plundered] in $. Afterwards, each affected Settlement‚Äôs Families score is halved & you may destroy any notable landmarks within the plundered Settlement(s). No Save is required.
You can repeatedly loot Settlements. If you want to obliterate them & all surrounding farmland, it takes 4 times as long as usual & turns all Settlements into voided ruins with 25% of their original SHP value (if any).
Move: Grow Follower Base
If your Faction has its Capital located inside a Settlement & only has Families equal to a portion of that Settlement‚Äôs total Families, you may attempt to raise your number of Families by converting more people within the Settlement to follow you, an ally, one of your Retainers/Subfactions or your Liege.
Describe to the GM how you are plausibly attempting to convert people & to whose side. It doesn‚Äôt have to be your Faction; you can work on someone else‚Äôs behalf.
The Deadline is 2 months for low switching costs (commodities & entertainment), 4 months for medium (guilds & banking, mercenary services), & 8 months for high switching costs (ethnic associations, religious faiths, nobility).
At the Deadline, make a Success Save to determine the base number of how many Families could be gained.





























ResultSuccess Save EffectCrit‚â•12100% of the target‚Äôs current Families.Pass8‚Äì1150% of the target‚Äôs current Families.Seven710% of the target‚Äôs current Families.Failure3‚Äì6Nothing.Fumble‚â§210% of your current Families (not the target‚Äôs) leave you.
Reduce the amount gained to at most:

([Settlement‚Äôs Families] ‚àí [Competitors‚Äô Families]) √∑ 2.

A Competitor is a Faction in the same Settlement that shares the same financial niche as you ‚Äî whether that be a mercantile, banking, legal, government, military, magical, educational, industrial, or entertainment niche matters not.
Examples abound: Two bakeries serving the same artisan niche; a messenger guild & a cult serving a divine messenger; two gate/guard societies; two banking houses; two divisions within the same company (they can‚Äôt share employees). When in doubt, ask: ‚ÄúWould a customer choosing Faction A have any reason to also patronize Faction B?‚Äù If not, they‚Äôre Competitors.
If you want to try to claim Families from a Competitor, you should try Sabotage (p. @), warfare, or other forms of attack. You can also differentiate your offerings so that you‚Äôre not directly competing with entrenched forces ‚Äî for example, a revolutionary movement would struggle to make open headway against an established town police force, so they can differentiate by instead going underground & only move to the open after growing in numbers.


                  
                  Wait, so can't I found a business or bank in a major city & get tons of Followers quickly, and then hit a high level? 
                  
                

Yes, but there are no Class Features for doing this since peacetime businesses aren‚Äôt relevant to any Classes, so you don‚Äôt get any heroic power, just institutional power. It‚Äôs also still difficult & time-consuming to make a business work & transferring business assets into conquest is a tricky business by itself.


Hidden Factions
A hidden Faction is trying to hide itself from others, or from a specific Faction. It may only be trying to hide a specific secret (such as that it‚Äôs actually the Subfaction of a different Faction). It suffers a ‚àí1 penalty to all Moves for as long as it continues to hide secrets.
A hidden Faction is revealed to everyone if it suffers a Fumble on a Save while trying to be hidden, & cannot hide itself thereafter.
Faction Types
A Faction has exactly 1 type. Nobody can be good at everything, & every institution has its own weaknesses reflected in its intrinsic character.
In economics, this is called ‚ÄúDutch Disease‚Äù ‚Äî the weakness of overspecialization ‚Äî but in truth, everyone has it. The Navy could never do as good a job at making burgers as McDonalds does. Even you, dear reader, have specializations (i.e. your chosen career skills) that you cannot take back. You too have Dutch Disease.
If you want to reap the benefits of a second type (i.e. you rule a city, but also want a militia), create a Subfaction (p. @) of that type.
@ Government
Your Capital is a Settlement. You rule the entire Settlement as its formal governmental authority, whether that be as a monarchy, oligarchic mayoral tenure, or republic. Your income is tax money from the population, expressed as labor, agricultural products, & some loose coin. You also rule the fertile lands surrounding the Settlement; in truth, your total realm‚Äôs population is maybe twice what the Settlement‚Äôs Families alone number.
You have no special features. Unless otherwise stated, your rules are as what‚Äôs stated as default.
Financier
Qualified Classes: None.
Your Capital is a banking family‚Äôs palace, a massive complex of vaults, offices, & residential quarters that oversees financing to uncountable clients.

Creditor Network: You know every financial debt, defaulted or otherwise, that all Factions owe to other Financier Factions. The GM reveals it by default as relevant.
Promissory Banking: Your Capital is equal to your silver $ reserves plus your Followers, who represent civilian depositors. You can extend loans up to your total Capital to an unlimited number of debtors without losing any $ ‚Äî you gave them your silver, but you have an IOU saying they‚Äôll pay it back (plus interest), which is as good as money to you. This suffers the following restrictions:

When a loan is fully repaid with interest, increase your $ by the interest (typically 10% to 20% of the principal).
If anyone defaults on a loan (p. @), even if you forgave it, you instantly lose that much $ ‚Äî because the IOU won‚Äôt be paid back. It goes bad. If you can‚Äôt lose $, you lose 1 Follower per $ instead.
If more than 25% of your Capital is lost from loans going bad within a single month, you trigger a liquidity crisis.
When a liquidity crisis occurs, you have 1 month to find the funds (i.e. by calling in other debts you‚Äôre owed) to cover the entirety of the $ value of the loans that went bad. If you fail by the deadline, your Faction implodes from a cascade of depositors & debtors realizing you‚Äôve become insolvent. If you succeed, you keep any $ you recovered to survive the crisis, but lost Followers are gone for good.


Non-Territorial: Your actual staff is only at most a couple hundred clerks & guards. Your Followers are debtors & civilian depositors, not productive labor assets. You cannot directly control any Location other than for your business centers (but you can have Subfactions that hold other property). If you lose your vaults (in the Settlement your Capital is in) & haven‚Äôt taken precautions, you lose all of your Faction‚Äôs $ (possibly triggering a liquidity crisis).



                  
                  Playing a Financier 
                  
                

Financiers are information brokers who turn debt relationships into political leverage. Your power comes from knowing who owes what to whom & twisting those relationships to ensure you can cover your bases.
Your primary tools are:

Debt coordination. Offer (complete or partial) debt forgiveness (p. @) to one client if they take hostile action against a second defaulting client. See Negotiation (p. @).
Selective credit. Choose who gets your money. Deny to enemies, extend to allies.
Intelligence. You know everyone‚Äôs financial position & can use this to predict future moves.
Reputation. One unpunished default encourages more. Make brutal examples of your defaulting debtors.
Caution. You can loan tremendous sums, but keeping loans beneath 20% of your Capital prevents a single loan from triggering a crisis.

You are vulnerable to sovereign risk. Your direct liege can seize your offices & vaults & expel you from the realm. If you loan to someone who can kill you, make sure you have allies you can rely on to scare them away from trying. Just remember: One defaulting lord can become the target of all his neighbors who owe you, who will leap at the chance to plunder his realm to pay off their own debts.




                  
                  GM Advice for a Financial Crisis 
                  
                

A Financier‚Äôs implosion sends shockwaves through every Faction that interacted with them.

Oustanding Debts are sold to the highest bidder ‚Äî rival Factions, vulture investors, foreign interests, or any other Financiers operating in the region, if any. Debts are sold for 30% of their actual value & the receiving Faction must pay for it. If no Faction can buy a Debt, find a new world entity that isn‚Äôt modeled yet who could see an opportunity in it. If there is genuinely nobody who could plausibly collect on the Debt (by forcing the debtor to pay), it will vanish ‚Äî but this is rare.
Credit availability becomes a problem, especially if the Financier was the only source of liquid cash. Mercenaries can no longer be hired & Factions can only rely on their own limited discretionary income ‚Äî only $1 per month per Follower.
Speaking of hiring mercenaries, mercenaries become a problem. If nobody is hiring them, they‚Äôre going to be plundering Settlements for $ to operate with.
Every Faction & everyone in a Faction or subordinate to a Faction that owed a Debt to the Financier gets a Economic Collapse until [date it ends] Condition that lasts for 1 year. It penalizes every financial & economic Save. Even random commoners who live in an affected Settlement receive this penalty.
New, smaller banking families have an opportunity to move into the capital city where the old one operated to take over the larger Settlement & thereby expand their basis of Capital. This may produce a brief zero-sum banking war as several families move from a previous city to war over the larger one.

Your Capital is a business, a mercantile or artisan venture. @

Army: Your entire Faction is an Army composed of soldiers, sans baggage train (which is another 25% or so, treated as invisible the same way Settlements don‚Äôt count rural population).
Supply Lines: You require an unobstructed road or path leading back to a Settlement with as many Families as you have soldiers at all times. Armies do not have a Families stat.

Godscult
Qualified Classes: Jesters & other magicians.
Your Capital is a shrine or temple, a complex of magical & spiritual artifacts & training that reflects your power.

Temple: Your Capital is a temple with a shrine that‚Äôs dedicated to a specific entity serving as your subject of worship. So long as you exist, you support the formation & extension of a Godhead (p. @) centered on that entity.
Expensive Maintenance: Cults require upkeep across sacrifice, worship, maintenance of services & other factors that causes them to punch below their weight on everything that isn‚Äôt related to religious or magical expertise. Take a ‚àí1 penalty to all Saves not explicitly relating to your Leader‚Äôs Facts.

Cult of Personality
Qualified Classes: All Classes.
Your Capital is your reputation in this Settlement ‚Äî the stories, songs & legends that follow you. You don‚Äôt command any of these people & you have no institutions to speak of (you certainly can‚Äôt hold a Subfaction with a fan club), but they‚Äôll show up when you need them.

Popular Support: Your Followers represent people who admire you, tell stories about you, or would come to your aid when called upon. You cannot command them to make Moves (this means you can‚Äôt use Grow Follower Base, p. @) & do not control Locations with them, but you can call upon them for shelter & supplies, information gathering, crowd support, & volunteers for desperate & heroic causes to the tune of at most $1 or 1 day of labor per Follower per month.
Reputation-Based: Your Follower count grows through heroic deeds, public victories, artistic works, or dramatic gestures that capture public imagination. It shrinks through public failures, betrayals, or extended absences.

Gain 1 Follower per HD of enemies you defeat (doesn‚Äôt have to be murder) & publicly champion. Gain 1 Follower per $30 you ‚Äúinvest‚Äù in carousing ‚Äî partying & schmoozing.
On any minor public victory/defeat, judged by the GM based on popular attitudes, (gain 50%/lose 33%) of your current Followers.
On any major public triumph/catastrophe, judged by the GM based on popular attitudes, (gain 100%/lose 50%) of your current Followers.
You don‚Äôt lose the Level benefits of a personality cult until you return (‚ÄúI‚Äôm still big back home!‚Äù), but when you do return to a Settlement where you have one, reduce Followers by 10% if it‚Äôs been 3 months, 50% if it‚Äôs been 1 year, 75% if it‚Äôs been 2 years, or 90% if it‚Äôs been 10+ years. Negate 10% of the loss for each wild victorious story you can bring home with you.





                  
                  How do I get a fanbase? 
                  
                

Just start wasting money on partying & bragging about your victories. Try not to brag about anything that will get you in trouble with the law ‚Äî remember that cash from tomb-raiding is taxable & might be the Lord‚Äôs property by law ‚Ä¶ keeping in mind that ‚Äúthe law‚Äù is his sword.
You‚Äôre still limited in Level by the Settlement you‚Äôre showing off in, though; you can‚Äôt have more Followers than there is Families to adore you. To get a high Level, you may want to move away from your little village to the big city, where you not only compete with other celebrities, but may end up involved in serpentine political skullduggery in your efforts to become a hero‚Ä¶


@ Mine Clan
Your Capital is a mine, an underground center for resource production. In Glass, every Mine is managed by Salamanders; Glassians no longer know how. Old restrictions on underground housing have long since worn away, & now every mine is virtually a fully-fledged colony.

Surface Dependency: You require regular food, water, & air from a Neutral, Friendly, or subjugated Subfaction surface trade partner. If these supplies are cut off by hostile forces controlling your surface access, you cannot perform Moves dependent on surface resources, & after 1 month, your Families begin starving en masse as per the rules on Exhaustion Saves (p. @).
Salamander Warrens: Your mine is an unwieldy nightmare of winding tunnels unnavigable by anyone other than salamanders. Armies composed of non-subterranean races cannot invade or occupy you.
Resource Monopoly (Minerals): As a Move with a 1-day Deadline, you can Deny Minerals to any Faction. If that Faction doesn‚Äôt have a Neutral or Friendly relationship with a sovereign who directly or indirectly (through a chain of Subfactions) controls a Mine, all Moves they take that require minerals have a doubled Deadline, and all mineral-dependent items have a doubled cost in $.

@ Growing a Settlement
In order to grow a Settlement to the minimum required Families for its Ruler to gain a Level, the following conditions must be true:

There is no pre-existing bigger Settlement within a radius of ([Families] √∑ 100) miles;
There are at least 3 Settlements with Families large enough to sustain the Ruler‚Äôs current Level in the same realm (counting ‚Äúrealm‚Äù as including your sovereign‚Äôs realm, if you are a vassal) that are not closer to a Settlement that is already larger;

If your Settlement‚Äôs Ruler is currently Level 7 or less, you instead need farmland of area meeting or exceeding your Ruler‚Äôs Level squared (i.e. to go from 7 to 8, you need ~50 square miles of farmland).


The new Families level must not exceed the value set by the adjacent table.














































TerrainTypeMax.FamiliesEffective Max.Ruler LevelDesert995Alpine/arctic2,9008Desert oasis/arid9,9009Mountain valley29,00010Hills/coast99,00011Riverless plains290,00012Fertile river2,900,00014
Settlements who by virtue of changing circumstances come to violate these rules do not lose Families for it, but cannot grow any further.


                  
                  Why these rules? 
                  
                

Central Place Theory, mostly, plus ensuring that cities can‚Äôt grow to unreasonable sizes unless they have suitable agricultural lands around them. Note that trade routes are a means by which massive empires can grow larger, but generally, large sums of food won‚Äôt be funneled to a city from foreign lands until those lands are conquered & made to do so ‚Äî Rome had to conquer Egypt before it reaped the full benefits of Alexandria & the Nile.


Investment
Once per month per Settlement, you can spend $ to raise a Settlement‚Äôs Families. For every $150 you spend, the Settlement gains 1 Family. You can only spend the greater of $1,500 or 5 times the Settlement‚Äôs current Families per month (so even in a village of 55 Families, you can still spend a full $1,500 to draw 10 new Families to the village).
Move: Settlement Growth
You may use your Faction‚Äôs ongoing revenues to produce growth in a Settlement you fully control. Tell the GM how you‚Äôre investing into your Settlement with your Faction‚Äôs labor & resources.
If a Faction has a Manpower Debt (p. @), this Move can be used to eliminate an amount of Debt equal to the number of Families that would otherwise be gained. No Families are added if this Move is used to pay off MP Debt.
Deadline: 1 month.
Result: Success Save, with additional effects.





























ResultAdditional EffectsCrit‚â•12The Settlement gains 10% of its current Families.Pass8‚Äì11The Settlement gains 5% of its current Families.Seven7The Settlement gains 1% of its current Families.Failure3‚Äì6Gain nothing.Fumble‚â§2Gain nothing.
In all cases, round to the nearest 3 significant digits.


                  
                  Example
                  
                

The City of Riverbrook gets a Seven, providing 1% of its current Families of 3,600 (36). This would put them at Pop. 3,636, rounded to 3,640.
A city with 124,000 getting a Seven would get 1,240 new Families, rounding to 125,000.


@ Capital Relocation
You can relocate your Capital.
Placeholder: This takes 1 month.
When you do, you may qualify for Advancement (p. @).
ah, but this means you have to merge the governments?

Book-1---Glass-Empires/Part-1---Rules/{-.filePath: Book 1 - Glass Empires/Part 1 - Rules/{ 5 } - Magic & Divination.md
Book-1---Glass-Empires/Part-1---Rules/{-.title: { 5 } - Magic & Divination
Book-1---Glass-Empires/Part-1---Rules/{-.content:
Magic is the art of working with spirits. In Aia, there are several principal means of doing so:

Casting is the act of expending your own life energy upon captive spirits that have been captured in a Totem to wield their powers in your service. A person who holds one or more Totems & regularly practices the art is a magician, otherwise known as a shaman, priest, wizard, warlock, witch, arcanist, or mage, among other terms.
Ritual cooking is the process of capturing a soul‚Äôs essence through meals that transfer power to a consumer.
Divination (p. @) is the act of communicating with a broadly worshiped entity whose cult activity allows contact to be established. A godscult (p. @) or the entity itself must be present somewhere to perform divination ‚Äî within (Entity Power) √ó 10 of the entity or a cult center.

This chapter explains all of them in detail.
Magic is dangerous. There is no form of magic that can be practiced without risk of permanent harm being dealt to you for tampering with the whims of spirits.
Spell Dice
Spell Dice (henceforth SD) are a resource gained by magicians representing their increased familiarity with & capability for handling spirits. Each represents a d6 die.
Totems
A Totem is a trinket made out of dead Wood or Flesh (the elements; such as bone or timber) that can hold a single spirit (spell or soul) for spellcasting or other purposes. They come in many shapes & sizes, can be heavily decorated, & may be comfortable or not to Spirits of various sorts.
Anyone with at least one Class Feature from a magician-like Class can make Totems. It takes 1 day of labor to make a Totem. You can use a spirited corpse‚Äôs bones as the components to a Totem made to hold the Spirit you‚Äôre capturing.
A Totem weighs a minimum of 1/4#.
Move: Casting
Casting is a Major Action. There are two casting methods to follow depending on the kind of spirit you have. There are two kinds of spell-spirits:

Spells are little spirits. They are captured or tamed & used as lenses for power by magicians of similar mindset & character.
Souls are the spiritual containers of more powerful entities. They are largely fixed in nature & cannot be changed simply by dumping power into them, but given an appropriate sacrifice will act on your behalf.

Both kinds of casting are dangerous.
After you cast a spell, you cannot heal HP until you spend at least a full day Resting (p. @).
Spell-casting
To cast a spell, you must have at least one personal Fact that resembles the nature of the spell. A geomancer needs to be Obsessed With Mathematics before he can use an organizational spell. An avian wizard needs to be Flighty to fly. The relationship can be poetic. More than half the relevant Players, GM included, have to agree. A Fact stating you are part of a Tradition that possesses the spell is always acceptable.
Assuming this condition is met, the process is as follows:

Have a Totem containing a spirit held in a hand in front of you (it doesn‚Äôt have to be your hand), with one of your hands free for gestures & invocation.
Decide how many Spell Dice you want to invest into the casting.
Roll all invested SD & determine the spell‚Äôs effects (i.e. choose targets). The number of SD you invest is the [Dice] variable; the sum of all rolled values is the [Sum] variable.

If all SD rolled 1 (i.e. if [Dice] = [Sum]), the spell fizzles ‚Äî it burns out with no effect.


For each pair of doubled results rolled on your SD, suffer a Mishap from the Mishaps table (p. @). If you only invested 1 SD, a 6 causes a Mishap.

Triples count as 2 Mishaps; quadruples as 3. Group SD results by the largest set of pairs achieved.


Each invested SD that rolls 4‚Äì6 is exhausted.

Exhausted SD are restored after 8 hours of rest.



Soul-casting
To cast a soul:

Have a Totem containing a soul held in a hand in front of you (it doesn‚Äôt have to be your hand), with one of your hands free for gestures & invocation.
Take Damage equal to the spirit‚Äôs Power. This Damage is called your sacrifice.

You may reduce Damage taken by using Spell Dice (p. @) if you have any.


Choose a benefit you wish to invoke from the spirit‚Äôs list of Features:

Grant a passive Feature (or natural weapon) to a target you‚Äôre touching for a chosen number of hours if you immediately take Damage equal to the spirit‚Äôs Power times the chosen number of hours, ignoring Armor.

If it doesn‚Äôt cost an Action to trigger, it‚Äôs Passive.


Activate an active Feature by taking Damage equal to the spirit‚Äôs Power, bypassing your Armor. You must still respect the Feature‚Äôs limitations on usage per period, if any.

If it costs an Action to trigger, it‚Äôs Active.




Roll a single d6 die. On a 1, the spirit-casting fails. On a 6, you suffer a Mishap from the Mishaps table (p. @). On any other value, the casting works as expected.

Mishaps

































1d6Mishaps1After this, no Spirit will respond to you until you spend a full day Resting.2The spell targets you (if harmful), an enemy (if beneficial), or fizzles (otherwise).3Suffer whatever incapacitating or painful Condition is most conceptually related to the Spirit for 1d6 Rounds, as determined by the GM.4Take [Spirit Power] Damage (if soul-casting) or [Sum] Damage (if spell-casting), ignoring Armor.5Suffer a permanent random Mutation.6Roll again on this table, twice.
Using Corpses & Spirits
A spirit in a newly-dead corpse can be manipulated in a number of useful ways.

Funerals can be held to ferry the deceased to an afterlife maintained by a godscult.
Extracting a spirit produces a soul usable for casting, provided you have a Totem (p. @) on hand.
Ritual cooking transfers some of the corpse‚Äôs innate power into you, potentially at a cost.

If a corpse is left to sit for 1 week, the spirit sinks out of the corpse & drifts down towards the Underworld. It is likely to end up in the grip of a hellbound beast & tortured/processed for its leftover energies. This is well-known & feared by all.
Funerals
Funerals require a priest & a spirited corpse. A suitable ritual & ceremony will summon a psychopomp to handle the spirit.
Psychopomps take many forms depending on the spirit pleaded to & their domains. They may appear as a mouse, crow, whirling ball of fire, troop of ants, very tired human runner from the nearest cult temple, wayward ferret or fox, or many others.
There are 2 main benefits to host a funeral for Players:

Lessons from the Dead: When a funeral is hosted, PCs in attendance who knew the deceased well (having been with them long enough for both to grow in Level) may state a truthful lesson they have learned from their dealings with the deceased, if any, & add it to their character sheet as a Fact.
Reincarnation: After creating or adopting a new PC, the Player may transfer a single Class Feature of their choice from their dead PC onto the new one.

A deceased PC‚Äôs Player may choose to begin playing as any Retainer of their former PC.
After death, someone must provide a ‚Äúdonation‚Äù to one of the temples of the cult that took you in within a month, equal to:

$1 √ó HD √ó ([Power] at the time of your death squared, minimum 1)

If your survivors fail to pay within a year, your spirit may be sold off as a debtor‚Äôs spirit or evicted & cast down to Hell. If this happens, PCs lose the benefits from having had the funeral in the first place.
Afterlifes
The afterlife is slumber in a Totem in a cult‚Äôs basement or graveyard. In exchange for funeral fees, the cult will keep your spirit at rest in one of their temples.
You may make advance arrangements (& donations) to receive better treatment in your afterlife. Expensive Totems & priestly attention are very comfortable for a spirit in its final rest. Some cultures consider it prestigious to have their entire body preserved as a single massive Totem.
Your excess spiritual energy will go towards sustaining the Godhead of the cult‚Äôs target of veneration until your soul runs dry, at which point a spirit withdraws into the immaterial Aether & is said to reincarnate.
Some wealthier families in Glass maintain private cults & mausoleums for their ancestors‚Äô souls & may hire a gravekeeper beast or anoint a family member to serve as a focus for an ancestral Godhead.
Desecrating Graves
The desecration of a grave Totem condemns a resting soul to Hell (by shattering the bond keeping it from sinking into the earth) & may create a ghost in the spirit‚Äôs desperation to stay alive. Breaking into an afterlife & destroying spirit vessels is a capital offense in many civilizations.
Debtor‚Äôs Spirits
Some temples sell off the spirits of those whose survivors failed to pay funeral fees as debtor‚Äôs spirits. Most men have little to offer other than for some practical skill with specific tasks.
Extracting a Spirit
Anyone whose Facts/background indicates they learned magic (as spirits are intrinsic to all magic) can extract a spirit. Doing so takes 1 hour per corpse HD & requires you have a viable Totem (p. @) on hand to stuff the Spirit into.
You must choose at the time of extraction if you wish to perform a complete extraction or a partial extraction.

Complete: Copies every Feature from the corpse, but produces a Spirit whose Power is equal to the corpse‚Äôs Power, minimum 1.
Partial: Copies just one Feature from the corpse, producing a Spirit whose Power is equal to half the corpse‚Äôs Power, rounded up, minimum 1.

If a partial extraction is performed, the remaining Features are permanently lost. In either case, the corpse is of course left empty.
Copying Features
When you move to transfer Feature off a corpse & onto its extracted Spirit, the following may all be copied:

Resistances & Immunities: These come as a single package. You get all of the Immunities, Weaknesses, & Resistances applied as a single Feature.
Weapons: Natural weapons are each considered a Feature, including oddball ones with special non-Damage effects.
Features: Anything listed as an explicit special ability, including Class Features.

You cannot copy:

Statblocks: HD, Speed scores, natural Armor, Facts.
Implicit Characteristics: Anything that isn‚Äôt listed as Features (i.e. a shark‚Äôs water-breathing, a bird‚Äôs ability to fly or lay eggs, an angel‚Äôs agelessness) is off the table.

Ritual Cooking
Anyone whose Facts/background indicates they learned cooking can make food out of a fresh spirited corpse. Such food is said to convey some of the blessings ‚Äî & horrors ‚Äî of a given creature.
Making a ritual meal takes 1 hour per corpse. This counts as Resting for everyone involved, including the chefs. When you make a ritual meal, split each corpse‚Äôs HD however is desired among any number of eating characters whose HD summed is no more than the corpse‚Äôs. Each character‚Äôs portion must be at least equal to their own HD.
Each qualifying character must roll 1d6 per HD they possess vs. 1d6 per HD they ate. If their roll is:

Lower than their meal‚Äôs: They:

Mutate (p. @) to become more like the creature they ate. For sapient creatures, this includes the mental qualities of that entity; write down every meal as a Fact indicating what you ate & treat it as applicable to mental & psychological acts.
May pick 1 Racial Feature (including natural weapons) off the corpse to copy that they don‚Äôt already have. Each Feature can only be copied by a single qualifying character. If there‚Äôs a dispute, each character rolls 1d6; highest gets it. Re-roll ties.


Equal to or higher than their meal‚Äôs: They gain 1 HD if their HD is less than their HD share of this corpse.

You may only make 1 meal per corpse. The corpse‚Äôs soul is drained during the cooking process & sinks down towards the Underworld afterwards.
Beastmen & Mass Ritual Sacrifices for Power
Power optimizes around & promulgates stable repeatable structures. Ritual Cooking is not generally optimal. It produces wild, dangerous soldiers of uncertain affiliation & unknown qualities ‚Äî in the case of beast consumption, the result is often called beastmen.
Some realms on Aia optimize around the costs of this by using mass sacrifices (often obtained through ritualistic, systematized war) to produce bestial berserker-like soldiers, accepting the loss rate of mad & undisciplinable soldiers as an acceptable sacrifice in the pursuit of power. Others survive by not doing this, retaining discipline & sanity at the cost of raw power.
Many harpy flocks & some salamanders & coralers prey on humans for the specific reason that doing so grants them a large boost in power ‚Äî going from 1 HD to 2 HD is a big deal for them. The cost is that eating a peasant makes you more peasantlike ‚Äî which includes an incapacity for war or violence. Furthermore, high-quality targets are rare & difficult to hunt specifically because they‚Äôre high-quality.
Adventurers, rulers & the exceptionally strong-willed have comparatively little to lose from a loss in ability to operate as disciplined soldiers & more readily engage in Ritual Cooking practices.
There have been rumored to be cases of resurrective possession wherein warriors have lost their minds to their victim through these rituals. It is said this occurs more commonly with powerful spirits.
Gods & Divination
There is no difference between a god & a creature. A god is a power differential. A king is a god to a peasant. Most ‚Äúgods‚Äù are exceptionally weird creatures or powerful people who, through guile, cunning, strength, or dumb luck, have become ageless or worshiped for various reasons.
There is extreme variety between ‚Äúgods‚Äù. The most potent are those who learn to wield human institutions in their name. Many kings claim divinity & thereby reap the rewards of power.
Godheads
Some creatures form cults that build shrines & temples. Unlike a government, dedicated cult activity creates a phenomenon called the Godhead, a subconscious mass of faith & power attached to a spirit‚Äôs existence like a spiritual exoskeleton.
The main practical benefit of a Godhead is that it lets people perform divination (p. @) to poll a god for small requests, such as yes/no answers to whether a certain act would please them, to reinforce an oath with the threat of a curse upon the violator, or for minor blessings within their power, among other things.
The average effect of miracles & blessings upon the population is not displayed mechanically in the Faction system because everyone is assumed to perform the sacrifices & rituals needed to maintain a decent relationship with the gods that matter the most to their life.
Domain
A god‚Äôs Domain is the sum total of correlations related to the god‚Äôs Facts, Features, cult presence or general existence; in short, it‚Äôs their knowledge & magical power.
A local beast‚Äôs Domain includes the forest it lives in. It doesn‚Äôt include forests in general unless that beast has some special characteristic that attunes it to all forests.
Limitations of a Godhead
A Godhead reflects a spirit‚Äôs attitude & domains of power, but not its conscious awareness. A ‚Äúgod‚Äù worshiped in this manner is no more immortal, immaterial, or possessing of attention than a mortal. A person cannot ‚Äúsee‚Äù through a Godhead formed around them any more than a CEO knows the day-to-day workings of his clerks ‚Äî only if it‚Äôs personally brought to his attention can he know.
Divination
Divination is a ritual developed to derive an answer to an inquiry from an entity‚Äôs Godhead. The Godhead of a worshiped entity is capable only of very subtle, general shifts & effects across the entity‚Äôs domain, so responses are usually issued through minor fluctuations in the environment. A cult at its outset creates a specific divination method to contact a given spirit; repetition & tradition reinforces that method. Common methods of divination include (but are not limited to):

Observing the flocks & rhythms of birds (augury);
Reading the qualities of corpses, such as bones or organs (necromancy & haruspicy);
Observing the movements of stars;
Burning bones & observing fracture patterns;
Observing the movement & quality of flames (pyromancy);
Reading the movement & shape of leaves in prepared tea (tasseography);
Casting dice in large quantities.

To perform divination, you must loudly state the true name of the entity you are trying to contact, the sacrifice you‚Äôre offering, & what you expect as a blessing. The following can be demanded:

The entity‚Äôs blessing (p. @) as a temporary Condition.

Alternatively, one can demand a minor coincidental miracle that a Blessing could achieve, such as commanding a local minor animal to perform an act.


A yes/no answer to whether a specific action would please the entity.
That the entity cancel a curse placed upon you by it.
That the entity appear before you (a summoning), possibly to perform a specific in-person service (such as to serve as a guide).

All requests must fall within the god‚Äôs Domain.
You must then perform the sacrificial rites, using the sacrifice in the act of divination required by the entity. Afterwards, the GM rolls you a Divination Save, in secret, with the following modifiers:

Add the parishioner‚Äôs Level, minus the entity‚Äôs Power.
+1 for each digit in the value of the sacrifice presently offered or promised in the future, up to +5 maximum.

‚àí2 if no sacrifice or vow for a future payment is offered.


+1 if performing at a shrine or temple dedicated to this god.
+1 if performing with the assistance of a priest dedicated to this god.
‚àí5 if outside the realm where the entity operates, measured as (Power √ó 10 in miles) from the entity itself or the nearest Settlement holding a cult to it.






























ResultDivination Save EffectsCrit‚â•12If the entity is within 1 hour‚Äôs journey, an omen appears & it personally shows up to offer advice or support. Otherwise, the Godhead answers yes or no to the request/petition according to the spirit‚Äôs interest, with some special insight into the circumstances or likely outcomes.Pass8‚Äì11The Godhead answers yes or no to the request/petition according to the spirit‚Äôs interests, with some special insight into the circumstances or likely outcomes.Seven7For inquiries, the Godhead answers yes or no according to the spirit‚Äôs interests, with no special foresight or knowledge into the situation, based entirely on whether it sounds good as described by the petitioner. All other requests are ignored.Failure3‚Äì6For inquiries, the Godhead answers yes on an odd Result & no on an even Result. All other requests are ignored.Fumble‚â§2For inquiries, the Godhead answers yes on an odd Result & no on an even Result. All other requests are ignored.
A Godhead is not an extension of the entity‚Äôs conscious mind. It is a swirling miasma of power that answers according to its will, but weakly. An entity is unaware of what its Godhead is doing & can only shape its actions by shaping its own ideas of what is or isn‚Äôt good.
Local demigods, spirits, magicians, & masters are often preferred over distant ones because they personally attend their own shrines & generally offer better, more direct support than distant gods do.
Divination with a Present Entity
Divination is only for talking to a Godhead. If the god is standing right in front of you, you can just talk to & negotiate with it the way you would a human of similar stature (i.e. presumably with much nervousness & terror).
An offering or exchange is still usually required & popular gods are likely to be busy. See Negotiation (p. @).
Blessings
A god‚Äôs blessing is a Condition that can be expended at any time to gain a +1 bonus on any Save or on other actions related to the god‚Äôs Domain.
A Player may also ask a god for a blessing to be expended on a small favor or act within its domain explainable by circumstance or happenstance. If the GM believes it plausible, the blessing is expended & the favor occurs.
Only one blessing may be held at a time.
Curses
A god‚Äôs curse is generally a negative Condition that sticks around until divination is successfully performed to ask for its removal. It penalizes any Save made by that Leader for actions related to the god‚Äôs Facts.
Familiars
A familiar is a fancy word for a beast of power that has chosen to follow you around. It is functionally a Retainer (p. @) & can be achieved simply by persuading an interesting creature to follow you, possibly under an oath overseen by a local god.
@ Enchanted Items
Enchanted items (or ‚Äúmagic items‚Äù) are items with an active spirit attached. They are not meaningfully distinguishable from any other item ‚Äî after all, water has a spirit too.
Totems Among the Living
Most people are untrained in the usage of Totems & are prone to angering the spirits inside, & their benefit is of niche utility. As a result, most people view them with suspicion & fear; their usage is mostly restrained to magicians who spend time practicing with spirits.
@ Loose Spells

Book-1---Glass-Empires/Part-1---Rules/{-.title: { 6 } - Marketplace
Book-1---Glass-Empires/Part-1---Rules/{-.content:
A marketplace is a gathering of people offering goods & services within a given domain. Every populated Settlement has a marketplace with its own prices & availability of goods. When you buy from a marketplace, you‚Äôre buying from the Settlement.

@ Debt & Credit
Debts
A Debt is a Condition (a temporary Fact, p.@) assigned a Value, whether monetary or intangible (i.e. blood debts, ‚Äúhe murdered my relative‚Äù). It represents what you owe to a creditor ‚Äî the person you took from. Both the nature of the Debt & the creditor must be described in the Debt‚Äôs text; for example:

Debt to [creditor] - [value of debt]

So, Debt to Baker - gift of dozen loaves $0.3 is a valid Debt meaning a Debt worth $0.3. You might shorten it to Debt to Baker $0.3, but story helps memory.
Or, if you wanted to note the debt on the creditor‚Äôs side:

Credit to [debtor] - [value of credit]

Debts can be one-sided & may be emotional, not just financial. If you murder a person‚Äôs servant, they may unilaterally mark your action as a Debt you owe to them, even if you are indifferent. (This is in part the source of feuds.)
Debts, like Facts, may be used to sway or influence Saves over which their existence would hold influence. For instance, the existence of Debt may impact your ability to fight off foreign Sabotage efforts, raise Armies, develop new assets, or other things dependent on financial health.
@ Loans
A loan is an act of giving that creates a Debt. The terms of a loan are subject to negotiation (p. @).
A loan is a trust issue. Loans are taken out against your estimated future ability to repay. That may be measured via your Families (if you rule a Faction) or against past records of success in business ventures (if you have any) or against your personal relationship with the financier.
Normal Factions can only ‚Äúloan‚Äù by giving a direct sum in $ to a character & can only maintain 1 cash loan at a time. They also have to manually mark a loan down.
Merchant & Financier Factions (p. @) can issue substantially larger sums; 10√ó Followers & unlimited respectively.
Calling a Debt in
When a creditor calls in a Debt, they declare a demand that would satisfy the Debt. In theory, creditors can demand anything. In practice, they are constrained by Negotiation (p. @) & perceived value. When the demand is issued, the debtor must choose:

Accept: Fulfill the demand. The Debt is erased (or reduced by the agreed Value, if not fully paid off).
Negotiate: Attempt to negotiate (p. @) with the creditor.
Default: The Debt remains, but its wording is adjusted to indicate it has been violated with a reason as to why. This may Anger (p. @) the creditor.

Creditors may also forgive a Debt. Beside repayment, forgiveness is the only way to eliminate a Debt. Forgiveness may Anger those who stood to benefit from the repayment.
Violation of Debts
If you violate the terms of a Debt (i.e. by defaulting ‚Äî canceling the agreed-upon terms), the Debt remains as a Fact on your sheet until every participant who could possibly call upon that Debt is dead ‚Äî but reword it to reflect your refusal to pay that Debt.
Trading debt
Debts have Value, and therefore you can trade a Debt that a debtor owes you to another person if they think the Debt owed is of value to them.
Debt Bondage
Debtors who cannot pay off their debts may become a slave so that their labor over a period may pay them off. This is the basis of Glass‚Äôs system of indentured servitude. Traditionally, one day‚Äôs unskilled labor is worth $1, minus cost of living.
Informers, Bounty Hunters, & Collections Agents
The traditional solution to a rogue debtor, particularly one on the run, is to offer a percentage of a debt or an unrelated reward for the debtor‚Äôs capture or execution, or for the recovery of assets associated with the debt.
Bounty hunters don‚Äôt always employ lethal force to capture targets, especially if the target is a large Faction of some complexity whose assets cannot be easily hidden. Instead, sophisticated bounty hunters hunting wealthy or powerful targets will use organizations (their own or their friends‚Äô) to find complex financial, legal, social, or blackmail-based pressure points that can quietly force even kings into compliance under threat of greater harm.

@ We could use charts of what kind of bounty hunters are attracted to what magnitudes of debts. Maybe tie it into income calculations & Level.
It should be approximately equal to the payment/interest rate agreed upon, or a fixed value of the Debt. Like, 10%? Per month? 3%?

Using This Chapter
Every RPG book has a price list. Normally, it doesn‚Äôt make much sense. Lots of the prices are cargo-cult listings copied down from many eras of history; some of the items have no apparent use in the game.
For Glass Empires, the situation is a bit worse. Faction Leaders are assumed to have a stable income & many items can be bought using a Faction‚Äôs influence rather than out-of-pocket with discrete coin.
Use this chapter to determine prices for large bulk purchases or for low-level play where characters manage only a couple hundred silver dollars each. Don‚Äôt use it to bicker over exact pricing for small purchases, especially when a character rules a Faction of such scale that obtaining small items is a trivial act.
Availability & Pricing
Item Availability by Price
If it‚Äôs necessary to determine the quantity of an item that can be bought in a given Settlement, divide 5 times the Settlement‚Äôs Families by the item‚Äôs Value. The result, rounded down, is how many can be found. If the result is less than 1, the GM may at his discretion set an X-in-6 chance that a single item can be found this month; otherwise, none are available.
Item Availability by Commission
Regardless of how many are available to purchase outright, items can also be commissioned from capable craftsmen. The average craftsman produces $50 worth of goods per day.
Weapon Categories
Weapons are categorized by hands occupied (1H vs. 2H) & Weight. Each category can represent a wide variety of weapons, including shields.

Gear Effects

Hand-and-a-half: If wielded with two hands, gain +1 Damage. This can represent an arming sword being wielded in two hands, or a shield that‚Äôs optionally storable.
Range: Weapon is a ranged weapon with the listed distance as its maximum reach.
Reach: Melee reach is doubled while measuring in Skirmish combat.
Reload: User must take a Reload Action after every shot in Skirmish combat.
Sling: You need a 15‚Äô diameter, 10‚Äô tall cylinder around yourself to be clear in order to use a sling.
Melee: This weapon can be dual-wielded as a half of a Medium Melee weapon category pair (increasing Damage by 1 in Melee only).



                  
                  What makes crossbows good? 
                  
                

They‚Äôre easy to train with & inexpensive, which is mostly irrelevant for PCs & in danger of being supplanted by the relatively new, equally trainable & far more lethal windgun.




                  
                  Why are shields weapons? 
                  
                

Simplification & fun. Rather than making one pairing of weapon + shield obviously superior, you can design a weapon of a given category to be many hypothetical weapon combinations. Besides, you wouldn‚Äôt like it if due to a dumb quirk of the math, a shortsword + shield was the only logical combination, would you?
That said, if you get a magical shield, you can feasibly swap it into a Medium Melee combo or pair it with a Light Melee weapon (& subsequently boost the Light Melee weapon‚Äôs Damage by +2 while wielding a shield so it‚Äôs on par with a Medium Melee combo).


Armor
Worn armor grants Armor (p. @), which provides ablative Armor Points (AP).

Barding, specialized armor for animals, is priced & weighted based on the size of the animal in question.

Magician‚Äôs Clothes
Properly engineered robes & equipment can substantially increase magical power. If worn from the point of waking onward, these robes increase their wearer‚Äôs Spell Dice (p. @) by the amount listed. When removed, the caster immediately loses that much SD, starting with any remaining unexhausted capacity.

Retainers

Number Available
To find out how many characters of a certain Level (# of Class Features) are available for hire in a Settlement per season, use the table below.

The vast majority of Glassians are agricultural laborers, but cities & settlements are disproportionately specialized. In a Settlement, of those available to hire, approximately:

Monthly Wages
A Retainer hired for monthly wages will expect wages measured by how many Class Features they have or by their Battle Rating score (p. @), depending on the purpose of their employment (specialist labor vs. military force).
Wages by Level/Class Features
Generally speaking, you cannot hire anyone over the rank of Level 4, but this table goes to Level 14 for completeness‚Äô sake. Mercenaries charge $10 per BR per month & usually come as a packaged unit consisting of at least a lance ‚Äî a 3-man fire-squad consisting of a swordsman, archer (or windgunner), & specialist.
An average human mercenary is 4.5 BR ‚Äî a Level 0 spearman with a shield & chain armor. That‚Äôs $45/month.
