Book-1---Glass-Empires/Part-1---Rules/{-.content:
@ This playmode is applicable to when you‚Äôre adjudicating the adventure & movement of a handful of characters through dangerous environments.

tldr: everything in this section is specifically for ‚Äúhow does [my character] interact with [the world] in a way that makes sense?‚Äù. it‚Äôs for resolving basic low-level tensions. it‚Äôs VERY STANDARD and covered in almost every RPG, and it is therefore in need of being SEPARATED by a GREAT WALL from our more interesting stuff about Factions. @@@

@ Encumbrance
10lb is 1 stone (1#). Everything is weighed in stone.
Characters have a Max. Load indicating how many stone they can carry safely (assuming they have storage for those goods). Each hand can hold up to 1# independently from your Max. Load. The impact of encumbrance depends on how much of a character‚Äôs Max. Load is occupied:

Up to half (rounded up): No effect.
Up to full: Suffer the Encumbered Condition.
More than full: You cannot usefully move.

Characters can Save to deadlift up to twice their Max. Load for up to 1 Round (10 seconds), after which they must Save again.
Small Items: Most small items weighing less than 1# but more than 2lb weigh ¬º#. Smaller items are weightless. Items sold as bundles in the marketplace weigh their listed weight for a bundle of that quantity.
Item Slots & Cards: On your sheet, Max. Load is represented by 1¬Ω‚Äù item slots on the edge of the sheet. 1 slot is 1#. Items weighing ¬º# can be stacked up to 4 per slot. Print out item cards & attach them with paperclips, or just write in the spaces with a pencil.
Encumbrance Condition
While Encumbered, characters halve their effective Speed scores while measuring long-distance travel. Short-term movement rates such as in combat are not affected.
In addition, being Encumbered is a ‚àí1 penalty on Saves made for dexterity or agility. This does not include Strike Saves made for Attacks.
@ Move: Foresight
PCs are generally assumed to be competent & to also notice a variety of details & niche planning that their players may not have had the time or focus to realize. For this reason, when presented with challenges or obstacles that weren‚Äôt explicitly foreseen but that may have potentially come up in off-screen planning or logistical actions, players may assert foresight by stating an action their PC made in advance to deal with the situation & making a Save to determine if their PC was active, wise, or perceptive enough to have done so.
The criteria for foresight is as follows:

Doesn‚Äôt require knowing anything the PCs had absolutely no way of knowing.
Doesn‚Äôt require things or events that are absolutely impossible or that contradict anything that‚Äôs already been established within the game world.
No other PC has attempted to have foresight over this specific obstacle.

You may receive a penalty on your Save depending on how complex & unlikely the action you want to take was, which fills in for the ordinary situational penalties a GM may apply on a case-by-case basis:

No penalty: Ordinary action which you had ample opportunity to perform.

Was the action trivial to perform?
If it wasn‚Äôt trivial to perform, was it at least cheap & the benefits foreseeable?


‚àí2 penalty: Complex action or unlikely opportunities.

Does the plan have several moving parts?
Does it rely on additional events which weren‚Äôt guaranteed?
Did the preparation rely on Retainers or other servants with an incomplete grasp of the motives at play, or a reason to interfere?


‚àí4 penalty: Exceptionally elaborate, specialized, or implausible actions or research.

Does it rely on unlikely facts about the world that are unestablished or uncorroborated ‚Äî facts that are made up on the spot by the player?
Did the players have at best a very tenuous opportunity to acquire information critical to the foresight?
Secretly, by GM‚Äôs judgement: Is the Player relying on an entity that wanted to betray or sabotage them & that would have seen this sabotage as worth the risk?



While most normal situational penalties are wrapped up in this scale, characters may still add various bonuses from their Facts and other relevant modifiers to the Save.

























ResultForesight Save EffectCrit & Pass8‚Äì11You planned in advance what you needed to overcome this specific obstacle more or less as stated.Seven7You have something similar to but insufficient for the task at hand, originally intended for a different contingency ‚Äî at least enough for a +1 bonus to a Save, if that‚Äôs what you‚Äôre trying to overcome. If no partial preparation is obviously possible, you suffer a Failure instead.Failure3‚Äì6You failed to have foresight & have nothing special prepared.Fumble‚â§2You were catastrophically wrong in your efforts. The situation at hand is as bad as the worst thing the GM can think of given the preparation you claimed to have done.
Conditions
Conditions are temporary Facts that affect characters or objects in various ways. Most are harmful, but some can be helpful in certain situations. Conditions last until narratively countered (i.e. being Prone is countered by taking the Stand Up Action) or until a specified duration elapses.
Generally, Conditions behave like Facts with special rules attached. They can be invoked by players & GMs for ¬±1 modifiers on applicable Saves.
Characters cannot suffer more than one Condition of the same type at once. If they would & the new duration is longer than the current one, the duration of their existing Condition is extended to meet it.
Damage, Incapacitation & Death
Characters lose 1 HP per point of Damage dealt to them. If a character is reduced to less than 0 HP, the character is Incapacitated. (Characters can have infinite negative HP.)
Incapacitated characters are unconscious & essentially useless.
When a character becomes Incapacitated or has just lost additional HP while already Incapacitated, if their current negative HP exceeds their Power, they die. Otherwise, they receive a Wound (p. @).
If an Incapacitated character is left alone for an hour, he may Save to revive himself. If he fails, he dies. If a character arrives to assist him before that hour is up, he may add a +2 bonus to his Save for being assisted & may also borrow the assistant‚Äôs Facts (i.e. Lessons pertaining to being a doctor, etc.) while making his Save.
Funerals
The survivors of characters who die may have a Jester (p. @) host a funeral (p. @) to alert a psychopomp to the dead soul in need of ferrying.
Armor & Armor Points
Armor is a score (usually from 1 to 4) granted by equipment or Features that lets a character gain Armor Points (hereafter AP), which is essentially a backup pool of HP.
If a character has AP & takes Damage, they may deplete their AP before subtracting Damage from their HP. If you run out of AP while absorbing a blow, the remaining Damage spills over to & is subtracted from your HP.
The amount of AP granted by Armor is variable, because Armor is a multiplier of Power (which is HD + Level). If you have 4 Armor and 8 Power (i.e. if you‚Äôre a HD 2 human at Lv6), your Armor will grant you 4 √ó 8 = 32 AP. In practical terms, with 4 HP being typical per HD for most cases, a full set of plate armor will double your effective HP.
Characters can have multiple Armor scores from many sources, but only the best is ever used for determining how much AP is gained. AP is reset to its maximum value whenever a character Rests (p. @).
Healing AP
Whenever you rest, you also heal AP. See the Resting Move (p. @) for details.
Removing Armor
If you remove equipment that‚Äôs giving you an Armor score, you must reduce your AP to your next highest Armor score (if any) times your Power. After you put on equipment with an Armor score, you must heal HP from resting in order to reset your AP.
Damage Types
Damage may have 1 or more Types. The 7 Types are:

Acid: Corrosive burns.
Electric: Powerful electrical currents.
Ice: Hypothermic exposure.
Fire: Hyperthermic exposure.
Mind: Psychic, mental & emotional trauma.
Physical: Slashing, puncturing, & blunt-force trauma damage.
Poison: Internal toxins.

If no Damage Type is specified, it is said to be Typeless Damage.
Resistances, Weaknesses, & Immunities
Characters may be Weak, Resistant, or Immune to certain Damage Types.

Resistance halves all Damage of that Type, rounded down.
Weakness doubles all Damage of that Type.
Immunity neutralizes all Damage of a given Type.

If a source of Damage has more than 1 Type (designated with a slash, i.e. ‚Äú6 Ice/Fire Damage‚Äù), the weakest resistance status is always used.

For example, if a character took 6 Ice/Fire Damage & had Ice Resistance while being Weak to Fire, the character would take 12 Fire Damage (instead of taking 3 Ice Damage).

Typeless Damage
Typeless Damage cannot be resisted.
Sociology
Mood
NPCs can only be Friendly, Neutral, or Hostile towards any given actor, including Factions such as the Party, or individual PCs. You don‚Äôt have to write down every NPC‚Äôs mood towards a given character if it‚Äôs obvious enough to remember.
Shifting Moods
When a character is Angered or Pleased, they move 1 step up or down on the scale if it‚Äôs appropriate in response to the trigger. In addition, if they‚Äôre managing a Move (i.e. if they‚Äôre Leaders of a Faction), they‚Äôll also reconsider their priorities in light of whatever new information they‚Äôve received & may adjust their Moves or other actions in response to it.
Characters who are already Friendly or Hostile when they become Pleased or Angered (respectively) will instead take action (decided by the GM) in response to the event.
Move: Persuasion
Persuasion is the act of convincing someone that something is true (regardless of whether it is or isn‚Äôt). When you try to persuade a target of something meaningful, consider:

Consistency: Does the information jarringly contradict anything the target already believes?

Is it immediately & obviously false based on currently observable data?
Does it upset/disrupt their existing worldview, life context, history, or biases?
Do they believe you would be likely to lie about this?


Incentive: Does the target have an incentive proportionate to the scale of the claim to believe you?

Is the target‚Äôs life made easier (or prevented from getting worse) by believing you?
Does the target like you?
Do they believe you to be an authority on the topic?





                  
                  But I have reasonable evidence! 
                  
                

Evidence is not an incentive to believe you unless you have constructed it in such a way as to confirm the target‚Äôs belief systems. Rigorous data or logical arguments are only valuable if the target‚Äôs belief systems explicitly prioritize it, and not all worldviews prize all forms of evidence the same way ‚Äî ask Galileo Galilei how evidence worked out for him.
People on Aia are not rational. They don‚Äôt understand the human mind. They don‚Äôt understand cognition or belief or what a bias or fallacy is. They care about association, group behavior, & hierarchical authority. If you want to speak to them, speak to their existing beliefs.


If both are true, the target will believe you. If both are false, the target will never believe you. If only one is true, a Save is required:





























ResultPersuasion Save EffectsCrit‚â•12Target wholly accepts your information & alters or synthesizes their worldview to adapt to it. This may cause a radical or unpredictable realignment.Pass8‚Äì11Target accepts your specific point, but works it into their existing worldview (i.e. as an exception or oddity) & will not strongly/openly espouse it or any downstream consequences.Seven7Target demands additional evidence (or action on your part) beyond what you‚Äôve already provided. If not provided, see Failure.Failure3‚Äì6The target doesn‚Äôt believe you. The GM rolls for a Disbelief penalty.Fumble‚â§2The target doesn‚Äôt believe you. The GM rolls 3 Disbelief penalties.
Disbelief Penalties
If the rolled consequence is irrelevant for the case, the GM may discard it & pick the most appropriate consequence given the circumstances.

































1d6Disbelief Penalties1The target is Angered.2The target takes the most immediate possible action against you as retribution, assuming they hold any power to do so as even a symbolic gesture.3The target (at the nearest appropriate time given the nature of the violation) calls in all of their like-minded allies (if any) in a collective action against you. The action may manifest as concerned questioning, immediate physical assault, public slander or attack, an attempted arrest, a rigorous examination of your assertions, etc. depending on the assertion & your relationship to the target.4If it‚Äôs a reasonable venture to do so, the target will begin plotting to prove your assertion wrong ‚Äî or, if they value truth, they will attempt to uncover it. If they‚Äôre your enemy, they‚Äôll bring your assertion to the people most likely to be upset by & act on it (alongside them, if possible). If doing anything is beyond their resources, the target does nothing.5The target (quietly or otherwise) no longer believes you‚Äôre an authority on the topic. If they didn‚Äôt believe this, they now believe you‚Äôre a liar/fraud.6No consequence, other than the target taking note of the incident for later.
Move: Negotiation
Intimidation, relationship-building, seduction, & salesmanship are all forms of negotiation. They are defined by an exchange with a target of something you have for something you want. Consider:

Value: Does the target believe what you‚Äôre offering provides sufficient value relative to what you‚Äôre asking, given their current priorities and available alternatives?

Is your offer noticeably better/worse than alternatives?
If you‚Äôre intimidating the target, do they believe that the demand is worse than the harm you‚Äôll inflict?


Trust: Does the target believe you can & will fulfill your commitments?

Is there clear means & motive by which you might violate the agreement?
Do you have the means & motive to carry out a threat?
Do you have a proven record of trustworthiness and/or obvious means by which you would suffer for failing to fulfill the deal?



If both are true, the target will accept your offer. If neither are true, the target will refuse. If only one is true, a Save is required:





























ResultNegotiation Save EffectsCrit‚â•12Target accepts your offer enthusiastically & becomes invested in your success. They offer additional value, recommend you to others, actively advocate for you, or improve their opinion of you by one step, based on which the GM believes is most appropriate.Pass8‚Äì11The target accepts your offer.Seven7The target makes a counter-offer, either asking for more or wanting to give less. If refused, repeated Saves for negotiating this deal are made with an additional ‚àí1 penalty.Failure3‚Äì6The target rejects your proposal & won‚Äôt listen to additional offers on this matter until something radical changes.Fumble‚â§2The target feels insulted. They will demand an act of apology to restore their honor; if refused, their relationship decays one step (friendly ‚Üí neutral ‚Üí hostile). Where appropriate, targets may also warn others (if neutral) or attack/flee (if hostile).
Move: Resting
Resting is an extended Move performable by both individual characters & whole Factions. It is the primary way of healing.
When you choose to Rest, your character (or Faction) remains idle (performing no strenuous physical or mental activity) until the end of the intended period or until interrupted by an intruding force. Generally, this will involve a timeskip.
Duration
The Deadline is set by the group Leader as the intended date of resuming activity.
On the Date
If the resting period was at least an hour, then at the end, for each involved character, count how many of the following conditions were true for the period:

No Wounds.
Good food & drink available (travel rations don‚Äôt count, but monsters cooked fresh do).
Rested for 8 hours or more (i.e. overnight) in a bed (sleeping bags count).

If this condition is met, you may also restore all exhausted Spell Dice (p. @).


Rested for a day or more.
Rested for a week or more.
Rested for a month or more.

Take the number of true conditions & multiply it by [Power]. If the result is greater than the character‚Äôs current HP, the character‚Äôs HP is increased to match the result.
Repairing Armor Points
When you heal any amount of HP by resting, you may reset your AP to be equal to your best Armor times your Power, up to at most as much HP was healed from the rest.
Movement
Rough Terrain
Stairs & hills steeper than 45¬∞ are Rough Terrain & halve your Run score. The GM may also rule certain other kinds of terrain (such as tightropes, which may require a Save to cross successfully, or narrow cracks) as being Rough Terrain due to difficulty of navigation.
Rough Terrain usually doesn‚Äôt matter unless you‚Äôre in a Skirmish (p. @) or are very pressed for time.
Climbing
Ladders & Ropes
Ladders, hand-holds & other implements made to be climbed can be climbed at a speed no greater than one quarter your Run score.
Free Climbing
You may Save to travel up a rough surface steeper than 60¬∞ to a point that you can see. For each additional 10¬∞ of slope (including if it becomes an overhang), add a ‚àí1 penalty. Add a bonus for the time spent studying the route:

‚â•1 minute: +1
‚â•10 minutes: +2
‚â•1 hour: +3

Climbing is too slow for combat if you lack a Feature that grants climbing ability.

























ResultClimbing Save EffectPass & Crit‚â•8You reach the point you‚Äôre climbing to safely.Seven7Fall if you cannot reach the top by the end of your movement at your chosen pace.Failure3‚Äì6Fall at the middle of your climb.Fumble‚â§2Fall just before reaching the top of your climb.
If your climb is longer than you can see (i.e. due to light source range), you must Save repeatedly.
Falling Damage
When a character falls, determine the distance fallen.

500‚Äô or less: You may not take any actions excepting those that are essentially instantaneous, & take ([Weight] + your carried equipment‚Äôs weight in #) Physical Damage upon striking the ground, capped to at most the distance in feet you fell (if you fall 3‚Äô, you can‚Äôt take more than 3 Physical Damage).
Greater than 500‚Äô: You take ([Weight] + your carried equipment‚Äôs weight in #) √ó 5 Physical Damage upon striking the ground & may take 1 Major Action during your fall for each complete 500 feet that you fall.

Water reduces fall damage by 5. Cushions (such as hay) reduce fall damage by 10.
If yo or an item lands on a character, they take the same amount of damage.
Catching an Idiot
Always succeeds, if you‚Äôre in a position to do so. Split Damage equally between everyone in a position to help.
Condition: Prone
Being on the ground is called being Prone, and this Condition covers crawling, sitting, & any other form of limited movement. A character‚Äôs effective Speed scores (& height) is quartered while Prone.
The Prone Condition counts as a Fact for any Save where it‚Äôs relevant (i.e. prone characters have a hard time defending against melee strikes, but an easier time against long-distance ranged attacks).
Jumping
In tense situations, you may make a Save to attempt a jump. Gain a +2 bonus if you spend at least half your Run score on a straight running start.





























ResultJump Save EffectsCrit‚â•12Travel up to 1/2 [Run] distance.Pass8‚Äì11Travel up to 1/3 [Run] distance.Seven7Travel up to 1/4 [Run] distance.Failure3‚Äì6Travel up to 1/5 [Run] distance.Fumble‚â§2You trip & fall immediately.
If your intended destination is less than your actual distance traveled, you fall at the furthest point you manage.
High Jumps
Measuring from the ground where you jump, you can reach up to twice your Size for a pull-up grab. You may also perform a vault over an object of up to your Size if you treat the vertical height of the object as distance traveled for movement‚Äôs sake.
Rounding Speed Scores
Always round Speed scores (whenever divided) to the nearest 5‚Äô increment.
Tidbits
These are pieces of trivia that have occasionally proved useful for calculating rulings.
Excavation
Characters can move cubic feet of earth equal to their Max. Load in # per hour, assuming appropriate tools are possessed. The rate is halved for improvised tools, and quartered for a lack of them. Dressed stone brick takes twice as long to excavate.
Making Rations
Creatures produce ¬º# in rations per 1# of weight when skinned, smoked, & salted. Salting consumes ¬º# of salt per 1# of rations.
Move: Travel
Travel by a Party or mobile Factions (such as Armies) is resolved via free movement tracked on a map of the campaign world by the GM.
In order to Travel, you must have a route & destination in mind. The route may be a path or a direction. The destination can be a literal place or a time-based trigger such as ‚Äúthree days north‚Äù. Announce both to the GM.
Travel Procedure

The GM determines if the route can actually take you to the destination.
The GM measures out the path to the destination & determines the total distance that either must be traveled (to reach a target place). or that will be traveled (to travel for a certain period of time).

If the party is following an erroneous route, the destination is the distance that the Party would have to travel before obviously realizing something is wrong (i.e. encountering an obviously out-of-the-way landmark or passing enough time to have reached the destination if on the correct course).


The GM determines if there‚Äôs possible overlap with any other Faction movement based on relative Speeds & routes taken. If there appears to be a point of encounter, the GM (secretly) alters the destination to instead be the encounter & recalculates travel distance & time.
The GM sets a (secret) Deadline as the time at which the destination will be reached.
For each week of travel (or at the midpoint, if less than one), the GM calls for PCs in a traveling party to check for an Exhaustion Save if necessary & simultaneously rolls on the Discoveries table.
When the Deadline for a Travel Move to finish comes up, the GM rolls a Travel Save for the party.

Exhaustion Save
Each week, each PC in a party should check if any of the following conditions are true:

A PC or their followers are carrying less than 2# of supplies each & are also not in a farmland, pastures, or otherwise settled region.

None at all.


The terrain is barrens/wasteland where food & water cannot be found easily.
Your mounts are unfit for the biome (horses in the desert, camels in the arctic).

For each true condition, each affected PC in the Party must perform an Exhaustion Save. Add leadership, quartermastery & hunting skills as +1 bonuses.





























ResultExhaustion Save EffectsCrit‚â•12You can bump the Result of 1 other PC in the Party who failed up by 1 step ‚Äî i.e. from a Fumble to a Failure.Pass8‚Äì11Nothing bad happens.Seven7Lose 10% of all nameless Followers traveling with you, rounded down. Lose [Power] HP.Failure3‚Äì6Lose 33% of all nameless Followers traveling with you, rounded down. Half of them are dead; the other half deserted. For each named Retainer or Follower, roll 1d6. On 1, they‚Äôre dead; on 2, they deserted. Lose [Power] √ó 2 HP.Fumble‚â§2Lose 50% of all nameless Followers traveling with you, rounded down. Two thirds of them are dead; the other third deserted. For each named Retainer or Follower, roll 1d6. On 1‚Äì2, they‚Äôre dead; on 3, they deserted. Lose [Power] √ó 3 HP.


                  
                  What if not all NPCs in the group are Followers of a PC? 
                  
                

If some members of the group are not Followers of any PC, one PC must on their Save treat them as their Followers for the sake of determining their fates, so as to prevent them from being immune to exhaustion or starvation.




                  
                  But I want food & water to matter on a general basis. 
                  
                

And it does. With 20lb of gear dedicated towards survival, people & animals have enough in excess capacity & storage to escape starvation & malnutrition 95% of the time. Instead of slowing down the game by explicitly modeling this, we just ask if you have that equipment & if you don‚Äôt (or if you‚Äôre in a desert where things are harsher), we punish you based on the rate of starvation you‚Äôd see versus your PC‚Äôs leadership & survival skills.
This may not be to your preference, but God forbid we make people do the rocket equation for equipping an army every time they wanted to go somewhere. I felt that this was beneath the scope of the game. It‚Äôs actually the same reason we don‚Äôt generally track discrete quantities of cash. When you‚Äôre rich & powerful, you rely more on the heuristics of cashflow versus expenditures, not on nickels & dimes; penny-pinching is for the poor.


Discoveries Table
The GM rolls once on the Discoveries table per week (or part thereof) that a Party spends traveling.

























1d6Discoveries1‚Äì2Encounter. An encounter with another traveler, travelers, or local Monsters. There is a 2-in-6 chance it is actually a Lair (mark it on the map as a Location), unless this Route already passes by a Lair.3‚Äì4Location. A new formerly unmarked Location is discovered. If the determined Location closely matches the identity of a Location that‚Äôs already marked along the Route (i.e. if you roll a tower, & there‚Äôs a castle on the way), there‚Äôs no new Location; it‚Äôs the existing one, instead.5Omen. Roll for 2 Encounters & determine the most likely thing that would happen if the two of them met. Traces of that outcome are discovered by the Party. A pointer indicating the identity of the first Encounter rolled remains (i.e. tattered banner, dead body, viscous slime, dropped letter, observant/curious local, paw-print) provided that anyone in the Party would recognize what it meant.6Nothing.
In geographic terms, each Discovery is spaced out more or less evenly across the route.
The GM‚Äôs Guide has instructions on how to create & manage tables of Encounters & Locations, as well as generators to help produce both.
Measuring distance & time taken
Characters can travel ~1 mile per day across a medium for each 1‚Äô of Speed score they possess. A human with a Run of 25‚Äô can travel 25 miles per day. A bird with a Fly of 40‚Äô can travel 40 miles per day.
The GM is recommended to use a measuring tool alongside a world map to track the actual location of each Party on the move, as well as to determine distances traveled.
The table of multipliers below is used for determining the effective distance traveled when moving through rough/obstructed terrain.








































TypeExamplesDistance MultiplierClear/roadPlains, grasslands, farmland, highways & roads√ó1RoughHills, desert, marsh, forest√ó2HeavyDunes, forested/snowy hills, swamp√ó3MountainousMountain√ó4ExtremeForested/snowy mountain√ó5RiverAdds +5 miles of the same terrain type it‚Äôs located in+5mi
Divide the effective distance in miles by the slowest Speed score among all traveling Party members. Remember: If any part of the route is off-road, the Speed of all wheeled vehicles is halved & if any Party member is Encumbered, their Speed is halved too.
The result is the number of days until the Deadline (at which point the Party reaches the destination).


                  
                  What about super short distances/high speeds? 
                  
                

If your Deadline before rounding is less than 1 day, you probably should have realized that instead of calculating this out ‚Äî but in any case, feel free to use the value in hours if they‚Äôre for some reason critical for the game.


Visibility
Aia is a flat disc & Glass is not an exception. Your view is not limited by the curvature of the Earth. Atmospheric scattering, weather, and terrain irregularities are the primary limiter of vision. Aia has a thick atmosphere, but it isn‚Äôt that thick.
The basic determinant of view distance is observer height.



































Observer PositionBase VisibilityDistinguishable at this distanceFlat ground5 milesIndividuals, buildingsWatchtower10 milesFormations, buildings, ship typesHills20 milesArmies, towns, fleets, castlesMountains40 milesArmies, cities, major terrainPeak80 milesOnly the largest features
Multiply by the following (all armies carry telescopes):

Hazy: √ó0.5
Foggy: √ó0.25
No telescope: √ó0.5

Advancement
When one of the Factions (p. @) you‚Äôre the Leader of or a cult Faction dedicated to you grows in Families past the minimum Families threshold required to support a Leader of the next Level up, AND you do not yet have as many Class Features as your highest Faction Level, you can pick a Class from those you qualify for (as defined by what you have done recently & what Factions you currently lead) to gain a Class Feature from according to that Class‚Äôs method.
There are two methods of qualifying for a Class:

Do something heroic since the last time you gained a Level that unarguably reflects the nature of that Class.
The Faction that gained enough Followers to qualify you for the next Level has a government type (p. @) that explicitly qualifies you for that Class.

Taking Control of a Bigger Faction
If you are given control of a large Faction, you don‚Äôt get any extra Class Features for it. You only gain a single Class Feature when:

Your largest controlled Faction‚Äôs Level increases.
Through guile, wit, & brute force, you seize control of a Faction larger than yours against the will of its current owner.

For this reason, apprentices to power are normally handed smaller responsibilities to govern & grow first. It‚Äôs why all aristocracy deliberately engages in frivolous & ritualized warfare. This is also one of the reasons why regencies & abrupt transitions of power are unstable periods.
Loss of Levels
Your Factions can be destroyed. If this happens, you don‚Äôt personally lose your Class Features ‚Äî but you won‚Äôt get any more until you build a new Faction up to meet & exceed the Level of your previous one.
You DO, however, lose the benefits of boosted HP while healing, since your greatest Level is now much smaller. If you lose your Faction but not your current HP, guard it zealously ‚Äî you won‚Äôt be able to raise it that high again for quite a while.
Resources
A Resource is a strategically valuable material produced only at certain Locations (p. @), which are called Resource Nodes.
Obtaining Resources
Glass Empires deliberately eschews complex modeling of commodities exchange markets on the basis that emulating a proper dynamic economy is nearly impossible for computers, let alone a GM‚Äôs brain. The point of the game is not individual acts of arbitrage!
As a result, we use a simplified model. We assume the following things:

The GM determines & places Resource Nodes at the time of building the campaign world.
Resource Nodes, when made into Settlements, produce 1 or more copies of a Resource.
Access to a Resource is a binary yes/no. We do not measure or combine sums. Each copy is a sufficient supply for a single sovereign Faction.
If a Faction has one of the following, they have access to the Resource:

Direct control over a Resource Node producing that specific Resource.
An agreement with a Faction who has a copy of that Resource to have that copy continuously shipped via a currently unblocked trade route.
Another Leader in the same sovereign realm who has met one of these other conditions.


A Faction with no access suffers penalties based on the Resource in question.



                  
                  Design Implications 
                  
                

GMs should note that this design implicitly means that a single copy is always sufficient for an empire of any size. There are various compromises you can try to make here (i.e. Resource Nodes that are only good up to a certain Level of Faction), but most will be quite ugly. For the sake of the core game, try ~1 Resource copies per 10,000 square miles on your campaign map.


Types of Resource
There are two types of Resource:

Ordinary: If you have no access, all Deadlines & items that would logically require that resource have their cost/necessary Deadline duration doubled.
Exotic: As Ordinary, but costs increase 10-fold.

Ordinary Resources are assumed to be abundant in the world, such that alternative trade routes & distant shipping could be arranged even if it came at great friction. Exotic Resources are assumed to be produced only by a handful of locations & in great demand.
Trade Routes
A trade route is a path along connected, unblocked roads or naval routes between a Resource & a target Location.

Connected means a continuous unbroken line of roads. Trade demands roads, even if it‚Äôs a simple dirt trail. Navigable rivers & open waters are included.
Unblocked means no hostile army occupies the road or is within a 50-mile radius of a traced route across the seas.

Resources on Glass
In Glass, there are two Resources in play, both of which are Ordinary:

Wood: High-quality timber required for ships & siege machines.

Impacts shipbuilding, siege duration, & lumber construction.


Metal: Needed for almost everything. All of Glass‚Äôs mines are managed by salamander slave clans.

Impacts Army raising time & all construction/manufacturing/fortifications.



Game Design Notes
Why Wood & Metal?
We track Wood because Aian warfare is heavily dependent on siege machinery due to extensive fortifications, & Metal because salamanders hold a total monopoly on mining in Glass ‚Äî as a consequence, we must track the leverage they hold, but need not differentiate in type of metal as not only are all critical, but all are commanded by the same group.
Distributed Resources
We might refer to the standard Resource described here as a monopoly Resource, as specific Locations are assumed to have a monopoly on their production. However, some cases will require tracking of the availability of generalized materials such as food ‚Äî which, notably, is produced by every Settlement in some quantity & is desired by those who cannot grow it.
Rather than tracking these explicitly, we generally use Features for Faction Types that demand access to one or more friendly Settlements exceeding a certain size. The Families metric of a Settlement is, in a real sense, the resource volume for such distributed/generalized resources.
What About Magical Resources?
Here‚Äôs a fun note for adventurous GMs with homebrewed Resources: Theoretically, control over a given Resource could grant any benefit desired to Factions so long as it reflected the underlying fiction. Magical rocks that are used in cool guns? Only those Factions can mix in cool gun units. Fancy construction materials? Construction bonus instead of a penalty. Cheaper castles & housing. All you have to do is plot out all of the logical consequences & use-cases of a given material‚Äôs existence & estimate how they should show up in the rules.

Book-1---Glass-Empires/Part-1---Rules/{-.title: { 8 } - Armies
Book-1---Glass-Empires/Part-1---Rules/{-.content:
@ Axioms
Aia is a flat discworld & the implications of this has a radical impact on military operations, which renders the entirety of the real world‚Äôs strategic & tactical doctrine incomplete, misleading, or entirely obsolete.
If you set out to lead an army & do not keep the following points in mind, you will make catastrophic errors.

The horizon does not exist. On a flat disc, you can see 10 miles on flat terrain ‚Äî 3 times greater than on Earth. From a decent hill or watchtower, you can see 20 miles away. From a mountain, 80 miles of low-fidelity terrain is visible. This is an order of magnitude greater than on Earth.
Fog of war does not exist. Every army employs harpy scouts. Harpies can fly 5 miles straight up & gain a mountain‚Äôs view within an hour. Every army can see a minimum of 80 miles around itself. Armies can only march 10 to 25 miles in a day ‚Äî a single harpy messenger manages 50 per day. Surprise is all but impossible for any band bigger than 10 or 20 people. You don‚Äôt have to fight if you don‚Äôt want to ‚Äî nobody can close distance fast enough.
Naval warfare is impacted too. Stealth is almost impossible on Aia‚Äôs waters for anyone other than coraler armies. Only storms & darkness provide cover.
Sieges & defenses are extremely important. Because there‚Äôs no fog of war, the best way to win a fight is to force engagement on favorable ground. The easiest way to force engagement is to build high walls, which also negate the speed advantages of horses or harpy flocks in open terrain.
Heroes are extremely important. One king or his right-hand man can fight with the force of a thousand soldiers, especially in bottlenecked one-on-one fights through urban interiors. It‚Äôs one thing for a company of men to face down Achilles; it‚Äôs another for a single soldier to meet him surging through the castle halls when an experienced general can shatter a gatehouse & penetrate the greatest defenses through force of will & supernatural experience. Just as ordinary men are critical for holding & taking ground, the heroic bravery of warriors & conquerors also holds a critical place on the battlefield.

@ Methods to Raise an Army
You can raise armies in 3 ways.

You can raise an army from an existing Settlement. This produces an army approximating that Settlement‚Äôs cultural retinues ‚Äî the soldiers its society naturally creates. This costs MP.
You can equip green troops to create retinues. This creates an army of custom design & training at great up-front & ongoing cost.
You can hire mercenaries. Mercenaries demand ongoing pay per month ‚Äî $10 per BR.

Army Proficiency
Armies have a proficiency ranking; they can be green, veteran, or elite.

























Army TypeSoldier HDFeat. BonusGreen‚Äî‚ÄîVeteran+1 HD+1 FeatureElite+2 HD+2 Feature
In an army, we always assume the average Level is 1. This assumption doesn‚Äôt change no matter how large or experienced the Faction gets ‚Äî remember that experience has no correlation to Level. Most soldiers have a family or other network & qualify for Level 1, & the ones that don‚Äôt are covered by high-ranking officers.
Similarly, we assume each soldier has 1 combat-useful Class Feature. Soldiers tend to have a mixture of Features drawn from Class Features (primarily officers or famous soldiers) or from the Racial Features of great beasts near their home. Some have accompanying Mutations (p. @).
It is possible to have exceptionally specialized & talented special units with a dramatically higher HD, Feature list, magical powers & other beneficial traits. These elite units are not modeled by the basic experience table & are best treated as similar to Resources (p. @): Defined at world creation as valuable assets tied to locations or titles that are worth fighting over.
Increasing Army Proficiency
Armies gain a level of proficiency when they defeat an army of equal proficiency.
Cultural Retinues
A culture in the context of war is a distinct military tradition shaped by a region‚Äôs social structure, strategic doctrine, technological capability, demographics & economic infrastructure that determines what an army (as a gathering of soldiers) can possess. Each Culture has a set of troop types & a muster table displaying the different kinds of soldiers that can be deployed in a given army based on that culture.
Raising a cultural retinue costs MP debt only. The soldiers already exist. They are supported in the quantities your culture manages. The only constraint is economic: you can only pull 1 soldier per Family before you risk economic collapse.
You can choose to only raise a specific troop type from a given Settlement. If you do so, you must specify the troop type(s) you raised & may only raise a percentage of the total MP available equal to that troop type‚Äôs percentage of the culture‚Äôs army.


                  
                  These are averaged stats, not specific stats 
                  
                

A troop type‚Äôs BR represents the averaged combat power of a group including its officers, not any individual person. A unit of 100 light foot soldiers includes Level 0 skirmishers with spears, Level 2 sergeants with swords & chain, and a Level 3 captain/lord in plate; its 6 BR is the mean of that distribution, not the actual value for every soldier.


Glassian Human
Humans fight in open terrain & if they cannot do so, they will force the terrain to be flattened.








































































































Troop Type%BR(Vet)(Elite)Equip CostNotes & FeaturesLight foot30%112338$100‚ÄîMedium foot30%183660$400‚ÄîHeavy foot6%244880$600‚ÄîLight cavalry4%233960$700CavalryMedium cavalry3%366396$1,500CavalryHeavy cavalry2%5696144$6,000Cavalry, ThunderousWindgun sniper20%153050$200WindgunsWindgun heavy4%244880$700WindgunsHarpy scout1%4813$50Flying, ReconnaissanceAverage‚Äî173456$450‚Äî
House Eagle
Humans fight in open terrain & if they cannot do so, they will force the terrain to be flattened.






















































































Troop Type%BR(Vet)(Elite)Equip CostNotes & FeaturesWindgun sniper50%153050$125WindgunsWindgun heavy16%244880$625WindgunsMedium foot20%183660$400‚ÄîHeavy foot6%244880$600‚ÄîLight cavalry4%233960$700CavalryMedium cavalry3%366396$1,500CavalryHarpy scout1%4813$50Flying, ReconnaissanceAverage‚Äî183660$350‚Äî
Outlander Harpy
Harpies do not fight in open terrain. Harpies fuck up your supply chains, snipe people from a distance, scatter when marched on, & hide in dense woods where they can forage & sleep during the warm months.


















































Troop Type%BR(Vet)(Elite)Equip CostNotes & FeaturesHarpy scout50%4813$50Flying, Guerrilla, ReconnaissanceHighwayman40%112338$80Flying, GuerrillaHunter10%233856$100Flying, GuerrillaAverage‚Äî91727$70‚Äî

Harpy scout: Lightweight harpy spies carrying little more than a purse for letters & some light padded armor while relying on natural claws for defense.
Highwayman: Leather plus real weapons for landing & ambushing targets. Extremely loose & discordant harassment units.
Hunter: Experienced highwaymen & raiders who often appear as half-beast chimeras from mutation.

Glassian Salamander
Salamanders do not fight in open terrain. Salamanders sink you into a quagmire of defense-in-depth. Engineered road sinkholes, windgun sniper murderholes in hillsides, trenches & tunnel warfare, mass punji spike pits & worse.



























































Troop Type%BR(Vet)(Elite)Equip CostNotes & FeaturesTunnelrat50%51120$50Tunneling, GuerrillaEngineer40%92036$80Tunneling, GuerrillaPrairie wolf9%122748$300Tunneling, Guerrilla, WindgunsHarpy scout1%4813$50Flying, ReconnaissanceAverage‚Äî71629$85‚Äî

Tunnelrat: Guerrilla warriors with light armor, natural ‚Äòmander claws & a toolkit for trapping, stabbing & digging. Some carry crossbows for sniping.
Engineer: Armored miners & sappers just as competent at combat as in engineering.
Prairie wolf: A mountainous variant of the snipers seen in Apogea ‚Äî elite hunters who adopted long-range windguns to act in squads both above- & underground.

Coraler
Coralers do not fight in open terrain. They live almost exclusively on warm coastlines, where amphibious cities sit mostly untouchable by land-locked armies. Searaiders plunder & raze enemy Settlements wherever they lie within spitting distance of a river or stream & move essentially unchallenged & invisibly through larger bodies of water.


















































Troop Type%BR(Vet)(Elite)Equip CostNotes & FeaturesSearaider80%81730$100AmphibiousLandguard19%163664$600Lightly amphibiousHarpy scout1%4813$50Flying, ReconnaissanceAverage‚Äî102036$200‚Äî

Searaider: Spears & long knives for close combat, minimal leather armor & maximum speed. Searaiders travel light, hit hard & carry a lot of loot.

Can treat map-marked rivers & water bodies as roads.


Landguard: Plate & polearms plus knives prohibit land-locked coralers from swimming effectively, but enable better stationary defenses. Almost never taken along on raids.

@ Personal Retinues
You can build your own army by paying the cost for equipment & training.
This costs $ to start up & acts as an ongoing drain on settlement income for other purposes comparable to army MP debt ‚Äî a permanent reduction in MP to the tune of 1 MP per soldier.
Move: Raise an Army
While in your Faction‚Äôs Capital (or otherwise able to issue orders to your officers), you may call to raise an Army as a Move.
Raising an Army incurs a Manpower Debt (MP Debt). You are taking the healthiest, most capable men of a Faction & marching them off to war. This is more severe than simple silver; it takes a long time to replace a young man & there are few substitutes. In financial terms, 1 MP Debt is equivalent to $1 in economic impact. When measuring the impact of a Debt on your ability to loan, convert accordingly.
Your maximum MP Debt is equal to your Faction‚Äôs Capital‚Äôs Families. The Deadline depends on your Families; it takes around 1 day per 100 Families to raise an army, up to 10 days at most.

























FamiliesRecruitment Time‚â§100Just a few hours1001 day3003 days1,00010 days
When the Deadline expires, you automatically raise the Army; no Success Save required.
If you need a bigger Army than your Capital alone can provide, consider having your loyal Retainers raise Armies from their Capitals so that you can combine your collective forces.
@ Army BR
This is a placeholder, but for now, assume Army BR is 5√ó the race‚Äôs base BR per character i.e. humans have a BR of 1, so their armies are 10 BR per soldier.

@ why? well, soldiers will have Levels & equipment. mounts & armor & weapons. even a very modest leather + medium 1H weapon (4 sharp) human (HD 2) soldier (Lv1) has a BR of ~4.5 if we assume he has no beneficial Class Feature (maybe unlikely if he‚Äôs a Mercenary) & BR 9 if he does have a good Class Feature for combat.
calculating an expected BR per soldier might actually be harder than this. we might expect that the BR per MP would be more like ‚Ä¶ 10 √ó MP, on the basis that officers & heavy soldiers will be much better equipped than the light grunt I just outlined. we need an economic basis for the cost of supporting 1 BR.
shit no, this is wrong. racial BR is heavily impacted by natural weaponry and some races are only decent on paper because their natural weaponry is boosting their BR. salamander claws are as good as a bronze sword in melee ‚Ä¶ but that‚Äôs a weight advantage at best. they‚Äôd have kickass light troops but ‚Ä¶ hey, wait. that‚Äôs a skirmishing thing.

Recovering from MP Debt
Procedure: Disbanding an Army
If an Army with soldiers from a Faction is within that Faction‚Äôs realm, those soldiers can be disbanded. All surviving troops are subtracted from the MP Debt owed by the Faction to its Followers.
If an Army is disbanded outside its Faction‚Äôs territory, only half of the surviving MP is subtracted from the Debt owed ‚Äî the rest of the soldiers desert & disappear into the world. In extreme cases (i.e. disbandment on another continent), the GM may rule that no MP is returned to the pool.
Family Growth & Replacement
If a Faction grows in Families via other means, for every Family it obtains, it may erase 1 MP Debt.
In addition, at the start of every in-game year, each Faction‚Äôs MP Debt is reduced by 10% of its current Families, rounded up.
@ Desperation Recruitment
If a Faction is truly desperate to recruit soldiers despite massive outstanding MP Debt, there are two main routes available.
Create a New Subfaction
New Subfactions (p. @) start with no MP Debt, but you can‚Äôt easily erase them once they exist, and creating new Subfactions may anger any existing Subfaction whose interests are threatened by the gesture. It also requires that you as a Leader have spare Settlements, competent & loyal Retainers, and enough time to spare to go through the procedures of setting one up.


                  
                  Why don't new Subfactions inherit MP Debt? 
                  
                

Subfactions partly represent administrative efficiency at the cost of loss of control. Delegation offers advantages that centralized control doesn‚Äôt have ‚Äî such as tapping into manpower that wouldn‚Äôt otherwise be available.
It also dramatically simplifies the math by preventing cascading population calculation dependencies, and you can never get enough of that when you have to do most of the math in your head.


@ Raising the Armies of Your Subfactions
Rulers can & often rely upon Armies raised from their Subfactions to supplement their forces. In fact, this is one of the main benefits of having Subfactions.
To do so, simply contact & ask whichever of your Retainers is in command of that Subfaction to do so. This is treated as any other request to a subservient NPC. Try not to rely on Retainers who have grown angry at you lately ‚Ä¶ or who have reasons to stick a knife in your back.
@ Fortification
See Structural HP (p. @).
@ Move: Siege
Deadline: Sum the defending armies‚Äô BR along with the Location‚Äôs Families & SHP. Divide it by the attacking armies‚Äô BR & multiply by 100. The result is how many days out the Deadline should be set for.
At the end of the period, Save.





















ResultSuccess Save EffectCrit &Pass‚â•8The besieged Location falls under the besieging armies‚Äô control.Seven7Make another Save tomorrow.Failure &Fumble‚â§6The siege has been delayed. Recalculate & reset the siege Deadline.
When the Location is captured, resolve casualties by hosting a Battle between the two sides ‚Äî sans any Families, SHP bonus, or terrain/defensive fortification bonuses. Flight for the defenders is considered surrender instead of escape.
Assaults
An assault is a rapid assault of a fortification. It is played out as a Battle (p. @) with each Turn covering 1 day of events. Add the assaulted Location‚Äôs Families & SHP to the defending side‚Äôs BR.
Sieges consist of comparing Defender Strength vs Attacker Strength & adding variables like relevant Facts (on each side‚Äôs commanders + the Factions themselves), determining a numerical signed integer score, then comparing that against a chart that produces a ‚Äúduration of siege‚Äù value. That‚Äôs how long before someone cracks if nothing changes.

Book-1---Glass-Empires/Part-1---Rules/{-.title: { 9 } - Skirmishes
Book-1---Glass-Empires/Part-1---Rules/{-.content:
One of the two potential playmodes for resolving combat. Skirmishes are intended for detailed or intricate fast-paced combat between a small number of relatively powerful individuals in a constrained combat area. They take longer to resolve than Battles and should be restrained to combats that hold a great deal of significance to the game.
Battles take place as a series of Rounds. Each Round represents a brief period of time (a few seconds) wherein each character receives a Turn on which they may perform Actions (extremely short-duration Moves) to eliminate their enemies or otherwise secure their objectives.
Skirmishes require the following components for a battle:

A map of the battlefield & its terrain;
Some means of measurement of distance ‚Äî by default, a ruler;
Markers to indicate combatant locations.

Round Order
Rounds in Skirmishes have 3 phases.

The GM provides the players a brief overview of the current situation, if necessary. He informs them of what the enemies appear to be planning on doing this Round; he may fully describe the intended Actions of slower NPCs, and not at all the Actions of faster NPCs at his discretion.
All players take Turns in whichever order they figure (or simultaneously).
All enemies take their Turns in whichever order the GM determines (or simultaneously).

Some characters may have Features that let them act before or after all other characters. In such a case, the Round phases look more like this:

Overview provided
Fast characters take Turns
Ordinary characters take Turns
Slow characters take Turns

In all cases, PCs move before NPCs. If for any reason two NPCs or PCs are fighting each other, assign odds to & roll a d6 to determine who goes first.
Surprise Rounds
A Surprise Round is a special kind of Round where only characters who are ambushing another set of combatants may act.
Turns & Actions
On their Turn, characters may:

Take 1 Major Action & 1 Minor Action.

A Major Action can be used to take a Minor Action, but not vice-versa.


Take as many Free Actions as desired.
Take a Full-Turn Action if they perform no Movement & take no other Actions on their Turn (excepting Free Actions).

On other character‚Äôs Turns, when other Actions occur (or at any time), characters may also take up to one Reaction per Round.
@ Movement & Space in a Skirmish
Glass Empires by default uses a gridless approach to combat.
Character Size
A character occupies a cylindrical battlefield area whose diameter & height is equal to their Size. If you‚Äôre using miniatures, you should try to make sure their bases are scaled appropriately to the battlefield scale in use.
Measuring Distance
A ruler is recommended! Any scale can be used for a given combat space so long as everyone‚Äôs using the same measurement system. The recommended scale is 1‚Äù of map space per 5ft in-game.
Character movement rate, when reduced or increased over a part of a journey, is measured from the point of impairment to the end of it.
Mixing Speed Scores
If a character uses multiple Speed scores for movement on their Turn, the lowest Speed score is the cap on how far the character can Move in total, overriding any higher scores used.
Compelled Movement
Compelled movement, such as falling, being shoved, or knockback is not counted against a character‚Äôs Speed score for determining movement rate or total moved distance during their Turn(s).
Difficult Terrain
Terrain or space that‚Äôs unusually difficult to navigate smoothly (such as stairs or thin suspended boards/walkways) halves a character‚Äôs movement rate.
Trampling
Actors may move through the space of foes whose Weight is less than one-tenth their own. If they are Dashing, Sprinting, or Charging, they deal 5 Physical Damage in the process to anyone in the way. If their Weight is less than one-hundredth their own, the Damage is increased to 50.
Skirmish Actions
Skirmish Actions are Moves that take a very brief amount of time to resolve. These Moves can be used outside of Skirmishes to resolve what can be accomplished with short-term action durations ‚Äî generally speaking, a moment‚Äôs notice in reaction time is a single Major Action.
Attack (Major Action)
Declare a single target who you intend to harm & how (kill, shove, knock out). If the defender allows it, it happens. If not, perform 1 or more Strikes; by default, characters receive 1 Strike per Attack (but may receive more through Features or other abilities).
In order to attack a target, they must be within your weapon‚Äôs reach. A character‚Äôs melee reach is equal to their Size by default. Ranged attacks use the weapon‚Äôs listed range as its reach.
To perform a Strike against a chosen valid target, choose an available weapon & roll a Save. You may not add personal Fact bonuses to a Save, but should otherwise add factors such as the environment, Conditions, & the like.





























ResultStrike Save EffectsCrit‚â•12The defender suffers the weapon‚Äôs effects at twice the normal severity (normally, by doubling Damage taken).Pass8‚Äì11The defender suffer‚Äôs the weapon‚Äôs effects, including Damage taken.Seven7The defender suffers the weapon‚Äôs effects with halved normal severity (normally, by halving Damage taken; round down).Failure3‚Äì6Your Strike is deflected, poorly aimed, or otherwise a failure.Fumble‚â§2Your Strike fails & if you‚Äôre using a windgun or weapon with Reload, it‚Äôs jammed or requires reloading.
If your Strike deals enough Damage to outright kill the defender, you receive the Action you spent on your Attack back.
Ranged Weapon Rules
The following extra rules apply only to ranged attacks.

Out of Range: Ranged attacks may be performed at up to twice a weapon‚Äôs listed range, but when doing so, each Strike Save suffers a ‚àí1 penalty.
Firing into Melee: When firing on a foe engaged in melee, the victim is chosen by random die roll from among all those visible within that foe‚Äôs melee reach whose Heights are greater than half the intended target‚Äôs Height.
Cover: If the target has low/weak cover (half a character‚Äôs Height), ranged attacks‚Äô Strike Saves suffer a ‚àí2 penalty. If the target has high/strong cover (covering their whole body), it‚Äôs a ‚àí4 penalty.

The Angry Step
While starting an Attack Action & while not suffering any terrain or Condition that limits or alters your free movement, you may choose to move a distance up to or equal to your melee reach before making any Strikes. This extra distance is called the Angry Step & it does not count against your movement for your Turn.
Cast (Major Action)
Perform the Casting Move (p. @).
Dash (Full-Turn Action)
Move up to twice any of your Speed scores. You must Save to pass through any form of difficult or slowing terrain without falling.
Sprint (Full-Turn Action)
Move at least & up to 3√ó any of your Speed scores, turning no more than 45¬∞ throughout the entire distance. You must Save to pass through any form of difficult or slowing terrain without falling.
Charge (Full-Turn Action)
Move at least & up to 3√ó any of your Speed scores in a straight unobstructed line & then perform an Attack at the end as per the Attack Action‚Äôs rules. At the end of the Attack, the target, if less than half your Weight, must Save or Panic.
Interact (Minor Action)
Swap items around in your hands, sheathe or draw a weapon, pick up an item within your reach, or interact with something nearby (lever, door, etc). Light a torch, lantern, or flask.
Take Cover/Stand Up (Minor Action)
Become Prone (p. @) or recover from the Prone Condition.
Reload/Unjam (Major Action)
Reload or unjam a crossbow or windgun.
Set Down Gently (Major Action)
Set something down gently without damaging it ‚Äî especially bags, backpacks, or fragile items.
Search Your Pack (Full-Turn Action)
Search a backpack or other bag for a specific item & pull it into your hand.
Jump (Minor Action)
Attempt a jump as per the rules for jumping (p. @).
Movement (Minor Action)
Move a distance up to one of your Speed scores (Run, Dig, Swim, Fly) across the appropriate medium. Keep in mind:

You may not take other Actions while in the middle of a Move; you must end your Move first.
Be mindful of Engagement (p. @) & Grappling (p. @), which punish & prevent normal movement respectively.
You may not move through space occupied by a foe or stop within space occupied by an ally.

Disengage (Free Action)
Break Engagement (p. @) with any number of enemies. If you don‚Äôt also move to a location outside their melee reach by the end of your Turn, you‚Äôll be Engaged again.
Shove (Minor Action)
Save to shove a target in any direction that moves them further away from you. Use the adjacent Weight modifier table in addition to any other modifiers.





































The opponent is‚Ä¶Mod‚â•10√ó heavier‚àí4‚â•2√ó heavier‚àí2At least 10% heavier‚àí1About the sameN/AAt least 10% lighter+1‚â•2√ó lighter+2‚â•10√ó lighter+4





























ResultShove Save EffectsCrit‚â•12Target moves up to four times their Size in distance.Pass8‚Äì11Target moves up to twice their Size in distance.Seven7Target moves up to their Size in distance, but may choose to pull you an equal distance along with them (into their previously occupied space), maintaining Engagement and/or initiating a Grapple if the target desires.Failure3‚Äì6Target doesn‚Äôt move.Fumble‚â§2Target may push you up to your Size in distance.
Shoving may break Engagement & Grappling automatically if the shover wishes; the shover may also follow the shoved into the space they used to occupy if desired.
Grappling (Minor Action)
If you have at least one free hand, you can Save to Grapple an opponent. Use the adjacent Weight modifier table in addition to any other modifiers.





































The opponent is‚Ä¶Mod‚â•10√ó heavier‚àí4‚â•2√ó heavier‚àí2At least 10% heavier‚àí1About the sameN/AAt least 10% lighter+1‚â•2√ó lighter+2‚â•10√ó lighter+4





























ResultGrapple Save EffectsCrit‚â•12Target is Grappled & may also be knocked Prone if desired.Pass8‚Äì11Target is Grappled.Seven7Target can choose to enter a Grapple, but you don‚Äôt receive any benefits from this until the start of your next Turn.Failure3‚Äì6Target can choose to enter a Grapple, but you don‚Äôt receive any benefits from this until the start of your next Turn.Fumble‚â§2Target can choose to enter a Grapple, but you don‚Äôt receive any benefits from this until the start of your next Turn, & if they do so they can also knock you Prone.
Condition: Grappled
Grappling is a Condition that affects both characters. Neither character can move except by Shoving the other, which is also the only way to break a Grapple. Melee attacks gain a +2 to hit anyone in a Grapple, even if the attacker is outside the Grapple.
Characters who are more than twice the Weight of the largest opponent in a Grapple may freely move around, dragging the Grapple with them.
Extinguish Fire (Full-Turn Action)
Drop to the ground (becoming Prone) & simultaneously remove your own Burning Condition (p. @).
Engagement
When you enter an enemy‚Äôs reach, you become Engaged with them. Not as in marriage; rather, if you attempt to leave their reach without first taking a Disengage Action (p. @), they may perform a free Strike (p. @) on you with any valid weapon. Characters may perform any number of free Strikes in response to enemies leaving their reach without first disengaging.
Flight/Pursuit
Flight & pursuit is played out as a single Round of Battle (p. @) between all pursued & pursuers.
Panic & Fear
Characters Save to avoid Panic (synonymous with  terror, fear, etc.) when any of the following happens:

Their Group is harmed by a Strike for the first time.
They personally reach ¬Ω HP.
When charged with threat of trampling.
Whenever something spectacular, frightening, or sorcerous happens.
A hostile Spell is cast whose HD exceeds their HD.

Panicked NPCs flee from the source of their battle. Panicked PCs take doubled Damage (& other negative effects/durations, where applicable) from all sources.
Panic lasts until the end of combat. If Panic occurs outside of combat, it lasts for 1 hour; NPCs who are Panicked outside of combat will flee from it & otherwise be anxiously trying to avoid the source of their fear.
Mounted Combat
Mounts are combatants if they‚Äôre either sapient or carnivorous. Otherwise, they‚Äôre noncombatants. The following adjustments apply for mounts & their riders:

Saddles: Riders without saddles must Save each time the mount takes damage or fall from a distance equal to half the mount‚Äôs Size.
Incapacitation: If a mount or rider is Incapacitated (p. @) or killed, the rider(s) must Save or fall from their saddle(s). A non-combatant mount will flee battle if all riders are Incapacitated or killed.
Movement: Uses the mount‚Äôs scores.
Attacks: Non-combatant mounts can only attack with their rider during a Charge Action. Combatant mounts get their own set of Actions on their rider‚Äôs Turn.

Sapient or Panicked (p. @) mounts may ignore their riders‚Äô wishes & take erratic action at the GM‚Äôs discretion.
Condition: Concentration
Concentration is a Condition for characters who are preoccupied with an ongoing activity & cannot focus clearly. Concentration ends if a character takes any amount of Damage or falls under a Condition that precludes Concentration (such as being Incapacitated). In addition, characters may have to Save up to once per round to maintain Concentration if exposed to violent motion (a moving mount, ship deck in a storm) or violent weather (blinding rain, hail).
If a character loses Concentration, whatever task they were focused on fails automatically.
Condition: Burning & Ignition
Characters who are ignited catch the Burning Condition. While Burning, at the start of each of their Turns, characters must Save or take [Power] Fire Damage.
The Burning Condition ends if a character is submerged in water. Characters may also receive a Save to attempt to put out a fire by spending a Full-Turn Action rolling on the ground.

Book-1---Glass-Empires/Part-2---The-Worl.filePath: Book 1 - Glass Empires/Part 2 - The World of Glass/{ 11 } - The World of Aia @.md
Book-1---Glass-Empires/Part-2---The-Worl.title: { 11 } - The World of Aia @
Book-1---Glass-Empires/Part-2---The-Worl.content:
@ Cosmology, disc geography, the moon, the sun, spiritual demography, elemental hierarchy. Condensed from the Aia appendix and Bestiary design notes (spirit world/incorporeality/metal/gods/demons). This is the physics layer that everything sits on.


Aia is a spinning flat disc approximating 2,000 miles in diameter ringed by a tremendous swirling blizzard upon its rim which sits atop a vast ocean of heavy liquid called the Blackwater, of such density that even lead will float. Beyond this is the astronomers‚Äô hypothesized Telluric Veil, which separates the heavens from the Blackwater.
Aia is obviously flat because there is no curvature; on a flat ocean, one can see for nearly ~20 miles before atmospheric scattering blinds you.
The Sun
Aia‚Äôs Sun does not consistently rise in the east & set in the west. Instead, it appears to change orbits on a daily basis, because Aia (or possibly the external cosmos) is spinning. Because the sun‚Äôs position changes on a daily basis, sundials do not work on Aia unless rotated to match the sun‚Äôs new location at the start of every day.
The Sun is not known to be round, but is believed to be so. It is believed to be around the same size as the Moon & obviously orbits closer to Aia than the Moon as it occasionally passes between the Moon & Aia (a ‚Äúsolar eclipse‚Äù).
The Moon
There is a kingdom on the Moon & it hates you. It will never extend its fabulous & wonderful offerings, except to those kings who can provide what the Moon lacks. Through the dense white swirling clouds, you can occasionally glimpse their crystalline spires ‚Äî and only ever will you glimpse it.
The Moon appears to bob up & down in the sky, serving as the ‚Äúnorth star‚Äù from the 8th (when it first rises above the Rim Blizzard) to the 22nd (when it dips below the horizon). It does so because it orbits Aia at the same rate that Aia spins (a period of precisely 30 days), but at an incline of around 40¬∞.
The Moon is obviously round because it catches light from the Sun in the same manner that a sphere in its place would; it has an extraordinarily unpredictable day/night rhythm as a result, but it is usually shrouded in dense clouds anyways, so it is hypothesized to be bleak & stormy.
Astronomers believe the Moon to be around 300 miles in diameter & situated at a distance of 5,000 to 8,000 miles from the center of Aia, or around 3,000 miles from the Telluric Veil.
Practical Cosmology
A magician will usually know most of what‚Äôs contained in this section.
The Universe is composed primarily of elements & spirits. There is also metal & the gemstones.
Elements
There are 8 elements, of which 4 are cardinal or oppositional ‚Äî a distinction that mostly only matters to alchemists, many of whom use alternative models of the elements.


















































ElementAdditional FormsOppositionWaterIce, clouds, acidFireFireSmoke, ashWaterStoneSoil, quartz, clayAirAirGas, lightningStoneWoodAll plant tissue‚ÄîFleshBone, chitin, blood‚ÄîAcid‚Äî‚ÄîLightning‚Äî‚Äî
A body of elemental matter is the source of Hit Dice (HD) in creatures.
Spirits
A spirit is the soul possessing matter ‚Äî most commonly Wood or Flesh, but occasionally the other elements as well. Spirits are immortal.
Nothing is truly immaterial. Even a ghost exists as a projection (an illusion) of a spirit from a corpse (& is anchored to that corpse). Even a human soul when extracted, though invisible to the normal man, resembles a small glimmering star (or a Will o‚Äô Wisp, p. @) through magician‚Äôs eyes.
There is no such thing as a disincarnate god. Gods cannot appear anywhere they want or teleport around. Every god is a creature with a body. If they are worshiped, it is because they have built a cult as a religious Faction around themselves.
Divine Hierarchies
Gods are fractious creatures. Since they can‚Äôt be everywhere at once, most of them tolerate local cults so long as that cult is nominally a part of an aligned pantheon. In practice, most local cults owe allegiance to & are Subfactions of the local lord or are wholly independent. On the other hand, many gods are also temporal rulers.
The boundary between a local lord & a local demigod is exceptionally thin.
Elemental Spirits
A stable expression of an element can be home to spirits, which are generally called elementals. These are not common & most elemental matter is devoid of a spirit.
Metals & Gemstones
Metal is not elemental. There are 8 metals: Lead, tin, copper, iron, mercury, silver, gold, & bismuth. Similarly, there are an enormous variety of gemstones, the vast majority of which are associated with a handful of domains. None have special significance.
Magnets & the Moon
The Heart of All Iron is said to be held by the Lunarian Princess on the Moon (p. @). Iron longs to return to it. This is why iron is magnetic & why it orients, in the absence of other magnetized iron, towards the Moon.
@ Demons & Underworld
Demons (or devils; same thing) are creatures that live in the mechanical underbelly of the Underworld, beneath the Undergarden.
@ where the hell did i put my notes on the underworld ecology & thermodynamics
@ Magical Demography
@ How many spirits are there, in what configuration, & how can you call on them?
Magician Demography
Most societies have approximately 1 magician per 100 people. At most, 5 of every 100 can practically be magicians ‚Äî in the sense of possessing the temperament, intelligence, & skill required to do so.
Elemental Hierarchy
Elementals have a saturated & mostly fixed hierarchy that rarely changes. A river is composed of droplets forming creeks forming branches forming a river. Each river is part of a much larger watershed. In theory, you could call upon the waters of an entire land at once. In practice, they would never answer you in unison.
The earth, air, acid, lightning, & a firestorm are similar.
Divine Benefits Cancel Out
Just like how we don‚Äôt explicitly model elemental hierarchies, we don‚Äôt explicitly model the myriad pantheons of spirits & gods that exist across all human societies on the basis that it cancels out en masse. Everyone maintains a basic relationship to the gods that matter most to them ‚Äî rice, water, war, threshold, ancestral spirits, so there‚Äôs no need to give a +1 to anyone for explicitly stating that they‚Äôre doing that.
We only model the effects of gods in the following cases:

Exceptional Devotion: A person or group of people is exceptionally devoted to a single entity. Creates both benefits & vulnerabilities.
Exceptional Neglect: You have really angered a specific god.
Competitive Advantages: A person or group‚Äôs access to a specific god or set of gods grants unique competitive advantages that others don‚Äôt have.



                  
                  But then why can you get an extra blessing from divination? 
                  
                

Assuming you have extra cash on hand, you can presumably plead hard enough to receive an extra blessing for yourself.


@ Settlement Gods
Worship is zero-sum. It costs resources & most people only have so much to give.
A god is constrained by their physical nature. Since a god is an actual creature with creature-like powers, they are often not worshiped outside of their center of activity.
Demigods are generated as monsters that occupy Locations on the map. There is generally 1 per 5-10 settlements?
Many demigods are humans that have acquired a lot of features over time.
Making More Spirits
Making spirits requires you murder powerful creatures & imprison their souls.
Why not just send ghosts to fight for you?
It‚Äôs super expensive to send disembodied spirits to do anything. Almost impossible, actually; nothing can be truly immaterial & a spirit by itself is mostly helpless. A ghost is just a tortured spirit stuck in a damaged Totem (normally, their own corpse). You can embed a soul in a skeleton, but why? It‚Äôd usually be easier & less expensive to just send a regular living soldier.
@ Making Things
@ This table is only good for making stuff out of stuff. Don‚Äôt have a system for that yet.









































MetalDescriptionLeadImmaterial spirits cannot pass through lead. It naturally absorbs magical energy, making it ideal for shielding.TinTin‚Äôs primary value is diffusion. Combined with copper, it produces bronze, which is comparable to iron but weaker & prone to shattering.CopperCopper induces cycling & true transformation.IronImmaterial spirits cannot pass through iron. Unlike lead, iron is well-suited for weaponry & therefore is associated with violence. It can also be used for binding & warding, but it absorbs magic poorly.MercuryMercury cannot induce changes, but does allow fluidity, adaption & mutation of qualities within existing boundaries.SilverSilver serves as a mirror & can be used to induce reflection & redirection in magical instruments.GoldFunctionally eternal & potent as a conductor. Magical energy stored in gold & wrapped in iron can last nearly forever.BismuthA lethal capacitor. Bismuth stores massive amounts of energy & then violently & unpredictably discharges it.
@ Apotheosis
A character with a cult & Godhead can treat their largest cult as the source of their Level even if they are not personally the Leader of that cult.
If they increase in Level by virtue of their cult‚Äôs expansion, then rather than taking a standard Class Level, they can take a Level in a divine Class related to their Domain (p. @).
Divine Classes convey supernatural powers related to the field that the character is purported to specialize in. Whatever people are praying to you for is the thing that you‚Äôre qualified for a divine Class in.
Besides the nature of the abilities unlocked, there is no special advantage to having Class Features from a divine Class.
