Book-1---Glass-Empires/Part-2---The-Worl.content: @ Racial write-ups for Glassian humans, salamanders, harpies, coralers. Cultural material like the Deep Nomads content goes here, cleaned up. This is where you learn what it feels like to be these people.

Book-1---Glass-Empires/Part-2---The-Worl.filePath: Book 1 - Glass Empires/Part 2 - The World of Glass/{ 14 } - Main Scenario.md
Book-1---Glass-Empires/Part-2---The-Worl.title: { 14 } - Main Scenario
Book-1---Glass-Empires/Part-2---The-Worl.content:
Victory Conditions
The main goal of every major Faction is to conquer Glass. In order to conquer Glass, one of the two following states must be achieved:

De Jure Emperor: Meet all three of the following conditions:

Control the City of Leaves.
Be declared Emperor with the blessings of Kain or Prince S≈´n.
Control at least 50% of the island‚Äôs area, as measured by the largest contiguous area that can be drawn between your Settlements & the ocean containing no Settlement not under your control.


Conquest: Annihilate or subjugate every other major faction.

If either state is met, the Faction who has done so is victorious. This is the intended & most probably ‚Äúclean‚Äù end state, but of course, this being a role-playing game, it may yet go on.
Bonus Victories
Each major Ruler also has an objective of their own. If a Ruler achieves their own objective by the end of the game, they are also victorious, even if they are not the new Emperor.
Major Factions
House Eagle

Capital: Warnwater (12,000)

Winding underground Jade Catacombs form intricate network beneath the city


Leader: Lady Eagle (Lv9)

Venetian League starts allied with you & will loan you unlimited sums so long as you still appear to be potentially capable of winning the war.
You may use the House Eagle army culture instead of the Glassian Human set.


Bonus Victory Agenda: Secure exclusive banking rights for the Venetians over all of Glass. You will be rewarded handsomely.

‚ÄúProtected‚Äù from the civil war by virtue of your trade with the Venetian League‚Äôs merchants, you are a cosmopolitan opportunistic warmonger bathed in banking influence from a young age. You believe that working alongside the Venetians will allow you & your House to seize total control over the island of Glass ‚Äî and even if that means you‚Äôd be a colonial or puppet ruler, you‚Äôre fine with that. The Venetians‚Äô investment is riding on your success.
You are a psychopathic hedonist. Your primary interest is in getting filthy rich & enjoying everything Aia has to offer, which is a tremendous deal for a wealthy lord.
House Arcades

Capital: Arcadia (8,000)

Hilltop Snow Jesters‚Äô Temple provides burial & reanimation services
The Panopticon grants instantaneous vision across the continent sharp enough to make out individual people provided the operator‚Äôs attention. (Practically, you know all orders & movements as soon as they happen & can watch for specific events in specific places.)
32 loyal Jiangshi (6,440 fearless BR w/ full Retainers, $4,800/month)

Every month, you may replace 1, up to 32.




Leader: Lord Arcades (Lv8)

The commoners distrust you.


Bonus Victory Agenda: Expel all foreigners from Glass (the coralers, Venetians, Hydreans, etc.) or return them to the servant caste where they belong (the salamanders, harpies) ‚Äî either alive or (un)dead.

You are the son of a line of fore-fathers going back to the earliest age of Glassian history, and This war has gone on long enough. It‚Äôs an embarrassment to your blood. An initial effort to revive the ancient imperial infrastructure has seen success, and so you now seek its end through the judicious application of your unparalleled black magic combined with the power of the Panopticon.
You are a necromantic isolationist. Total control over Glass is the only way to drive out the invading forces ‚Äî and your only hope of posing enough of a threat to drive out the demonic creditors you‚Äôve borrowed from in order to fuel your ambitions. You are sworn to the island‚Äôs people, gods & history, but have contempt for the immortal Kain as a failure & a coward who ran from power in a moment of weakness. You will be better. Man cannot shy from horrors when they‚Äôre what is needed. There is only one grip strong enough to reforge this empire, and it is yours.
House Beacon

Capital: Sun Bay (5,770)
Leader: Lord Beacon (Lv8)

The Hydreans have shipped you Jesuits & Zealots.
You possess 3 of the 5 metals mines on Glass & harbor an uneasy relationship with the salamander operators.


Bonus Victory Agenda: Convert all remaining major factions to Chimatocoatl‚Äôs cult & sacrifice all of the Gods of Glass upon her altar.

You are the heir of a Lord who lost his original lands & home to a rampaging native god, and while your father may have forgiven it, you have not. The Hydrean Jesuits discovered this, and, in secret, reached out to you with condolences & an opportunity for revenge. You are a whole-hearted believer in the Hydrean doctrine. The false gods are the broken fragments of Chimatocoatl, and they must be captured & reunited with her.
You are still a thoroughbred Glassian. You believe in the strength of Glass & the superiority of its people over the rest of the Disc. You may or may not respect Kain for what he is, but you know he isn‚Äôt sufficient as-is for the state of the island. His cult has failed. The gods are killing everyone. The Rainbow Serpent of Wisdom is the only thing that can bring a permanent peace to the island, and you are her sword.
Commons Revolt

Capital: Cerces (8,000)
Leader: General Ram (Lv8)

One of the five salamander mining guilds have an uneasy alliance with you in the belief you will liberate them.
If General Ram is leading a siege, the Families of a Settlement cannot be added to the BR of the defenders while determining siege duration.


Bonus Victory Agenda: Stop the war by any means and totally strip the Governors (Lords & Magistrates) of their special privileges. Establish an at least nominally republican government or persuade Kain to return to the throne as God-Emperor of Glass.

Everything went to hell the instant Kain abdicated his throne. There was five years of ‚Äúpeace‚Äù between the Autumn Massacre of the Imperial court and the outbreak of the Coraler invasion, & even those were fraught with confusion, accusations of legitimacy, murder, rapid imposition of tyranny, defection & decay. And before anyone could finish with the invasion, the Mother Empress went insane & everyone started killing each other for the right to rule. All of the old wonders are broken. Famines are raging. Bandits flood the lands. The entire world is on fire. If someone doesn‚Äôt act, there will be nothing left.
You are the populist military leader of a massive Commons revolt against the corruption, decadence, and collective failures of the entire leadership of Glass that has been steadily accumulating since the day the Second Phoenix delegated his command to an unworthy mortal bastard. Born to a potter & raised by a Magistrate as the ‚Äúson‚Äù of a Lord, you‚Äôve seen every level of Glassian society & every flaw & crack that‚Äôs splintered this society from cornice to pedestal. Every single one of these ‚ÄúLords‚Äù is an illegitimate pretender. Kill them all.
Your plan is simple: Force every single Lord to bend the knee & renounce their nobility, march into the temple-city of Heaven‚Äôs Peak, and either bring Kain back to the throne as God-Emperor or set up a republican government with his counsel.
You are not strictly egalitarian & are still knowing of the superiority of Glassians, but your popular base includes many salamanders, harpies, and a few Coralers who believe that at the very least you won‚Äôt throw them out of Glass.

Principal Assets: You are the most accomplished & dangerous general in present Glassian history. You are beloved across the Commons to almost the extent that Kain is, and receive no resistance from the average man. You have minimal experience in stewardship. You are a murderer and a mercenary. Every ounce of populist energy you summon is the embodiment of rage, and if it has form it is only because you shape it. Your people are furiously loyal but unstable and untrained. Eschewing the extant elite means you have no counter-elite to work with, which is the most dangerous possible position for an upstart rebellion.

Glass Coralers

Capital: Inkland Hold (10,100)
Leader: Admiral Edward (Lv9)

Coastal cities grow & recover soldiers at 5√ó the normal investment rate ‚Äî you are buffeted by Coraler refugees fleeing the Inner Sea.
Your soldiers are entirely Coraler. You have the only functional naval stealth.


Bonus Victory Agenda: Secure a permanent claim on Glass for the Coralers. Expel the Venetians from Glass as revenge & for future protection ‚Äî your interests lie in their complete genocide.


It‚Äôs not just Glass that‚Äôs burning. Plague of the reef, economic strangulation, & civil war has swept the Inner Sea. Sixteen hundred years of total domination over the waves where no man nor bird nor beast could touch you for fear of watery death had lulled you & your fellow Coralers into a sense of false security that was obliterated with one act of a foreign god.
Now, with vast white concrete shells hiding amphibious cities & stealth legions that could sweep the economic centre of the Grand Disc hollowed out by deathly plague & war, you‚Äôre on the run, desperate, & struggling to find a new home. And on Glass‚Äôs crumbling shores, that home was built through bloody conquest.
You have no plan B. This is not your land, but it will be your land, because you will die otherwise. Nobody has ever challenged the Coralers until five years ago. Now is the time for stability, that revenge may follow. Humans know nothing & will learn nothing that the depths have to teach. On a world with 40-mile diameter visibility on the open sea, your every soldier is a submarine. The brush of a Coraler‚Äôs tentacled fist over a contract ended empires. One word summoned blockades that crushed trade Disc-wide. And a bunch of arrogant upstarts think a single plague will put your people down?
You are the most accomplished leader the Coraler clans have ever known. Every new wave of carnage brings you reinforcements ‚Äî desperate, furious warriors with a world to reclaim. See to it the merchants & empires of Man learns what it means to fear the sea.

Book-1---Glass-Empires/Part-2---The-Worl.title: { 15 } - Minor Powers @
Book-1---Glass-Empires/Part-2---The-Worl.content:
@ Gods of Glass, Imperial Family, Papermark, Theomni, Kain & the Oropolis, Mountain Imps. The secondary players and wildcards.

Not everyone on Glass is fighting for dominance. Some are just looking to survive. Some have grand ideals. Some simply don‚Äôt have the means to conquer ‚Ä¶ yet.
Unlike the major factions (p. @), minor powers are defined by a disinterest in the conquest of Glass. They may be suitable as
Gods of Glass @

Gods of Glass (Island of Glass Lv14):

Agitated beyond reconciliation. Every sacred rite has been neglected in the chaos. There is no clear heir to the throne who can resolve it. A billion knives lash out from the forests & mountains at the Empire of Glass.
Agenda: Acausal inhuman evolutionary selectors.

YOU HAVE ALL FAILED. WE WILL KILL UNTIL YOU LEARN AGAIN TO STOP US.
Principal benefactors: Themselves. They are going to kill you.





Salamander Clans @

Leader: Each clan has its own leader.
Capital: Each clan has a separate mine as its heart.

There are ~2,000 salamander families on Glass split across five mine hubs. Each is descended from the slaves brought from wars centuries ago. Each is straining under the demands of the war. Each has been preparing for this day for centuries.
The salamanders have no hope in conquest. They face down a 30:1 disadvantage in manpower alone. But they can be brokers. In war, steel is better than gold.
The Imperial Family (Lv10) @

Leader: Mother Empress

A possessed & possibly dead madwoman regent possessing terrible magical power, arrayed by a host of demons.


Capital: City of Leaves (15,200)

Assets

S≈´n, Prince of Glass: The 6-year-old heir to the throne. Recognized as the legitimate heir by everyone who doesn‚Äôt have a stake in potentially claiming the throne for themselves.

Papermark (Lv9) @

Leader:
Capital:

Assets

The singularly most valuable archive of knowledge & magic in Glass, possibly all of Aia.

Theomni & the Templevault @

Theomni

An independent land-locked city-state in the middle of the island, surrounded by the Golden Birchwood and overlooked by the Oropolis. Holds its status as independent by virtue of Kain‚Äôs implied protection & by virtue of the wealth it‚Äôs digging out of the Templevaults the city sits upon. Ultimate mega-dungeon dig-site for those who‚Äôd rather be dungeon-crawling instead of faction-crawling.
ROLE: Megadungeon-crawl site for future dungeon crawl system. Doubles as a strategic asset if conquered.



The Oropolis & Kain @

Kain & the Oropolis

The Golden God-Emperor. The Second Phoenix. The Absent King. Here he‚Äôs hid for 20 years, cloistered from his children in a vast temple-mountain of every wonder & pleasure. Who can breach his fortress?

He could be persuaded. He is on the edge, watching the smoke and fire rise from his realm, watching the bloodshed and the slaughter. But he could just as easily sink into history‚Äôs grasp ‚Äî he‚Äôs seen it all, after all. The last four universes & their countless billions of years. What‚Äôs one more failed zen garden to let die by abandonment?
His wife, Yuko, is out there somewhere. Maybe she has the zealousness to restore her husband‚Äôs mind.


ROLE: Several.

He built Glass. He‚Äôs the axis for the entire empire‚Äôs history.
Persuading him is a valid means of obtaining legitimacy for conquest, or to re-stabilize the realm after conquest via obtaining blessing.
It is also a potential victory for parties who‚Äôd rather delegate the role of ‚Äúgod‚Äù to an external justification. ‚ÄúRestore the rightful emperor‚Äù.





The Mountain Imps @

The Mountain Imps

Imps are coal goblin-demon-things. ‚ÄúGemfuckers‚Äù. They‚Äôve seized most of the Imperial mines & are choking off Glass from its critical mineral resources at the time they‚Äôre needed most ‚Äî possibly a good thing, since if Glass could leverage its full natural resources, it‚Äôd be a smoking crater by the end of the war.
ROLE: Interlopers that occupy various strategic assets & provide friction for conquest. Comic relief-style ‚Äúgoblin‚Äù psychopaths ‚Äî someone you can kill and not feel bad about killing, but who are also incompetent or comical in ways that lighten tension.

Book-1---Glass-Empires/Part-2---The-Worl.content:
Design Notes
General Bestiary Template
[T/N: Laid out roughly in order. See other finished samples for finished product examples.]
An entry‚Äôs Description, Wants, Intellect & Morality are invokable Facts.





























StatsHD [[HD]+[Level]] ([HP] + [Armor] AP), BR @, Size @‚Äô, Weight @#, Max. Load @#, Run @‚Äô, Fly @‚Äô, Swim @‚Äô, Dig @‚ÄòGroup@ / @ / @ [T/N: Group size, as a set of possible encounter sizes, in ascending size. i.e. Solo, group/pack/murder/sounder, warband/horde.]Desc.[T/N: VERY brief physical description. 2 goals: cover anything that isn‚Äôt obvious to a GM by name/convention & provide tactile interesting fragmentary terms to bolster descriptions. no more than 200 characters.]Wants[T/N: As terse a description of the actor‚Äôs core interests as possible. i.e. golem: ‚ÄúFollow commandments to the letter & no further.‚Äù wolf: ‚ÄúFood, shelter, territory. The pack worships Tanto.‚Äú]Intellect[T/N: Terse, colorful assessment of intelligence. Clarification & instructions on handling alien neurology or behavior only if required/non-obvious. ‚ÄúSpells are like animals that can interpret simple speech.‚Äù ‚ÄúBrilliant only at solving riddles & idiotic otherwise.‚Äú]Morality[T/N: Terse, colorful assessment of morality. ‚ÄúOpportunistic savagery.‚Äù ‚ÄúLudicrously & comically malevolent.‚Äù ‚ÄúWell-intentioned foppery.‚Äú]


                  
                  Translator's notes 
                  
                

HD & HP
HD is physical & spiritual durability. Humans are 2 HD & harpies, salamanders, & Coralers are all 1 HD. Otherwise, we stick kinda close to OSR, but maybe add 1 HD to normal assumptions. All HD are d6. HD ¬Ω = 2 HP & halved rolls. HD 0 = always 0 HP. this isn‚Äôt lethal but it‚Äôs instant death if you take a hit. HP is always (HD √ó 4).
Level
We assume Level is 0 (& don‚Äôt bother writing it down) unless this entry is SPECIFICALLY for a ruler of a Faction (i.e. it‚Äôs a ‚ÄúDuke‚Äù or ‚ÄúGeneral‚Äù or ‚ÄúBusiness Magnate‚Äù entry) in which case defer to core rules‚Äô Level table to determine average assumed size.
Armor (AP)
Armor indicates absorption via Armor Points. Strictly speaking, Armor Points is a multiple of Power (HD + Level) added as an additional HP-equivalent & is based on the quality of gear. Leather is Power √ó 1, Chain is √ó 2, Plate is √ó 4. We can conceivably have bigger Armor bonuses for better Armor materials ‚Ä¶ but probably not.
That said, we don‚Äôt show the Armor multiplier value since it can be worked out from just the final AP number. To get the original Armor number, you can divide AP by Power.
Entities that have high HP but low HD (i.e. they are of weak character/strength of mind) should have an Armor bonus instead of getting extra HD. In addition, beasts that are literally heavily armored (like dragons) should have AP. We‚Äôd call this natural armor but we have no reason to make a distinction. In mechanical terms, some attacks can pierce Armor & attack HP directly & Armor provided from gear doesn‚Äôt heal from rest. Those are the main practical differences.
Calculating BR
As per the rules in Battles, under Battle Rating (p. @).
Size
Size is the cylindrical area you can fit into while fighting. Any value over 5‚Äô should be rounded to 5‚Äô increments. Standard sizes are 0‚Äô, 1‚Äô, 2‚Äô, 5‚Äô, 10‚Äô, 15‚Äô, 20‚Äô, 30‚Äô, 40‚Äô, & 60‚Äô. Arbitrarily larger sizes are also acceptable.
Weight & Max. Load
Weight is measured in stone (#). 1# = 10lb. We accept ¬Ω#, ¬º#, & N/A. Should generally be rounded to increments of 3# where possible. Weight should reflect realistic mass (i.e. ‚Äúthis thing is 1,000 pounds‚Äù = it should be Weight 100#). Max. Load is carry capacity & should generally be 2/3 of Weight unless your knowledge of the creature‚Äôs biology for some reason makes an exception worthwhile.
Speed scores
Run, Fly, Swim & Dig (in that order) should be in 5‚Äô increments wherever possible. Don‚Äôt list Speeds that a creature doesn‚Äôt have. Humans don‚Äôt have a Dig score. Possessing a score indicates a natural capacity for it; Humans can swim, but they don‚Äôt have Swim 15‚Äô. Climbing (i.e. like a spider) is not a separate score; it is a Feature that lets you use Run to climb. Humans move at Run 25‚Äô. We treat 5‚Äô as 1‚Äù on a combat surface normally.
Weapon & attack design philosophy
Classic B/X or OSR design gives various monsters a bunch of redundant/similar attacks. bite+bite+claw? pls, they all do Slash damage. A weapon is meaningfully differentiated if its damage, type, usage patterns, or special effects (i.e. range, armor pierce, burning) matter. if there‚Äôs no reason to ever choose between two different weapons (natural or otherwise), why separate them?
All weapons are phrased as Features tagged with (Weapon). Natural weapons are tagged (Natural Weapon). Weapons are implicitly melee unless a range is provided (in feet, or as ‚ÄúLoS‚Äù for line-of-sight effects).
Weapons usually don‚Äôt grant multiple Strikes except as particularly special abilities. Extra Strikes are a separate Feature & most natural weapons should be combined into one big weapon if they have no differentiation.



Feature 1: Description of Feature. [T/N: Features can be stolen by players who cook & eat the monster. Keep that in mind while designing them. features should be simple enough that a paragraph of text fully describes their mechanics. if that‚Äôs not enough space for whatever weird shit this thing does, add it to the description & include whatever fancy tools you need i.e. md tables there.]



                  
                  T/N: Feature design philosophy 
                  
                


Simplicity over fidelity. Avoid fiddly conditionals or mental spoon load. Strive for obvious/mnemonic conditions if you need conditionals.
Avoid referencing specific weaponry unless it‚Äôs essential to the Feature. (‚ÄúNatural weapons‚Äù is better than ‚Äúclaws‚Äù; think like, if a PC gets this power, would it make no sense? sometimes i.e. mind flayer tentacles, it‚Äôs unavoidable and that‚Äôs okay. other times, you can correct for it.)
If it only works in rare circumstances or just grants a +whatever bonus to niche actions, consider cutting it or merging it with another Feature. Each Feature should be ideally punchy and central to the thing‚Äôs identity.



[T/N: Description goes here. Very brief text description of whatever is even worth mentioning that you haven‚Äôt already. We don‚Äôt really talk about ecology or all the gay bullshit AD&D liked to bloat its page count with. Anything you put here should be witty, terse, and/or very highly relevant to what a panicked GM flipping to this page might want to know about the creature that isn‚Äôt already covered. No more than 1-2 sentences unless you really have to include a bunch of rules text that can‚Äôt be a Feature (i.e. because players should NEVER be able to get it) or if you want to do something fun like mention that you can re-use an animated snowman‚Äôs hat to animate other snow things or whatever the entry demands.]
Treasure: [Only if any consistent treasures to note; otherwise, N/A. Entries that demand interesting/legendary treasures may have a reference to a table i.e. ‚ÄúSee table.‚Äù plus adjacent table(s) of treasures.]


                  
                  T/N: Optional: GM Prep Tables 
                  
                

Determine the most difficult part of using the creature. Basically, ask yourself ‚Äúwhat‚Äôs the hardest thing for a GM to make up on the spot that will almost always be needed with this creature?‚Äù pick the top 0-3. Sphinxes need riddles & GMs never have a book of riddles on-hand. We want to maximize utility. a prep table should NEVER consist of:

Mundanities (the dwarf has a family)
easy stereotypes (the dwarf is a miner with a beard & is named greyhammer & lives in a hill)
random bullshit (the dwarf is a gay cake-maker)

it should be structured, difficult-to-ad-hoc content with extremely high conceptual density. whatever‚Äôs critical to its identity & hard to come up with. ‚ÄúDo the hard work for the GM.‚Äù
some ideas: ‚ÄúWhat is this thing doing?‚Äù ‚ÄúWhat is its nature?‚Äù ‚ÄúCause/casus belli for [stupid shit they do but that‚Äôs difficult to wrap your head around as a human who doesn‚Äôt think like they do]‚Äù. victims/slaves/captives, symbiotes/parasites & other common/universal relationships, ‚Äúwhy the fuck would we ever bother this thing/who‚Äôs prompting us into it & why‚Äù, ‚Äúcommon/interesting variants or very similar creatures you can make by changing this thing‚Äôs name & giving it some minor tweaks that you can contain in 160-200 characters‚Äù, ‚Äúorganizational structure‚Äù, ‚Äúpremade encounters‚Äù, ‚Äúhazards that are required to go along with this entity to make it work conceptually‚Äù, ‚Äúweird weapons & armor‚Äù, ‚ÄúWHY the FUCK does this thing EXIST, who did this and why???‚Äù, ‚Äúset of specific highly interesting & thematically appropriate loot‚Äù, ‚Äúlegendary/mythical powers associated with this creature or with special/unique versions of it‚Äù.
write tables as proper Markdown tables. don‚Äôt bother giving them headings since you can just put the heading into the table itself like the examples below. you can include multiple columns if they all go together i.e. ‚ÄúGem color‚Äù ‚ÄúValue‚Äù ‚ÄúPower‚Äù or whatever. we only use d6 dice, so 1d6 for most tables, or 2d6 if you need lots of results or weighted results. you can also use d66 numbered double per entry i.e. 11‚Äì12, 13‚Äì14 [‚Ä¶] 65‚Äì66 to get an 18-entry table w/ a flat curve ‚Ä¶ but you‚Äôd better have a really exciting reason to make an 18-entry table.




                  
                  Optional: When eaten 
                  
                

Flavor: [Terse, colorful description of flavor or experience of consumption. ‚ÄúDisgustingly fatty pork.‚Äù ‚ÄúLike eating coal, because you are, and it‚Äôs just as unhealthy.‚Äú]
Notes: [Description of effects, either as a flat-out ‚Äúthis happens‚Äù or ‚ÄúRoll on table below.‚Äù followed by a table (1d6 or 2d6) of 2-4 side effects. Side effects should be equally balanced by permanence & positive/negative effects. They can just outright kill the players via poison or horrible nightmare magical fate if that‚Äôs appropriate for the creature in question. You can include qualifiers.]




                  
                  T/N: Regarding When Eaten entries 
                  
                

Players can eat monsters to gain better stats (if it had better stats than them) & their Features or Facts (as mutations, kinda). If a creature has other unusual effects or characteristics related to its consumption (i.e. some beasts are HORRIBLY CURSED or KNOWN TO CURE DISEASE or THE FEAST OF KINGS), that‚Äôs what this section is for. It should NOT be included for anything of blisteringly mundane quality. Basically, if you‚Äôre describing a normal meal, this section should not be here. A goat entry doesn‚Äôt deserve a When Eaten section (unless it‚Äôs a Scapegoat & eating it transfers all the awful sins the goat bears onto the consumer or some shit).
write tables as proper Markdown tables. don‚Äôt nest rolls inside them. Don‚Äôt use Saves, just do straight 1d6 or 2d6 tables with entries weighted appropriately.




                  
                  T/N: Other general design principles 
                  
                


Try not to use funny names where English words or calques would be better. Names should be mnemonic where possible; trivial to remember.
Natural weapons are not Features for the sake of BR. No weapon is.
We do some lumping. Basically, ask yourself if a creature could be achieved with a very minor edit to the stats of this creature. A name edit, a tweak to a stat or two, maybe one extra ability. The giant badger is a slight edit of the wolf. The dolphin is a slight edit of the seal.

Tables of variant creatures should never include the base creature. If a GM wanted to use the creature as-is, he‚Äôd just do that.


Generally speaking, pack hunters don‚Äôt need abilities that specifically represent their skill for hunting in packs; this is implicit to the way the Battles system works. A pack has a huge advantage in claiming tactical bonuses (terrain, perception, flanking, maneuverability, morale) which can potentially add up to a +4 collectively (on a 2d6 Save!). This could allow a relatively small pack to fight on even ground against a creature with up to 4√ó more BR than the pack does. Ergo, don‚Äôt give a ‚Äúpack hunter‚Äù bonus unless it‚Äôs really, sincerely justified.
AP must always be granted by an Armor score. If you want to give a creature an ablative pool of HP somehow (i.e. a robot having parts with separate HP), you should give it fixed HP and/or HD.
Immunities, Resistances, and Weaknesses are conveyed via Features because we want them to be copiable by alchemy. (Every Feature is potentially an ability that a player could obtain.)
Unusual/magical equipment is listed as a Feature, tagged with (Equipment), i.e. ‚ÄúMagic Shield (Equipment)‚Äú.

Unusual/magical weapons are similarly tagged as {Weapon}.
Unusual natural weapons are tagged as (Natural Weapon).
Class Features (i.e. derived from profession or knowledge) are labeled (Class) & are otherwise presumed to be Racial (or natural) Features.





Description, Wants, Intellect, & Morality can all be invoked as Facts where necessary.

@ Our Fellow Glassian
Commoner





























StatsHD 2 (8), BR 2, Size 5‚Äô, Weight 15#, Max. Load 10#, Run 25‚ÄôGroupSolo / 1d6 household / 2d6 work gang / 3d6 √ó 10 angry mobDesc.Calloused hands, sunburnt face. Carries tools or a basket. Moves with purpose.WantsStability, prosperity, & to be left the hell alone. Failing that, revenge.IntellectShrewd about anything they can see & touch. Credulous about anything they can‚Äôt.MoralityGenerous in times of plenty, ruthless in scarcity. Always hedging.

Improvised Weapon (Weapon): 4 Physical. Hatchet, mattock, hammer, kitchen knife, or whatever‚Äôs at hand.

Grand & free Glass ‚Äî the Commoner will sing to its history, wherever they hold a voice to sing with, for they believe themselves its blood & soul. And a betrayed blood & soul!
Commoners will rarely attack directly, but when they do, they attack with ruthless mania. They feel emotions stronger than you do. The most dangerous thing is a tight-knit village that‚Äôs got nothing left to lose.
Treasure: 1d6‚àí2 in pocket money. A household has 3d6 √ó 10 in savings, tools, home & trade goods investments, for a plundering bandit.


                  
                  Playing a Glassian Commoner 
                  
                

These are not medieval serfs. Kain‚Äôs long reign suppressed the worst excesses of intermediate lordship. Glassian commoners are commercially literate, own property, haggle confidently, & hold strong opinions about the quality of their local products versus the next village‚Äôs. They remember prosperity & are furious about losing it.
Spirits are landlords. Commoners negotiate with river spirits the way you negotiate with your landlords. They set out offering plates at tree roots, consult the shrine-keeper before building an extension, & teach their children which mushrooms have spirits in them.
Glassians know their trade, their village, their local spirits & rumors from the last trader or harpy courier who came through. They don‚Äôt know politics or how magic works, but they‚Äôre not stupid. Everything they know works because it worked for the last thousand years.
Glassians are not neutral. Under competing warlords, commoners hedge between factions the way a trader diversifies debts. They hide grain, maintain polite relations with whoever‚Äôs currently armed, & gossip as intelligence-gathering. They will sell information for safety. Their children are simultaneously their retirement, labor force, & army.

Very proud of it.35FlintCloveInnkeeperPortable clay spirit-house36GaleSorrelTeahouse operatorSomeone else‚Äôs family seal; why?41HartTealShrine keeperRope, bundled42JemAlderSpirit-ward braiderOffering bowl & morning rice43KnoxCressMidwife & herbalistHerbs, bundled44LanDaleBee-keeperDeed to cottage & farm45NollElmPig-keeperLive belligerent chicken46PikeFlaxSalt-makerStolen military rations51RoanGlenRope-makerOld flute52TorIrisThatcherCeramic honey jug53VossMyrtleKiln-workerHand-drawn local map54WardSageHarpy-mail clerkIncense sticks for offerings55CadeWrenIron wholesalerSingle ancient tool, treated like gold56LorneOpalBath-house keeperBasket of fresh vegetables61SettBirchFestival organizerOut-of-season festival decorations62ThaneLeaMilitia drill leaderShield, salvaged & dented.63OakKitWall-menderBag of lime morite & trowel64MaceRueScavenger/rag-pickerHandful of bent nails & scrap metal65WrenNettieGrave-diggerSmall knife & whetstone66YewJuneNight-soil collectorNothing but worn, ragged clothes

Roll again for regular name. ‚ÄúAster Pearl.‚Äù5Deed. Something they or an ancestor did. ‚ÄúWard Bridgebuilder,‚Äù ‚ÄúFlint Pigkiller.‚Äù Almost never chosen; imposed by the community.6None. Follows hurricane season, surfaces during worst weather. Standard stats but always encountered during storms (‚àí2 to all nautical Saves). Pod numbering 3d6 includes several orca.6The Cartographer. Uniquely scarred whale known to every sea-faring faction. Sighting indicates specific deep-water currents. Harming it enrages every seafaring culture simultaneously. Grants +1 to navigation Saves if followed.

































1d6Why are we hunting whales?1Disputed Hunt. Two Coraler clans both claim to have struck the same whale first. Each demands the Party arbitrate or provide warriors. Losing clan will remember the slight.2Migration Chaos. Pod of 3d6 whales migrates through active naval battle or blockade. Creates 500-yard corridors of churning water. Ships must Save to avoid collision or breach their formation.3Contracted Harvest. A Faction hires the Party to secure a whale carcass‚Äîmust defend the kill site from rival whalers, scavengers, and the returning pod while processing takes 2-3 days.4The Revenge Hunt. Specific whale (roll on Legendary Whales table) destroyed someone important‚Äôs ship/family/livelihood. They‚Äôre offering a substantial bounty but the hunt requires open ocean.5Coraler Intelligence. Coraler military is trying to train/recruit a whale for war against Hydrean fleets. Party hired to help.6Beached Leviathan. Dying whale beached on contested coastline. Multiple factions converge to claim the carcass. Salvage rights unclear. Violence imminent.
@ Elementals
@ Fire Elemental





























Stats@@@@GroupCampfire/bush (HD 1) / Hut/tree (HD 3) / Cottage (HD 10)Desc.A hideous swirling mass or streaking firebolt of roaring & lapping flame.WantsIntellectMorality

Blazing Resistances: You are Immune to Fire Damage & Weak to Ice.
Ignite (Attack): Touched target ignites & takes [HD]d6 Fire Damage.
Explosion (Attack): You detonate, dealing [HD] Fire/Physical Damage to & igniting everything within a [HD]√ó10‚Äô radius.

Agitated fire elementals do not dissipate from lack of fuel. If they grow beyond 11 HD, they split. A Settlement on fire can be home to 5 times as many HD in fire elementals as there is Families, proportionate to the portion on fire.
Blaze





























StatsHD varies, BR N/A, Fly 10‚ÄôGroupCampfire/bush (HD 1) / Hut/tree (HD 3) / Cottage (HD 10)Desc.A roiling mass of flame.WantsFuel.IntellectNone. It is the simplest expression of the element of fire.MoralityHahaha.

Blazing Resistances: You are Immune to Fire Damage & Weak to Ice.

A blaze has 1 HD per Capacity (number of people the structure normally holds), proportionate to the percentage on fire, minimum 1 (i.e. a barrel or campfire).
When exposed to fuel, every 10 minutes, a blaze doubles its HD & gains HP equal to 4 √ó the HD it gained. Once 100% of the structure‚Äôs Capacity is on fire, it‚Äôs irrecoverable & the blaze loses half its HP per hour as it breaks down & consumes what‚Äôs left.
A blaze has no spirit to extract.
Treasure: None.
Time Fairy

Steal the Future: Take an extra Turn in combat instantly, but spend your next turn frozen, unable to act & vulnerable.

@ Blizzard
@ Foreign Beasts
@ Lion





























StatsHD 5 (20), BR 35, Size 10‚Äô, Weight 45#, Max. Load 30#, Run 50‚ÄôGroup1d6 hunting pack / 2d6 prideDesc.Giant colden cat. Yellow eyes. Low growl may warn before the pounce. Males have a mane & a lazy streak.WantsTerritory, prey, the pride‚Äôs dominance. To be recognized as king.IntellectSocial hunters. Complex group tactics. Males arrogant; females cunning.MoralityRegal brutality. Won‚Äôt attack what doesn‚Äôt threaten or feed the pride.

Claws & Teeth (Natural Weapon): 5 Physical.
Camouflage: You may always have surprise when initiating combat, provided cover availability.
Pounce: On a Charge, you may also perform a Grapple before you perform the Attack.
Claws & Teeth: You get an extra Strike per Attack made with a natural weapon.
Territorial Roar: Once per day as a Full-Turn Action, roar. All creatures within earshot (~1 mile) know a lion is present. Creatures of lesser Power within eyesight must Save or Panic until safe from lions.

Lions never roamed Glass, but some still use them for family heraldry. Despite their reputation, they only hunt isolated humans. They rarely travel far from their pride‚Äôs dens.
Treasure: Mane (male only, Value 5)





















1d6Lion variants1‚Äì3Lioness. HD 3 (12), Weight 30#, Max. Load 20#, Run 55‚Äô. No mane.4‚Äì5Male lion. Standard entry.6Old King. HD 5 (20), Weight 50#, Max. Load 33#. Scarred. Knows tactics.

































1d6Pride situation1Pride war. Two males fighting. Entering the area provokes both.2Hunting party. 3‚Äì5 females stalking prey. Will redirect to party if they seem weak.3Male on patrol, marking territory. Will challenge but not immediately attack.4Female with cubs, hidden. Won‚Äôt attack unless cubs discovered. Then: maximum violence.5Post-kill feast. Defending food. May allow observers to watch from distance.6Starving pride. Desperate. Will attack even well-armed parties.
@ Hippopotamus
@ Elephant
@ Pack Raptor
Ancient Artifacts
@ Slumberman
the remnants of the old civilization from before the Blooming. most of them still recall it. put you to sleep. seize control, possess you from up to a mile away. continually farm & consume cave lotus leaves (subterranean, extremely hard/slow to grow) to stay asleep. continually replenishes magical power while asleep. they built the templevaults. palaces of the mind.
cyclolucidites + mind flayers. ancient evil masterminds. ruled the world. slowly waking up.
@ Dwarf
the dwarves are gone. there is no such thing as a dwarf. you cannot see the dwarf. the dwarves do not exist. they are all gone. they are all dead. they are crawling in your blood. there are no dwarves here. you will leave because there is nobody watching you. you will not be followed and watched because they are all dead. you could bring them back. your hand moves by your own accord.
you should bring them back.

Something that is & always was unreal can still find a way to cast a shadow.

Imperial Court
True Immortal





























StatsHD 2d6 (8‚Äì24), BR ‚àû, Size 5‚Äô, Weight 60#, Max. Load 40#, Run 25‚Äô, Fly 250‚ÄôGroupSolo / Cult patron (Level 2d6, Godhead enables summoning)Desc.A radiant, hyper-real human, possibly disguised as a lesser being.WantsInscrutably mad whims fulfilled. An end to the ennui.IntellectBrilliant but ordinary human, augmented by magic or tools.MoralityTwisted into knots by uncountable & unaccountable aeons.

Hourai Immortal: You are a true immortal & are subject to the following conditions:

Your body replaces limbs, implants, Mutations, Wounds, new HD or Racial Features, & other abnormalities to perfectly restore itself to ordinary functionality within 24 hours of any damage or change being inflicted.
If killed, in 1d6 Rounds, you immolate into ash & reconstitute instantly in a burst of light & cold flame at a place of your choosing within 100‚Äô, displacing or destroying any material present if no empty space is available.
You are always conscious, even in death, & as a Free Action may choose to die & begin resurrecting.
Your soul is not in your body; it is beyond the cycle of life & death. No effect targeting a soul works on you.
You are still subject to all natural needs & vulnerabilities & have no special resistances to Damage, but you regenerate fast enough that only an absence of air will kill you.
This Feature can never be copied, borrowed, stolen, given, removed, inherited, overruled, etc. by any means whatsoever.



Even an undying force, so long as it be carved in the shape of Man, can be swayed & broken. A god with boundaries is one that can be defeated. A human plus eternity is a predictable cycle.
An immortal knows every trade, has seen all of history, knows a man‚Äôs inner character better than they do. You would have to be desperate or stupid to get in the way of an immortal ‚Ä¶ & there are plenty of desperate & stupid people in the world.
To generate an immortal, roll on the tables until you have a coherent picture. Pilfer liberally from other bestiary entries. Assume enormous competence, but play fairly. Immortals are still human & they aren‚Äôt always smart ‚Äî just very well-learned, with vast resources.
Treasure: A slain immortal may carry a treasure & have 1d4‚àí1 hidden elsewhere. It may not willingly relinquish them.

























































d66Current project11‚Äì13Trying to die for good. Heard there was a method. Testing it out. Collateral damage is mounting? Who cares?14‚Äì16Waiting for an event. Political confluence, meeting, stars‚Äô alignment. It was calculated centuries ago. Don‚Äôt distract me.21‚Äì23Brokering a treaty. Acting as a neutral party. Both sides hate the other & will pay happily for anyone who can corrupt the immortal or destroy the other side‚Äôs bargaining position.24‚Äì26Rebuilding. There used to be a monument here. A palace, a canal, a machine. All this will go, one way or another.31‚Äì33Hiding. May be on the run from the Lunar Princess (p. @). Scared badly enough that they won‚Äôt tell you what. You might die for discovering them.34‚Äì36Governing. Or rather, I‚Äôm just that good. They worship me. They do everything I say. What, you want something? Are you gonna get in the way?41‚Äì43Collecting. Every left shoe in the kingdom. All of the nobles‚Äô eyeballs. Every book containing an old king‚Äôs name.44‚Äì46Teaching a single mortal. He‚Äôs becoming dangerously powerful & terrifically unaccountable. Good!51‚Äì53Feuding with another neighboring immortal. They enjoy the game. Two kingdoms exist entirely to sustain the conflict. Every proximal relationship is another front.54‚Äì56Familicide. Systemically hunting down an entire bloodline across a continent.61‚Äì63Sleeping. Everyone is terrified of the awakening. Nobody remembers what this one was like.64‚Äì66Prolonging a war. Might be for entertainment; may be for a scheme. Might be testing the limits of a participant, or pressuring them.

























































d66Immortal abilities11‚Äì13True Teleportation: As a Major Action, instantly move to anywhere you can see or have been before.14‚Äì16Blood Memory: As a Major Action, shapeshift into any humanoid form you have previously touched. The disguise is physically perfect. Features are not mimicked.21‚Äì23Perfect Memory: You recall every face, word, & event you have ever witnessed. You can reproduce any document, map, or conversation verbatim. You cannot forget, even if you wish to.24‚Äì26Undeniable: You may choose to be impossible to ignore. At any time, no creature within line-of-sight or earshot can look away, leave, or take any action not directed at you.31‚Äì33Spirit Sight: You can see invisible entities, the structure of spirits, enchantments, illusions, & everything else as they truly are; nothing is hidden from your direct sight.34‚Äì36Compulsion of Truth: Anyone speaking directly to you cannot knowingly lie, even by omission. They may refuse to speak. This is not magic; it is the sheer weight of your attention.41‚Äì43Iron Fortitude: You do not tire, hunger, thirst, or need sleep, though you may choose to. You can march, work, or fight indefinitely without penalty.44‚Äì46Thousand Names: You possess valid, established identities in dozens of cultures, courts, & institutions across the Disc. You can arrive almost anywhere and be recognized as someone important, expected & trusted.51‚Äì53Prophecy: Once per month, you may ask the GM a single yes/no question about the future. The GM answers truthfully based on his understanding of current trajectories. The future is not fixed.54‚Äì56Master of All: You have mastered every mundane trade, art, and science known to civilization. Given materials & time, you can build or create anything a perfectionist master might.61‚Äì63Weather Controller: Command the weather within three miles‚Äô radius to anything desired with minutes of notice. Summon hurricanes, snowstorms, rainstorms, or tornadoes.64‚Äì66Pronounce Death: Once per day, speak aloud a character‚Äôs true name & the deadly fate they‚Äôll meet. If it‚Äôs possible that they meet it in the next 6 minutes & it affects nobody else, they meet it. No Save. Works on everything, including elementals.






































































d66DispositionSwirling legends11‚Äì13FuriousOnce sank a fleet by standing on the shore & screaming.14‚Äì16AmiableTaught the first salamanders how to smelt bronze.21‚Äì23IrritableCursed a bloodline for a thousand years over an insult at dinner.24‚Äì26InquisitiveHas asked every mortal they‚Äôve met the same question. No one has answered correctly yet. Nobody remembers the question.31‚Äì33DismissiveUsed to rule a kingdom. Left because it bored them. The kingdom still exists & claims them as patron.34‚Äì36PompousWrote the foundational legal code of a civilization that collapsed centuries ago. Still quotes it.41‚Äì43MelacholicWas last seen weeping over a grave so old the name has weathered away.44‚Äì46PlayfulRegularly disguises themselves as a beggar & tests the hospitality of settlements. Rewards & punishes accordingly.51‚Äì53ParanoidHas not been seen in decades. The last person who found them was returned alive but unable to speak.54‚Äì56GenerousGives gifts freely ‚Ä¶ but every gift creates an obligation. The immortal remembers every debt.61‚Äì63ColdSpeaks of mortal lives the way a farmer speaks of seasons.64‚Äì66ManicAlternates between frenzied activity & catatonic withdrawal on a cycle no one has been able to predict.

























































d66Peculiarities & proclivations11‚Äì13Genuinely all-loving. Would probably help if not already busy & if aid wouldn‚Äôt hurt anyone. Never helps for free.14‚Äì16Distant ancestor of a PC. Wants them to be more alike.21‚Äì23Recently became immortal. Doesn‚Äôt remember how. Wants ‚Äî needs ‚Äî to find out.24‚Äì26Trapped in an intangible state. Can only interact with world via Godhead (p. @). Would like to restore physical body.31‚Äì33Collects mortal apprentices. Teaches them everything, then abandons them at the peak of their ability. Former students are scattered across the world, many in positions of power, all with complicated feelings about their teacher.34‚Äì36Has fallen in love with a mortal. Knows they will die. This has happened before. It always ends the same way. The immortal does it anyway.41‚Äì43Maintains a personal archive of every text they‚Äôve ever read. Will do almost anything to acquire one they lack. Will kill to prevent one being destroyed.44‚Äì46Has sworn a binding oath to another immortal & regrets it. The terms constrain their actions in ways they refuse to explain.51‚Äì53Is playing both sides of the current conflict. Has real affection for people on each side. Cannot bring themselves to choose.54‚Äì56Lives as a mortal. New name, new trade, new town every few decades. Currently deep in a life they care about, with a spouse, children, neighbors who will grow old & die. Reacts to exposure/recognition the way a cornered animal reacts to a torch.61‚Äì63Convinced it‚Äôs being tested by an older, more powerful immortal. Can‚Äôt prove it. Paranoia manifests as irrational behavior.64‚Äì66Ridiculously formal. Demands letters & formal proclamations. Keeps a massive calendar of debts, which substitutes for a moral compass.

























































d66Immortal treasures11‚Äì13Apple of Mania. Everyone who lays eyes upon it desires it. The desire is irrational, immediate, equal in strength to the character‚Äôs greatest desire. & does not diminish with distance or time. The immortal is immune only by familiarity.14‚Äì16The Last Letter. A sealed document from someone who died. Never opened, never will be. Not dangerous for what it says; dangerous for what the immortal will do if it is threatened or destroyed.21‚Äì23Unbreakable Chain. A completely indestructible 244‚Äô chain of thin adamantine. It weighs 1#.24‚Äì26The Antidote. A cure for the current worst thing. The immortal won‚Äôt use it because the last time, the consequences were worse than the disease.31‚Äì33The Elixir. The singular source of Hourai Immortality. Intended to be delivered to a specific person as a gift. One use; the entire container must be consumed.34‚Äì36Hunting Horn. If blown loudly, this immortal will immediately appear. It‚Äôs a voluntary thing. It will respect the horn.41‚Äì43Necklace of Ears. Literally made from severed ears. The wearer can understand every language, including animal emotions, but not speak them.44‚Äì46Spare Fate. A single slim red thread. Can be sacrificed to miraculously invert a fate that has just been sealed ‚Äî a king‚Äôs declaration of war, a love lost, your own death.51‚Äì53Tianshima. A brass & redwood treasure junk, sans sails. Given a crew of 20, it can sail through the sky. Holds 6,000# in cargo. One-of-a-kind anomaly. Currently in use by the immortal.54‚Äì56Small metal tablet. Hand-sized, scuffed. Glassy panel on one surface; if touched, a trapped fairy mathematician appears to provide guidance.61‚Äì63Martyr‚Äôs Bracelet. Jagged thorns dig into skin. If an ally within line-of-sight or 1,000‚Äô would suffer a negative effect, you may transfer its full effect onto yourself.64‚Äì66Respect. This immortal is borderline-undefeated. If you can kill it, it will perform a single favor for you to the spirit of your word & the limits of its abilities.


                  
                  When Eaten 
                  
                

Flavor: Bitter pork. Degenerates gradually into ash over 24 hours.
Notes: The Hourai Immortal Feature cannot be obtained, but other Features rolled on the Abilities table can be gained via the normal consumption rules. Even still, the immortal will reconstitute, remember who ate them, & have opinions about it.


Lunarians
The Lunarians do not have an ‚Äúeconomy‚Äù & are not best understood as a Faction, but rather instead as a den of powerful spirits (the Royal Family) overseeing a collection of pets (the moon rabbits) in a kind of post-scarcity bubble society (the Lunar Kingdom) on a bizarre & hostile world (the Moon) full of their scattered, experimental, discarded, or disabled weaponry & utilities (the Lunarian flora & fauna, plus their artifacts).
The moon rabbits rarely deal with the Lunar ecosystem directly. They have spirits who keep the worst of the Moon‚Äôs hazards away from their villages.
Moon Rabbit Operator





























StatsHD 1 (4), BR 12, Size 5‚Äô, Weight 12#, Max. Load 8#, Run 35‚ÄôGroup1d6+1 away team / 3d6 squadDesc.Neotenous human with rabbit ears, tail & legs. Always dressed in camouflage or (bad) disguise suitable for the environment.WantsVegetables. Not to be discovered.IntellectFollows orders with such literal precision it loops back to stupidity.MoralityThe mission is morally correct because it is the mission.

Fists (Natural Weapon): 2 Physical.
Jade Pestle (Weapon): 1/4#, 1H, 4 Physical.
Ray Gun (Weapon): 1#; 2H; 15 Fire/Ice.
Ray Shield (Equipment): 1/4#. Immune to all Damage until struck by an effect that deals 6 or more Damage, at which point the shield gem instantly shatters, destroying the item (& fully absorbing that particular effect‚Äôs Damage).
Moon Telepathy: You are Immune to Mind Damage. You can broadcast & receive speech as if talking to everyone with Moon Telepathy up to at most a 1-mile radius. You can dim the range or mute your ability to hear other broadcasts.
Rabbit Instincts: You must Save to not Panic from unexpected loud noises, dogs, or eagles (outside ongoing combat). You must Save to not investigate interesting smells.
Bound: Once per Turn as a Free Action, leap up to 15‚Äô in any direction.
Rabbit Guard‚Äôs Feet: You never make sounds while moving. You can jump hard enough to move between the Moon & Aia (takes 1 day) provided that a landing point on the Moon (or Aia) is currently visible. You cannot reach anywhere else with this ability.

The Lunar Kingdom hates everyone on Aia. If moon rabbits show up at your doorstep, you are being incompetently assassinated.
Moon rabbits are excellent at holding defensive positions because they naturally freeze when threatened.
Treasure: Jade name tag ($30), 2d6 cakes.

































1d6Current Mission1Retrieve a lunar artifact. They have a sketch but cannot identify it. Will attempt to take anything vaguely similar back to the Moon, because they are all tired. The artifact has a 3-in-6 chance of even existing nearby & is extremely dangerous & powerful if it does.2Observe and report. They are observing. They have been observing for three weeks. Nobody told them what to report or how & everyone‚Äôs afraid to ask.3Secure the perimeter. They have formed a perfect circle around something strategically valuable & will not let anyone cross it, including each other. They are now split across both sides of their own perimeter.4Punish the random Aian who offended the Princess. They have a name but no description. They will ask every person they meet ‚ÄúAre you [name]?‚Äú5Training exercise. You are part of the training simulation & are the targets.6Lost. Their commander received contradictory orders and they are now marching in a perfect holding pattern until resolution.
@ Moon Rabbit





























StatsHD 1 (4), BR 0.5, Size 5‚Äô, Weight 12#, Max. Load 8#, Run 35‚ÄôGroup1d6+1 fluffle / 3d6√ó10 warrenDesc.Neotenous human with rabbit ears, tail & legs, friendly smile. Light, casual, brightly-patterned clothes.WantsVegetables. To be sociable. Gossip & rumors. To cause trouble & confusion for fun.IntellectVery literal. Diligent & systemic. Prone to logician‚Äôs fallacies.MoralityLaid-back & easy-going. Genuinely well-intentioned.

Fists (Natural Weapon): 2 Physical.
Moon Telepathy: You are Immune to Mind Damage. You can broadcast & receive speech as if talking to everyone with Moon Telepathy up to at most a 1-mile radius. You can dim the range or mute your ability to hear other broadcasts.
Bound: Once per Turn as a Free Action, leap up to 15‚Äô in any direction.
Rabbit Instincts: You must Save to not Panic from unexpected loud noises, dogs, or eagles (outside ongoing combat). You must Save to not investigate interesting smells.

Unchanging aeons of relative ease in Lunar cities has divorced them from the realities of life. They know almost nothing about Aia & are astonished (& easily entranced) by stories of war, conquest, famine, charisma, tournaments, politics, monster-slaying, etc. They will follow interesting people around asking lots of questions & then broadcast the answers to everyone.
Moon rabbits are immortal. On the Moon, they have a half-life of around 500 years.
Treasure: Jade coins (3d6 √ó $10).

































1d6What they want from you1Stories about Aia. Will trade local information for entertainment. 3-in-6 chance they immediately tell everyone everything.2Help with task. Needs something fetched from dangerous/restricted area. Doesn‚Äôt understand why it‚Äôs dangerous. ‚ÄúCould you get this from the vault? I‚Äôm not allowed in there but YOU don‚Äôt work here so it should be fine.‚Äù3Vegetables from Aia. Will pay absurd amounts of jade coins for normal carrots.4Opinion on dispute. Two rabbits are having an argument that‚Äôs been going on for three months. They want outside perspective. The argument is deeply stupid.5Introduction to interesting person. Heard a rumor about someone (PC or NPC) and wants to meet them. Will drag you to them OR drag them to you, whichever is less convenient.6Nothing yet, but‚Ä¶ Following you around because you‚Äôre the most interesting thing to happen in weeks. Broadcasting running commentary on everything you do to all nearby rabbits. The Royal Guards will hear within hours.

































1d6The useful information they can offer1Guard rotations: Knows when Royal Guards change shifts, which routes they take, which ones are more alert. Learned this through decades of watching.2Secret passages: ‚ÄúOh, that wall? It‚Äôs not really a wall. You just push on the third crystal from the left.‚Äù Has no idea this is supposed to be secret.3Current gossip: Knows where everyone important is right now and what they‚Äôre doing. Updates in real-time via telepathy network.4Artifact locations: ‚ÄúThat thing you‚Äôre looking for? I think it‚Äôs in the Eastern Vault. Or maybe the Archive? One of those buildings that glows purple.‚Äù5Cultural taboos: Can explain what will DEFINITELY get you caught. ‚ÄúDon‚Äôt touch the statues. The Princess gets really mad. Last time someone did that she petrified an entire village.‚Äù6How things work: Can explain lunar technology in terms that are technically accurate but utterly useless. ‚ÄúThe door opens when it recognizes you. How does it recognize you? It looks at you. With its eyes. The crystal eyes. They‚Äôre in the frame.‚Äù

































1d6Rabbit problems1Lost something important. ‚ÄúI‚Äôll get in trouble! I mean, it‚Äôs not that important, but it‚Äôs in a restricted area. Could you help me? I‚Äôll help you get in if you‚Äôll help me.‚Äù2Witnessed something unintended. Royal Guard moving artifact, classified gossip, mysterious break-in. Desperately want to tell people but I can‚Äôt, so I‚Äôm telling you.3Accidentally broke something. ‚ÄúDon‚Äôt tell my supervisor! I didn‚Äôt mean to! I don‚Äôt care if it‚Äôs tiny! Help me replace it, or get him to stop looking or something!‚Äú4In love with someone inappropriate. Another rabbit, a Royal Guard, possibly one of the PCs. Their telepathic yearning is embarrassing everyone.5Caught between orders. ‚ÄúI can‚Äôt do A because then I‚Äôm not doing B, but I can‚Äôt do B because then I‚Äôm not doing A!‚Äú6Bored beyond measure. Has done the same job for 300 years. Will absolutely help you do anything illegal if it‚Äôs interesting. Doesn‚Äôt understand consequences because nothing consequential has happened to them ever.

































1d6Rabbit jobs @1Mochi production specialist. Pounds rice all day. Very strong opinions about mochi texture.2@3Agricultural tender. Grows the deeply weird lunar crops. @4Archive assistant. Files paperwork that no one will ever read. Has access to restricted information but doesn‚Äôt realize it‚Äôs restricted.5Messenger runner. Carries physical messages between locations that are within telepathic range. The job only still exists for bureaucratic reasons.6Guard. Stands places. Not actually capable of stopping anyone. Has never encountered an actual security threat and would not know what to do.





















































2d6Today‚Äôs rumor2THE PRINCESS IS HAVING ANOTHER PARTY! She does this every month.3Hey, there‚Äôs some weird foreigners here! This is you. You have 10 minutes to an hour before the guards show up.4Someone broke into one of the vaults over at [exact vault location] yesterday. 1-in-6 chance it‚Äôs another Aian interloper.5The Guard is looking for [powerful magical item], if anyone misplaced it! Someone had a really awful reason to borrow a very dangerous tool.6Someone really screwed up. A local jobber (see jobs table) had a problem.7Did you hear about [rabbit]‚Äòs promotion? Banal, pointless gossip.8[Rabbit name] is in love with [guard name]! Both parties mortified.9Something fell from space recently! Meteor, dead bird, celestial horror.10The Archive is doing inventory today! 3-in-6: restricted areas are especially active and watched. Else, areas are totally unwatched & chaotic.11The Princess is angry! Everyone starts panicking & hyperventilating over anything that could possibly be a problem, especially strange foreigners.12Something strange last night happened in the sky! Meteor shower, visible volcanic eruption on Aia, perfectly clear night on the Moon.

Book-1---Glass-Empires/{-Appendix-A-}---.title: { Appendix A } - Mutations & Wounds
Book-1---Glass-Empires/{-Appendix-A-}---.content:
Mutations
When a character suffers a Mutation:

The GM rolls 1d6 to choose the target nature & effects of the Mutation, shifting down the list if the rolled result is inapplicable:

1‚Äì3: The mutating effect‚Äôs description or source, if any.
4‚Äì5: The characteristics of the victim themselves.
6: The characteristics of the immediate environment surrounding the victim.


The GM rolls twice on the Mutations & Wounds Table (p. @) to determine Location, and chooses the most thematic of the two rolled results, discarding the other.
The GM determines the nature & effects of the Mutation based on the words & location & writes a description for the new Fact representing it.

Mutations are never inherently lethal & they shouldn‚Äôt prohibit continued play for PCs, but they can be very unpleasant ‚Ä¶ or quite helpful.
Wounds
When you receive a Wound, generate its location by rolling on the Mutation Location table. If you don‚Äôt have the location in question, stop; you don‚Äôt receive a Wound! Otherwise, perform an unmodifed Wound Save (straight 2d6 with no modifiers).





























ResultWound SaveCrit‚â•12You don‚Äôt actually suffer a Wound. You‚Äôre fine!Pass8‚Äì11The body part is merely injured & is unusable until the Wound heals.Seven7You permanently lose half your capacity (i.e. weak/unsteady leg, half your breath).Failure3‚Äì6The body part is destroyed.Fumble‚â§2The body part is destroyed. Roll for a second Wound location.
If the body part of the Wound isn‚Äôt destroyed, then whenever you perform the Rest Move (p. @), cross-reference the Rest Threshold for a period longer than that, the Wound is healed.

If you roll ‚ÄúGain additional‚Ä¶‚Äù while checking for a Wound, the Wound affects two or more parts; make a Wound Save for each part‚Äôs destruction separately. If you roll it for a Mutation, the Mutation adds an additional body part (if applicable).
If you don‚Äôt have the named body part, a Mutation will add it while a Wound will do nothing & be negated.

This is where the condensed versions of your theoretical frameworks live ‚Äî not the full Bronze Age Optimization Trap essay, but the core insights distilled into actionable GM principles.

the importance of first-principles axioms
power always optimizes
Classes are Socioeconomic Niches
Glass Empires is About Power, Not Economics

Book-2---Gamemaster's-Guide/{-4-}---Enco.title: { 4 } - Encounters & Locations @
Book-2---Gamemaster's-Guide/{-4-}---Enco.content:
@ Locations
You should create a 1d6 random table of at least 6 distinct Locations for each distinct area, biome, or region in play. This table is used for small, hidden/secretive, or outwardly unremarkable landmarks or objects that are flexible in placement.


                  
                  Tip
                  
                

By not marking a location-independent Location ahead of time, you can minimize prep work required to start playing & simply regenerate the list as it gets used up.


Locations can be a variety of things, but are generally small, hidden/secretive, or outwardly unremarkable landmarks or objects. Examples may include but aren‚Äôt limited to small castles/forts, the homes of colorful NPCs, graveyards or battle sites, merchants or artisans of particular character, dungeons, secret treasures, ruins, potential settlement sites, or locations of intense immaterial value/significance to one or more existing worldly Factions.
You only need a new separate table for each area that has a wildly distinct character in terms of what can be found there (i.e. deserts vs. tundras vs. jungles). Major differences in cultures (i.e. cultures whose homes are built underground) may also justify the creation of a separate table.
Whenever the game requires that a new & unmarked Location be brought into play (i.e. as a consequence of the Travel Move), roll on your Locations table for that area to figure out what Location in question is the topic. From that point onward, the Location is marked as being at the spot where it was discovered. Remove it from your table & replace the entry with a new Location.
Generator: Random Locations
The GM may use this Location generator if he has no Random Locations table suitable for a given region, area, or biome, or to generate new entries to fill out a region-specific table.

Roll d66 on the Location table & then on the Location Status table, then determine Faction ownership.
If the location/status result is nonsense for the area & you can‚Äôt figure out how to marry it to the fiction, discard the Location. Nothing is found.

Random Locations may not be immediately actionable, but they always stick around. Mark them on the map after they‚Äôre discovered.
Locations Table























































































































d66Locationd66Location11Watchtower. Flimsy. Oversees local area.41Military encampment. Palisades, towers, ordered layout.12Shrine. A random Spirit is housed inside.42Hunting camp. Skins, smoking grills, tents & dog-posts.13Mining camp. Extracts something valuable.43Mass grave. Dozens or hundreds dead, poorly honored.14Walled manor. Small hamlet & local nobleman.44Ancient settlement. Reduced mostly to rubble.15Farmstead. Makes something extremely weird.45Castle ruin. Broken hulk hidden in landscape.16Tower. De jure owns (& overlooks) the local land.46Natural cave. Hidden, large, good for ambushing the Route.21Village. Makes an interesting trade good, but doesn‚Äôt export it.51Bend in the road. Excellent for army pincers.22Graveyard. Ancient walled field of gravestones.52Hill. Renowned for historical event. Good vantage point.23Ruined temple. A forgotten Spirit dwells here.53Valley/gully. Good for surprising anyone on the Route.24Obelisk. A carved pillar to heaven. Possibly older than the earth.54Statue. Possessed by protective, vengeful or trapped Spirit.25Crypt. An old tomb, crypt, or necropolis.55Gigantic monolith. Might be a natural formation.26Rocky outcrop. Good for secretly bombarding the Route from.56Carved cliff face. Writings or a face. Vaguely rude.31Blessed ground. Natural space warped by local Spirit.61Standing circle. A mythical ring of trees or stones.32Trading post. Finds resources from nearby area & sells to travelers.62Fountain. A spring of fresh water. Local possessive Spirit taints it.33Fortified bridge. Difficult to cross obstacle without it. Charges a toll.63Abyssal hole. Plummets into the veins of the earth.34Great wall. Runs for miles. Now largely undefended.64Staircase. Unmarked. Leads down into the earth.35Quarry. Critical for nearest Faction‚Äôs local project.65Pilgrimage site. Regularly visited. Sacred to locals. Random Spirit.36Workshop. Occupied by local master artisan.66Cloister. Occupied by secluded single-gender community.
