Determining Faction Ownership
Locations are generally owned. The GM decides one of the following on the spot based on narrative logic:

If only a single major Faction would plausibly claim this: It‚Äôs a Lv1d6 Subfaction of theirs.
If multiple Factions would or did fight over it: Assign odds to a d6 & roll to determine owner, contestants, etc.
If it‚Äôs too remote, worthless, hidden, or obscure: It‚Äôs a new independent Lv1d6 Faction.
If unsure: It‚Äôs abandoned.

Book-2---Gamemaster's-Guide/{-5-}---Runn.filePath: Book 2 - Gamemaster's Guide/{ 5 } - Running a Braunstein @.md
Book-2---Gamemaster's-Guide/{-5-}---Runn.title: { 5 } - Running a Braunstein @
Book-2---Gamemaster's-Guide/{-5-}---Runn.content:
@ Currently the Braunstein appendix, expanded. Periods, phases, diplomacy, order execution, conflict resolution, communication rules, multiple-GM coordination. This is its own campaign mode and needs dedicated treatment.

Glass Empires is built to be managed either as a traditional RPG campaign featuring a party of Players whose Party is based on the premise of working together to achieve a larger goal set out at the start of a campaign or as what is known as a Braunstein.
A Braunstein is an open-ended scenario featuring Player vs. Player conflict under a fog of war with simultaneous order execution in a scenario that pits participants against each other. The role of a GM (of which there may be more than one) in a Braunstein is to organize players, manage information & resolve conflicts in order execution while maintaining the world in a way that exacerbates the tensions & conflicts that forms the heart of a Braunstein.
What makes a Braunstein?

Conflict between players. The GM must engineer an initial scenario wherein players have a reason to interact & potentially fight over resolutions to their problems. A ‚Äúhard‚Äù solution is to design a scenario wherein not all players can win; where the world is at least in some way zero-sum.
Asymmetric capabilities. Players who have variable assets have something to trade, and negotiation is the lifeblood of a game like this.

Setup
The GM should arrange for the following:

A public space wherein all Players may communicate with each other;
Private channels wherein Players may privately communicate with selected other Players;
A means of conveying announcements, such as the outcome of issued orders;
A selection of times wherein GMs can gather Players whose orders conflict in such a way that requires they be present at an ad-hoc session to resolve the conflict.

An instant messaging server & a virtual tabletop platform is perfect for both of these.
Player Communication
All Players may freely communicate with each other via direct conversations (in text or voice). No Player may provide proof of conversations or statements (i.e. via screenshot), although if both players agree, a written document can be created as an artifact as part of written orders submitted to the GM.
Periods & Phases
The game is played out as a series of Periods representing a certain amount of real-world & in-game time. By default, 1 Period is:

1 week of in-game time;
Up to 1 week in real-world time, depending on how long it takes for each Player to issue orders to the GM(s).

Phase Structure
There are two real-world Phases that occur during each Period: Diplomacy & Execution.
Diplomacy Phase
During the Diplomacy Phase, Players may scheme, communicate, and otherwise talk among each other in public or private. In addition, throughout this phase, each Player determines & issues private written orders for their actions during this Period to the GM.
Until the Phase ends, players can edit or override orders they previously set in.
Diplomacy ends when all Players issue orders or it has been 1 real-world week since the start of the Phase. Players who haven‚Äôt issued orders by the end of the Phase are assumed to do nothing other than defend themselves during the Execution Phase.


                  
                  Why can we communicate when our PCs shouldn't be able to? 
                  
                

Because players may always have communication with each other in order to facilitate role-playing & engagement in the game, the Diplomacy Phase does not necessarily correlate to actual conversations that happen in-game. Instead, this phase represents:


Public knowledge being shared, such as rumors or leaks about behaviors or events being shared between players through intermediaries such as courtiers, friends, or allies;
Past conversations held at an oppoirtunity
Strategic wargaming or intuitive reasoning performed by both sides not through talking but rather by reasoning out in private what the other members of the conversation would do in any given situation.

Figure whichever one suits the situation. The important part is that no orders, agreements, or material exchanges can happen during the Diplomacy Phase. Only (verbal, non-binding) agreements can be made, and in order for actions to be executed upon, each Player must explicitly issue orders to a GM that would facilitate those agreements or exchanges, such as by sending messengers to the other parties involved in the agreement(s). The only exception to this rule is if the Players‚Äô PCs are in close in-game proximity to each other during the Diplomacy Phase, and even then, both must still issue orders to the GM explaining their actions in order to execute upon it.

Execution Phase
During the Execution Phase, the GM(s) analyze all written orders received during Diplomacy & determines what happens during the actual Period. In particular, the GM figures out if any of the Players‚Äô orders results in a collision or potential point of conflict that must be resolved before the next Phase can begin.
The Execution Phase lasts as long as is required for the GM(s) to find all points of conflict that would occur during the in-game Period, bring the affected Players together in order to resolve those conflict(s) (i.e. via ad-hoc sessions to host battles or in-character discussions, text chats, or whatever other methods are required), & then declare the results of all orders to all parties that would have heard of them.
The Execution Phase ends as soon as the GM(s) are finished resolving all conflicts & issuing new information to the Players.
Order Formatting
Orders are a sequence of Moves that are undertaken by the Player‚Äôs PC over the course of the in-game Period. Players can only order their own PC to take actions. PCs can delegate via communication with Retainers (to order Subfactions) or courtiers (to order their directly-ruled Factions).
The total action economy for each PC is, therefore, down to how many Retainers/Factions they can issue orders to in-game in addition to their PC‚Äôs own personal time that can be dedicated towards a Move during the Period.
Players must provide a strict order of operations for their orders. Otherwise, orders will be executed in the order received by the GM.
Players may specify breaks or conditional reasoning based on the outcome of orders.
Other than for these rules, there is no strict formatting on what orders can be issued so long as the orders are coherent enough to be understood by the GM.
Special Conflict Cases

Two player-led armies march to same location ‚Äî who has initiative? Is there a battle, or does one arrive first?

GM determines which army becomes aware of the other first, & during the execution phase provides this information to the Player in control of that army. The Player may make decisions based on that information within the scope of the conflict.
If no PC was directly leading an involved army, the GM has the ability to adjudicate how the NPCs in command react according to the data they receive at the time when they receive it.
They have a battle if one or both sides can force it & want to force it given the world scenario. The GM has the information to adjudicate an outcome.


Mutual assassination attempts

Strictly speaking, ‚Äúassassination‚Äù isn‚Äôt something the game actually has rules for! This is a good point & something to fix.


A player wants to discover a piece of information & then decide what to do upon receiving it.

GM determines when during the in-game Period the PC would uncover the information, and then (after resolving any preceding conflicts) describes the scenario to the Player at the time of their discovery of the information. The GM may set a time limit for the Player to react beforehand.


Racing for the same NPC faction‚Äôs allegiance

Who gets there first? Who has the most compelling offer?


Spy/counter-spy collisions

Again, not something we really have rules for ‚Äî infiltration. But it‚Äôd fall out similarly to the player-led army example.



Special Notes
The Negotiation rules (p. @) don‚Äôt apply when dealing with other PCs.
Written Contracts
Players can agree to create written contracts. In-game violation of contracts might Please or Anger NPC Factions or Subfactions who have an interest in the upholding of law (or in the stakes at play regarding the contract‚Äôs contents).
Asset Transfers
You have to literally issue orders to give another PC items. This might come down to a GM-resolved conflict or negotiation period.
Fog of War Rules
Much of this comes down to the GM determining what PCs would have the opportunity to observe or see during the Period‚Äôs events.

What if players want to negotiate or trade in-person?

They can arrange to meet up during Diplomacy, issue orders to do so, and then have a meeting (with or without GM mediation, depending on if anything happens in the negotiation that demands GM presence to resolve).


What do players learn about other players‚Äô actions at period end?

‚ÄúAs much as they could observe, and nothing outside that unless the players in question choose to share it.‚Äù


How do spy networks/intelligence gathering work as orders?

Again, espionage is not something we‚Äôve rules for, which is bad since it will come up literally instantly.


What triggers discovery of covert actions?

See above.


The Panopticon presumably gives Arcades special information ‚Äî what exactly?

The controller of the Panopticon can see armies, towns, fleets, & castles as per the Hills visibility level across the entirety of Aia & much of the surrounding Rim ocean.



NPC Faction behavior
All Factions that aren‚Äôt PC-controlled are ordinarily managed by the GM. This includes Subfactions managed by PC Retainers. A GM may delegate decision-making for a PC‚Äôs Subfaction to that Player if the Subfaction in question is compliant or has no reason to rebel against that Player.

Book-2---Gamemaster's-Guide/{-6-}---Cust.filePath: Book 2 - Gamemaster's Guide/{ 6 } - Custom & Optional Rules.md
Book-2---Gamemaster's-Guide/{-6-}---Cust.title: { 6 } - Custom & Optional Rules
Book-2---Gamemaster's-Guide/{-6-}---Cust.content:
@ GM-facing rules on building custom Classes, Features, Origins, et cetera.
@ Custom Classes
@ Custom Magical Schools
Magicians mechanically consist of a Magical School possessing a mixture of Class Features & a Spell Tradition. The means & methods by which these are obtained varies.
In narrative terms, magicians have a philosophy that encompasses the way they see & interact with the world. The art of dealing with spirits is intrinsically tied to your mindset as a practitioner of magic.
Class Features
Magician Class Features tend to be double-edged swords relating to their nature in reality.
chosen via method:

Linear
Random
Choice of selection

Magical Traditions
Each magical school has a tradition of 12 spells based on the most fundamental aspects of how they interact with & view the world.
Traditions represent minor spirits; grease elementals, the spirit of an echo, a newborn god with nothing to offer. These spirits are common & easy to locate, or else they deliberately seek out magicians of the appropriate mentality & presentation.
Most magicians can capture & sell these traditional spells fairly easily. It takes about a month of effort. It is not a tremendously profitable market.
More complex, powerful & dangerous spells come from the beast markets or from hunting.
@ Custom Features
@ Custom Monsters
@ Custom Worlds
@ Optional: Tech Levels

Book-2---Gamemaster's-Guide/{-Appendix-A.filePath: Book 2 - Gamemaster's Guide/{ Appendix A } - Design Essays @.md
Book-2---Gamemaster's-Guide/{-Appendix-A.title: { Appendix A } - Design Essays @
Book-2---Gamemaster's-Guide/{-Appendix-A.content:
@ A series of design essays that guided the development of Glass Empires.
The Existence of Literal Genies
In a world where gods:

Exist;
Have limited time & attention;
Can be contacted from anywhere;
Have many worshipers making frivolous pleas;
Require those worshipers to stay loyal for any reason;

Gods will be blamed for failing to show up when a petitioner is in need. This can potentially cascade & prohibit gods of exceptional power whose rituals allow perfectly unambiguous contact & information transmission methods to become increasingly at risk against smaller, more local competitors who can serve needs quickly, accurately & at length.
For example, imagine Zeus as the Father of all Gods being pulled every which way across the Mediterranean, especially if we assume he physically exists & cannot manifest on-the-spot with fast-as-light movement. If he communicated by literally manifesting before you & saying ‚ÄúNo,‚Äù he would be at a disadvantage because it would be very obvious whenever he didn‚Äôt show up. Humans are far more spiteful over a clear failure or refusal than for a miscommunication or ambiguity.
The peasant cannot see the volume of requests & worldy complexity that Zeus faces. What he thinks instead is: ‚ÄúI cannot reach Zeus. I sacrificed that goat on his altar for NOTHING. I have been ROBBED.‚Äù He becomes a very angry customer.
As a result, gods may deliberately introduce contact rituals that are opaque & noisy so that interpretation becomes a difficult art. This minimizes points of contact & potentially creates false signals which works in favor of confirmation bias. A 50% false signal feels like only a 25% error rate, assuming that a false signal is correct 50% of the time ‚Äî & humans are exceptionally bad at handling or understanding probabilistic outcomes.
A Simplified Economic Trade Model
Rather than emulate the inputs & outputs of every Location in the game & their necessary demands & productive centers, we can observe an essential tenant key to any population: Everyone must be economically sustainable.
There must be inputs & outputs. Furthermore, traders will optimize for functionality.
If we assume that the system is stable & functional ‚Äî which is a big caveat, especially during times of war & unrest ‚Äî then we can simplify trade into something resembling the following approximate structure:

You buy Trade Goods at a Settlement. You have a target Settlement in mind.
If sold at that place, it produces a random but usually profitable profit margin based on distance.
If sold elsewhere, it produces a random profit margin that is weighted lower, such that it is usually not profitable accounting for risk.

Consider that this system has at least the following flaws:

You can‚Äôt capture anomalies in production level.
You can‚Äôt capture disruptions in other risk factors that don‚Äôt directly relate to distance. What if the only route is exceptionally dangerous? What if it‚Äôs safe & stable?
You can‚Äôt capture obvious similarities in inputs/outputs. What if a village has identical needs to the original destination?

Simplifications fail to capture edge cases as per usual. (We might note that arguably, the areas where a simplification fails are more interesting than anything else.) In any case, I believed that this framework is only suitable for a game where detailed economic & trade warfare is not expected to play a prominent role ‚Äî in fact, that it may be best left out altogether, then, to avoid distracting players with the promise of something that isn‚Äôt meant to be a meaningful component. Why over-complicate matters?
Glass Empires‚Äô Faction System Only Works for Pre-industrial Societies
The only reason the implicit economic simulation in Glass Empires functions is because we have made massive simplifications based on an implicit model of human society. Most critically, we assume that human population has a tight relationship to economic productivity. This is the key assumption that the Industrial Age rips to shreds.
In addition, we assume that population centers correspond tightly to power, which is why we can get away with assuming a Capital‚Äôs population is reflective of the rest of its power. This is true for almost all of history but decouples more extensively the more industrialized a state becomes.
Our assumptions are also dependent on a relatively inefficient tribal power structure. And in addition to real-world tribal & factional factors, Aia has several compounding confounding factors that prohibit centralization of power:

Absence of true firearms keeps cheap stone fortifications viable
Highly decentralized mail & courier service courtesy of the local bird-brains prohibits rulers from monopolizing high-speed communications;
Hostile & dangerous wilderness teeming with inexplicable & deadly magic provides continual ‚Äúshatterzone‚Äù pressures from nearly every wild region;
Realms artificially sustained through a single ruler‚Äôs combat & magical prowess often struggles to survive a succession crisis;
And more still!

In the context of Aia, none of this is a problem. The Grand Disc is not yet near an industrial revolution ‚Ä¶ although if you ran out the clock another couple hundred years, it might get there. But if you set about deploying the Glass Empires ruleset in another setting, be careful of the assumptions you import.
It is worth keeping in mind as a GM that the Lunarians (p. @) do not operate on a clear-cut economy at all & that attempting to model their existence using the Faction system is a terribly flawed route to pursue. They are rather like an odd endgame bonus stage, in this sense.

Book-2---Gamemaster's-Guide/{-Appendix-B.filePath: Book 2 - Gamemaster's Guide/{ Appendix B } - The Moon.md
Book-2---Gamemaster's-Guide/{-Appendix-B.title: { Appendix B } - The Moon
Book-2---Gamemaster's-Guide/{-Appendix-B.content:
Getting to the Moon

Kill a Moon Rabbit Operator, steal its Rabbit Guard‚Äôs Feet Feature with alchemy, and then jump to the Moon. This is extremely risky & is usually a one-way trip because the Moon is under perpetual cloud cover. Once you pass through the clouds, Aia isn‚Äôt visible until you can climb to the top of a Lunarian crystal spire or one of the mountains that penetrates the cloud cover, which means you can‚Äôt return even if you brought a spare to return with.
Find a Lunarian Wormhole & pass through it, and then escape detection & expulsion by the Lunarian Royal Guard. This is extremely risky because the Lunarians keep extremely tight watch over every wormhole they operate specifically to prevent Aian creatures from slipping through.
Build a magical contraption & fly to the Moon. Not impossible, but very unlikely & difficult. I don‚Äôt really know what kind of engineering would be required to achieve this. It‚Äôs certainly beyond any mortal ability.
Magic. A sufficiently powerful spirit of movement could likely teleport you to the Moon in a manner not dissimilar to the moon rabbit operators.

Leaving the Moon

Tell the Lunarians you‚Äôre there. If the Lunarian Royal Family learns that an Aian made it to the Moon, they will happily strip you of everything from the Moon you possess & kick you butt-naked out of one of their wormholes to freeze to death in a distant wilderness. It‚Äôs not a dignified or easy method of exit, but they will let you go.
Kill a Moon Rabbit Operator, steal its lunar hop & climb a crystal spire. The Lunarian towers that penetrate the cloud cover are the launching points for rabbit operators that get deployed to Aia. During the lunar apex, anyone that can match its hop can reach any point on Aia.

Lunar Landscape
Aia‚Äôs Moon is a strange & unpleasant landscape for anything not born there.
Half the Moon is blanketed in a nearly perpetual night, as the Moon is situated further from Aia than the Sun is, and therefore never sees light on the side facing away from Aia. Even if you‚Äôre not on the dark side of the Moon, the entire Moon has a nearly-constant cloud cover that rarely dissipates, granting only very brief glimpses of direct sunlight.
There is an extremely powerful & omnipresent curvature visible in all directions, because the Moon is only ~300 miles in diameter. This creates a ‚Äúclaustrophobic horizon‚Äù effect. You can only see around half a mile into the distance on flat terrain, around 40 times shorter than on Aia‚Äôs surface (where on a clear day, one can see for maybe 20 miles). Everywhere you stand, it always feels like you‚Äôre standing on a very gentle hill, even if the ground is level. For similar reasons, flat maps are almost totally useless beyond an extremely local scale, because distortions grow so rapidly.
The entire Moon essentially shares a single weather system. When it rains on the Moon, it rains everywhere. The ~280,000 square miles of terrain pegs the Moon at around the size of Texas, or France plus the U.K. combined.
There is no consistent method of keeping time. Without the Sun orbiting in a regular manner & with the omnipresent cloud cover, the entire surface is perpetually dark, with only occasional breaks in the sky on the bright side of the moon. Any reference to ‚Äúdays‚Äù is purely for convenience‚Äôs sake.
And that‚Äôs not even getting into the geography, flora, or fauna.
Lunarian Kingdom
Ludicrously elitist towards anyone on the surface world. Absolutely not better than them in any way, except that they actually are. Lunarians are beautiful, intelligent, highly developed, mostly ignorant to the world beyond themselves, & kept in a developmental stasis by their rulers, who have no desire to ever allow the Moon to change in any meaningful way.
Lunarian Royal Family
The Lunarian Royal Family consists of the Lunarian Princess & her direct family. They are all immortal, terrifyingly deadly, & essentially content to stay in their palace.
They have layers & layers of reactive defenses of every sort that can be imagined & many of those that cannot. They have Continuity of Governance protocols that extend back to the creation of the Moon. They have plans for every possible contingency. They are hell-bent on things staying exactly the way they are within their bubble and will brook no competition nor challenge that will disrupt them in any way.
And yet, they have weaknesses.

Passivity: Most of their defenses are based on passive detection. They make no personal effort to protect themselves.
Aian Ignorance: They cannot and do not pay attention to most of the Grand Disc. They assume that no meaningful force can or would fly from Aia to the Disc without Lunarian magic/wormholes.

They watch wormhole usage closely for intruders, but have no tracking for celestial object entry & exits, in part because birds & some other creatures regularly visit the Moon.


Novel Magic: They usually rely on broad-spectrum anti-magic effects rather than specific anti-magic effects in part because of the difficulty of predicting magical evolution.

Despite this, their ability to deploy deadly troops (albeit somewhat incompetent ones, & in small numbers) to anywhere on Aia instantaneously combined with their enormous magical & technological advantages means that they could freely manipulate the state of Aia on a whim if they chose to do so. (Some say that the Lunarian Princess drove Kain‚Äôs wife away in order to drive him from the throne of Glass.)
Practical role in a game
The Lunarians have a stockpile of enormously powerful & dangerous magical weaponry & utilities that could turn the Grand Disc on its head if even a handful were brought back to the surface. Going to the Moon & coming back is something that Lv10+ PCs would do in order to supercharge their conquests with world-wrecking power at the risk of bringing down the wrath of aeons-old proto-gods onto their heads.
Alternatively, much of the Moon is not actually under Lunarian occupation as it consists of a bizarre & deadly alien landscape with a variety of interesting rewards that by themselves could be extraordinarily valuable even if they didn‚Äôt change the fate of empires (merely kingdoms).

Book-2---Gamemaster's-Guide/{-Appendix-C.filePath: Book 2 - Gamemaster's Guide/{ Appendix C } - Twintail Priestess.md
Book-2---Gamemaster's-Guide/{-Appendix-C.title: { Appendix C } - Twintail Priestess
Book-2---Gamemaster's-Guide/{-Appendix-C.content:
The Solar Priestess may have become an imperial cultist priestess, but it originated as a joke Tradition.
I never was good at killing my darlings.
The following may be considered a ‚Äúpurer‚Äù representation of Solar‚Äôs most fanatical followers.
Twintail Cultist

Races: Mostly human, but theoretically open to any.
Sex: Both.

It is said that long ago, in the annals of the world‚Äôs deepest history ‚Äî before the ice & the horrors of past ages, before even the Aether existed ‚Äî that a past world existed, over which a beautiful goddess reigned.
And that the goddess wore twintails.
Detractors may call you an insane fertility cult. What a bunch of idiots! The efficacy of the magic is the proof itself. From one came two & from there spilled out fractal spiraling eternities. All the world emerged from a single act of division. Heaven‚Äôs breath is harmonics; it is a duality given charge & direction.
@
Tradition Feature

Twintail Cultist: Two is company. Everything comes in a duality. There‚Äôs magic in the harmony of the stars, in the love between two hearts, & in the song of two blades in war. You love symbolism. You love harmonics, rhythm, multiplication, fertility, hair, bonds & numismatics. You hate death, singularities, absolutes, finite reality & boundaries.

Hands used for dual-wielding two one-handed weapons count as empty hands for the sake of casting.
You cannot hurt twintails, or people who are wearing twintails. If you‚Äôre female, you must wear twintails to cast spells.
A single touch is sufficient to tie a willing character‚Äôs hair up in twintails & simultaneously protect the hair & roots from damage (such as loss of pigmentation or stress from wearing hair up too long) for one week.



Spells

The Twintails of Miracles: Double the quantity of up to [Sum] edible objects within 30‚Äô. The copied objects persist forever. Special effects beyond satiation of hunger are not copied ‚Ä¶ including poisons & other harmful effects.
Two Revolution: Touched target character or object is immediately spun a number of degrees according to the caster‚Äôs desire, up to 360¬∞ around an axle that passes through the object‚Äôs touched point. You may choose to make the rotation fast enough to automatically injure struck characters for up to [Dice] + [Sum] Damage of the appropriate Type. The spinning target is immune to Damage while being spun.
Stirring of the Kami: Grow the head hair of a target within 30‚Äô by up to [Sum] feet, permanently. This immediately adds a ‚àí1 penalty to vision & if the length exceeds the target‚Äôs height, their effective movement rate is halved.
Twintails Tied Together: Prevent a bond (of any sort) whose body or ends are within 120‚Äô from being undone, willingly or unwillingly, for [Dice] √ó [Dice] days. Doesn‚Äôt matter if it‚Äôs a rope knot, patch of glue, or relationship.
Twintailed Days: Create a twintail-wearing female clone of a target character within 30‚Äô who loves twintails. The twin does not come with any kind of gear or any other possessions that the original was wearing. In [Sum] hours, the twin abruptly re-merges with their originator, who receives and keeps all of the twin‚Äôs memories.

If a twin is killed, their originator must choose between either permanently losing half their Hit Dice or permanently losing their love for twintails. In the case of those possessing this Tradition, this causes a permanent loss of the school‚Äôs perk, cantrips, & ability to attract any additional spells in this list. In either case, they can never receive another twin again.
If cast with 4+ MD, the twin lasts forever. The consequences of the twin‚Äôs death remains.


The Twintailed Foe: For [Sum] Rounds, up to [Sum] targets within 30‚Äô have their Saves fail automatically if any die involved in the roll produces a result containing the digit 2.
What Twintails Wish For: Touched twintail-wearing female gains a +2 bonus on Persuasion & Negotiation Saves for [Dice] hours. In addition, characters without a good & immediate reason to refuse requests from the target do not do so.
The Day Twintails Disappeared: Up to [Dice] characters turn invisible for [Dice] √ó 10 minutes. Their shadow remains visible. Spellcasting or taking Damage breaks the veil. At 2+ SD, your shadow is concealed; at 3+ SD, sound is muffled; at 4+ SD, affected characters may cast & take Damage while invisible.
The Steel Twintail: Up to [Dice] targets within 30‚Äô divide Damage they receive by [Dice] (& round down) for [Sum] rounds. Twintails are indestructible for the duration of the spell.
Colors of the Soul: Target single character within 15‚Äô has their intentions, motives, & desires laid bare to you in a flash of light & color, who gains a permanent +[Dice] bonus on Persuasion Saves against them in the future; if cast repeatedly, only the highest [Dice] is used. If the target loves twintails, or if 3 or more SD are used, you may in the future attempt a Save to divine the target‚Äôs likely course of action on a given topic (assuming your information about the topic is correct).
Twintails of the Goddess: You briefly take on the form of Solar, the twintailed goddess, for [Sum] rounds. The blinding halo surrounding her fills everything within sight with brilliant white light; all characters who do not love twintails are counted as blinded while within line-of-sight of her. Violence against twintail-wearing characters is impossible in her presence. Solar appears as a naked teenage girl with long red twintails.
The Second Coming of the Gentleman Warriors: Up to [Sum] characters whose spirits are within 120‚Äô of you & who you were personally friends with resurrects. You die. Nobody can recover your spirit. It‚Äôs gone.

index.title: Glass Empires SRD
index.content:
Glass Empires
Glass Empires is a tabletop RPG about pre-industrial power dynamics on an animistic discworld ‚Äî faction-building, debt, rulership, and the nature of the divine. It is OSR-adjacent but not a dungeon-crawl game. Your Level is your Followers. Your Faction is more important than your character.


                  
                  Danger
                  
                

This SRD is a live draft and reference document for an ongoing Braunstein campaign. Sections marked with @ are incomplete! Refer to Saki for clarification on any details.


The navigation in the sidebar lists all chapters.
